6. **Frame update:** New frames are generated at the **frame rate** (typically 15–60 frames per second) to allow **real-time imaging** of moving structures (fetal motion, heart beat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-Time B-Mode** is the standard clinical mode. The frame rate $f_f$ is governed by the depth of imaging and the number of scan lines.

#### 2.2.3 Clinical Applications of B-Mode
- **Obstetric imaging** (fetal development, pregnancy monitoring).
- **Abdominal imaging** (liver, kidneys, spleen, gallbladder).
- **Cardiac imaging (2D Echocardiography)**.
- **Vascular imaging (carotid, peripheral arteries)**.
- **Musculoskeletal imaging**.
- **Emergency/point-of-care imaging**.

---

### 2.3 M-Mode (Motion Mode) — The 1-Dimensional "Time-Motion Display"

#### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
M-Mode is essentially **B-Mode data plotted as a function of time**. The transducer is held stationary over a structure of interest (typically the heart), and the **brightness-modulated echoes from a single line of sight are continuously written to the display**, with the **horizontal axis representing time** and the **vertical axis representing depth**.

The result is a **"strip-chart" type image** showing how the depth positions of tissue interfaces change over time. This makes it exceptionally powerful for visualizing **rapidly moving structures** such as heart valves and ventricular walls.

> [!NOTE]
> M-Mode is also called **TM-Mode (Time-Motion Mode)** in some textbooks, but the KTU syllabus uses the term **M-Mode**.

#### 2.3.2 Logic Steps in M-Mode Operation
1. **Stationary transducer:** The probe is held in a fixed position; only one A-line is fired repeatedly.
2. **PRF timing:** Pulses are emitted at a fixed **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**.
3. **Brightness mapping:** Each returning echo modulates the brightness of the display (similar to B-Mode on a single line).
4. **Slow horizontal sweep:** Instead of sweeping across the body (as in B-Mode), the display sweeps **across the screen at a slow constant rate** (e.g., 1–5 seconds across the screen).
5. **Strip-chart formation:** Successive echo lines are placed next to each other horizontally → produces a pattern where moving structures (e.g., heart valves) trace wavy lines.

#### 2.3.3 Clinical Applications of M-Mode
- **Echocardiography** — measurement of:
  - Ventricular wall thickness and motion.
  - Valve leaflet motion (e.g., mitral valve).
  - Chamber dimensions during systole and diastole.
  - **Ejection fraction** and **fractional shortening**.
- **Fetal heart rate monitoring (M-Mode fetal cardiography)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> M-Mode has **superior temporal resolution** compared to 2D B-Mode (since only one line is acquired), making it ideal for analyzing **fast-moving structures** like cardiac valves.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exam-critical formula bank** for Module 1. Master every equation in this table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Depth of reflector | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$ = depth in metres, $c$ = 1540 m/s, $t$ = round-trip time in s. **Factor 2** because pulse travels down and back. |
| 2 | Round-trip time per cm | $t_{1cm} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (0.01)}{c} \approx 13 \ \mu s$ | Time for echo to return from 1 cm depth. |
| 3 | Velocity of sound in tissue | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | $K$ = bulk modulus, $\rho$ = density. $c \approx 1540$ m/s in soft tissue. |
| 4 | Reflection coefficient (intensity) | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^{2}$ | $Z_i$ = acoustic impedance of medium $i$ = $\rho_i \cdot c_i$. |
| 5 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Units: **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹). |
| 6 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | $f_0$ = centre frequency of transducer. |
| 7 | Axial (range) resolution | $\Delta d = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot n}$ | $n$ = number of cycles in pulse (typically 2–3). |
| 8 | Lateral resolution | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot z}{D}$ | $z$ = depth, $D$ = transducer diameter / aperture. |
| 9 | Maximum unambiguous depth | $D_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | Beyond this depth, range ambiguity occurs. |
| 10 | Frame rate (B-Mode) | $f_f = \dfrac{PRF}{N_{lines}}$ | $N_{lines}$ = number of scan lines per frame. |
| 11 | Attenuation coefficient | $\alpha \approx 0.5 \ \text{dB/cm/MHz (soft tissue, average)}$ | Frequency-dependent; guides TGC slope. |
| 12 | Attenuation loss | $A = \alpha \cdot f_0 \cdot d$ | In dB; must be compensated by receiver gain. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL ESCAPE RULES (KTU exam trap):**
> - Always write the **factor of 2** in depth and PRF formulas — forgetting it is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in KTU valuation.
> - For depth, use **round-trip time**, not one-way time.
> - Reflection coefficient uses **intensity ratio** — amplitude reflection coefficient is $\dfrac{Z_2-Z_1}{Z_2+Z_1}$ (no square).

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Modes

| Mode | Engineering / Medical Utility |
|------|------------------------------|
| A-Mode | Foundation of all pulse-echo ranging systems; basis of SONAR, RADAR, and non-destructive testing. |
| B-Mode | Real-time 2-D anatomical visualization; used in obstetrics, abdominal, vascular, MSK, emergency medicine. |
| M-Mode | High-temporal-resolution motion tracking; standard in cardiology for valve and wall motion analysis. |
| Modern extensions | Doppler (Color/Power/Spectral), 3-D/4-D, Elastography, Contrast-enhanced ultrasound. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth of a Reflector (The Pulse-Echo Range Equation)

This is the **single most important equation** in ultrasound imaging. Every KTU exam will test it.

**Statement to derive:** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted into tissue. It reflects from a tissue interface and returns to the transducer. If the round-trip time is $t$, derive the depth of the reflector.

#### Step 1: Define the physical setup
Let the depth of the reflecting interface be $d$ (in metres), measured from the transducer surface. The ultrasound pulse travels **down to the reflector** and **back up to the transducer**, covering a total path length of $2d$.

#### Step 2: Apply the basic kinematic relationship
Distance equals velocity multiplied by time. For a uniform medium with constant velocity $c$:

$$
\text{One-way distance} = c \cdot t_{one-way}
$$

#### Step 3: Account for round-trip propagation
The transducer measures the **round-trip time** $t$, not the one-way time. Therefore:

$$
2d = c \cdot t
$$

#### Step 4: Solve for depth $d$
Rearranging both sides:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

#### Step 5: Substitute the standard value of $c$ in soft tissue
For medical ultrasound, $c = 1540$ m/s. Substituting:

$$
d = \frac{1540 \cdot t}{2} = 770 \cdot t \quad \text{(with } t \text{ in seconds, } d \text{ in metres)}
$$

Or equivalently, in clinically convenient units (depth in cm, time in microseconds):

$$
d \text{ [in cm]} = \frac{t \text{ [in } \mu\text{s]}}{13}
$$

since $t_{1cm} = 2 \times 0.01 / 1540 \approx 13 \ \mu s$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Unambiguous Depth (PRF Limit)

**Statement:** In pulse-echo ultrasound, the pulse repetition frequency $PRF$ sets a hard limit on the maximum depth that can be imaged without range ambiguity. Derive this limit.

#### Step 1: Recall the constraint
A new pulse can only be transmitted **after the echo from the deepest structure of interest has returned**. Otherwise, returning echoes from the previous pulse will be mis-mapped onto the new pulse's time-base, producing **range ambiguity artefacts**.

#### Step 2: Express the round-trip time for maximum depth
For maximum depth $D_{max}$, the round-trip time is:

$$
t_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot D_{max}}{c}
$$

#### Step 3: Relate $t_{max}$ to PRF
By definition, PRF is the reciprocal of the pulse repetition period $T_R$:

$$
PRF = \frac{1}{T_R}
$$

For unambiguous ranging, $T_R \geq t_{max}$:

$$
T_R \geq \frac{2 \cdot D_{max}}{c}
$$

#### Step 4: Solve for $D_{max}$
Inverting both sides:

$$
D_{max} \leq \frac{c \cdot T_R}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Axial (Range) Resolution

**Statement:** Derive the minimum axial separation of two reflectors that can be resolved as distinct echoes.

#### Step 1: Definition
**Axial resolution** $\Delta d$ is the minimum distance along the beam axis between two reflectors such that their echoes are received as **non-overlapping** pulses at the receiver.

#### Step 2: Pulse length in tissue
A typical ultrasound pulse contains $n$ cycles at centre frequency $f_0$. The spatial length of the pulse in tissue is:

$$
L_{pulse} = n \cdot \lambda = \frac{n \cdot c}{f_0}
$$

#### Step 3: Round-trip consideration
For two reflectors separated by $\Delta d$, the **difference in round-trip distance** is $2 \Delta d$. The two returning echoes are just resolved when:

$$
2 \cdot \Delta d = L_{pulse}
$$

#### Step 4: Solve for $\Delta d$

$$
\Delta d = \frac{L_{pulse}}{2} = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0}
$$

#### Step 5: Special case — short pulse
For an ideal short pulse ($n = 1$ cycle):

$$
\Delta d = \frac{c}{2 \cdot f_0} = \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam shortcut:** For a 5 MHz transducer, $\Delta d \approx 0.154$ mm. For 10 MHz, $\Delta d \approx 0.077$ mm. Higher frequency = finer axial resolution, but **less penetration** due to higher attenuation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of A, B, and M Modes

| Parameter | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|
| Dimensionality of display | 1-D (single line) | 2-D (image) | 1-D (time strip) |
| Y-axis information | Echo **amplitude** (voltage) | Echo **brightness** (grayscale) | Echo **brightness** (grayscale) |
| X-axis information | Time / Depth (one line) | Lateral position (across scan) | **Time** (horizontal sweep) |
| Sweep direction | Horizontal (fast) | Horizontal + Vertical raster | Horizontal (slow, e.g., 1–5 s/trace) |
| Image type | Spike trace (oscilloscope-like) | Anatomical 2-D grayscale image | Wavy time-motion strip |
| Number of lines per frame | 1 | 64–256 | 1 (repeating) |
| Spatial resolution | Excellent **axial** (1-D) | Good **axial + lateral** (2-D) | Excellent **axial + temporal** |
| Temporal resolution | Very high (1 line/μs) | Moderate (limited by frame rate) | Very high (PRF-limited) |
| Best suited for | Precise **distance/biometry** | **Anatomy** / morphology | **Motion** (cardiac valves, walls) |
| Clinical example | Axial eye length | Fetal scan, abdominal scan | Mitral valve motion, fetal heart rate |
| Origin in time | 1940s–1950s | 1950s–present | 1950s–present (cardiology) |
| Common modern use | Embedded as 1-D acquisition | **Workhorse of clinical imaging** | Cardiac and fetal heart analysis |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Question:** An ultrasound scan uses a 3.5 MHz transducer in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s). A pulse-echo returns from a structure at round-trip time $t = 26 \ \mu s$. Calculate: (i) the depth of the structure, (ii) the wavelength, and (iii) the axial resolution for a 2-cycle pulse.

#### Solution

**(i) Depth of the structure**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \cdot 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{1540 \cdot 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{40040 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 20020 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 0.02002 \ \text{m} \approx 2.0 \ \text{cm}}
$$

**(ii) Wavelength in tissue**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{1540}{3.5 \times 10^{6}} = 440 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 0.44 \ \text{mm}}
$$

**(iii) Axial resolution for $n = 2$ cycles**

$$
\Delta d = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0} = \frac{2 \cdot 1540}{2 \cdot 3.5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1540}{3.5 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\Delta d = 440 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m} = 0.44 \ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta d \approx 0.44 \ \text{mm}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generalized Pulse-Echo Ultrasound System (Basis of A, B, and M Modes)

This diagram shows the **signal flow inside a generic ultrasound scanner**; the mode switch simply re-routes the display mapping.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pulser / Transmitter] -- High voltage pulse --> A2[Transducer Probe Piezoelectric Crystal]
    A2 -- Ultrasound pulse into tissue --> A3[Patient Body Acoustic Interfaces]
    A3 -- Reflected echoes --> A2
    A2 -- RF echo voltage --> A4[TGC Amplifier Time Gain Compensation]
    A4 --> A5[Rectifier and Envelope Detector]
    A5 --> A6[Log Compression and Scan Conversion]
    A6 -- Processed video signal --> A7{Mode Selector Switch}
    A7 -- AMode path --> A8[AMode Display 1D Amplitude vs Depth on CRT]
    A7 -- BMode path --> A9[BMode Display 2D Brightness Map on Monitor]
    A7 -- MMode path --> A10[MMode Display 1D Brightness vs Depth vs Time on Strip]
    A11[Time Base Generator Master Clock] -- sync --> A1
    A11 -- sync --> A7
    A11 -- sweep signals --> A8
    A11 -- raster signals --> A9
    A11 -- slow sweep --> A10
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — A-Mode Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Sync Generator issues trigger] --> B2[Pulser fires transducer]
    B2 --> B3[US pulse travels into tissue]
    B3 --> B4[Echoes return to same transducer]
    B4 --> B5[RF amplifier with TGC]
    B5 --> B6[Vertical amplifier of CRT]
    B6 --> B7[CRT electron beam deflected vertically by echo amplitude]
    B1 --> B8[Horizontal ramp generator]
    B8 --> B9[CRT electron beam sweeps horizontally at rate corresponding to depth]
    B7 --> B10[Spike trace on CRT screen]
    B9 --> B10
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — B-Mode Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Sequencer fires multiple lines across scan plane] --> C2[Beamformer steers and focuses beam]
    C2 --> C3[Transducer elements fire one line at a time]
    C3 --> C4[Echoes from each line processed by TGC amplifier]
    C4 --> C5[Envelope detector and log compression]
    C5 --> C6[Digital Scan Converter writes pixel brightness into 2D memory]
    C6 --> C7[Frame buffer read out as video]
    C7 --> C8[Monitor displays 2D grayscale image]
    C1 --> C9[Frame rate generator]
    C9 --> C6
    C9 --> C8
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — M-Mode Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Transducer held stationary over moving structure e.g heart] --> D2[Single A-line fired at fixed PRF]
    D2 --> D3[Echoes from moving interfaces received continuously]
    D3 --> D4[TGC amplifier and envelope detector]
    D4 --> D5[Brightness modulated Z axis of display]
    D5 --> D6[Slow horizontal time base sweeps at e.g 1 to 5 seconds per trace]
    D6 --> D7[Vertical axis position equals depth in tissue]
    D6 --> D8[Horizontal axis position equals elapsed time]
    D7 --> D9[Wavy time motion strip on display]
    D8 --> D9
```

---

### 4.5 Comparison Diagram — Display Mapping of A, B, and M Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Echo from tissue interface] --> E2{Display Mode}
    E2 -- A path --> E3[Plot amplitude vs depth on one line]
    E2 -- B path --> E4[Plot brightness at correct X Y position in 2D image]
    E2 -- M path --> E5[Plot brightness vs depth repeatedly vs time]
    E3 --> E6[Vertical spike height proportional to echo strength]
    E4 --> E7[Pixel gray value proportional to echo strength]
    E5 --> E8[Wavy trace shows motion of structure over time]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The same physical echo $E_1$ is shown as a **spike** in A-Mode, a **bright dot** in B-Mode, and a **wavy line on a time strip** in M-Mode. The physical origin is identical — only the display mapping changes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is A-Mode ultrasound scanning? State any two clinical applications.*

**Model Answer:**

A-Mode (Amplitude Mode) ultrasound scanning is a **one-dimensional pulse-echo display technique** in which the **amplitude of the reflected echo** is plotted as a **vertical deflection (spike)** against the **time-of-flight (or depth)** of the echo on the horizontal axis of a CRT. Stronger echoes produce taller spikes; weaker echoes produce smaller spikes. A-Mode displays echoes from a **single line of sight** through the tissue.

**Clinical Applications:**
1. **Ophthalmology** — measurement of axial length of the eye, lens thickness, and detection of retinal detachment.
2. **Echoencephalography** — detection of midline shift in the brain to identify intracranial lesions or hematomas.

*(Any two valid applications accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between B-Mode and M-Mode ultrasound scanning in terms of display and clinical use.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Display | 2-D grayscale anatomical image | 1-D brightness strip vs time |
| Axes | X-Y = spatial position, brightness = echo strength | Y = depth, X = time |
| Motion visualization | Real-time anatomical motion | Quantitative motion of single line over time |
| Clinical use | Fetal imaging, abdominal imaging, vascular | Cardiac valve motion, ventricular wall motion, fetal heart rate |
| Temporal resolution | Lower (frame rate limited) | Very high (PRF limited) |

B-Mode is used primarily for **anatomical visualization**, while M-Mode is used for **motion analysis** of rapidly moving structures such as heart valves and chamber walls.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, each Part B question carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **operating principle of an A-Mode ultrasound scanner**. Describe the function of the pulser, transducer, time-base generator, and CRT in the system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted into soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s) using a 5 MHz transducer. If the round-trip time for an echo is $39 \ \mu s$, calculate:
(i) the depth of the reflecting interface,
(ii) the wavelength in tissue, and
(iii) the axial resolution for a 2-cycle pulse.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — A-Mode Scanner Operation

The A-Mode scanner is a **1-D pulse-echo ranging system**. Its block diagram consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Synchronizer / Trigger Generator:** Provides timing pulses that synchronize the entire system. It triggers the pulser and the time-base ramp generator simultaneously.

2. **Pulser (Transmitter):** Generates a **short high-voltage spike** (typically 100–400 V) of duration equal to a few cycles of the transducer's centre frequency. This excites the piezoelectric crystal.

3. **Transducer Probe:** Contains a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., PZT, lead zirconate titanate) that converts the electrical pulse into an **ultrasound wave** and vice versa. It is coupled to the patient's skin via an acoustic gel to eliminate air gaps.

4. **Receiver (TGC Amplifier):** Receives the weak returning RF echoes. The receiver applies **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)** to amplify echoes from deeper structures more strongly, compensating for attenuation.

5. **Time-Base Generator (Ramp Generator):** Produces a **linear sawtooth voltage ramp** that deflects the CRT electron beam horizontally at a constant rate. The ramp is calibrated such that the horizontal sweep corresponds directly to **depth in tissue** (1 cm ≈ 13 μs).

6. **CRT Display:** The horizontal time-base represents **time/depth**, while the **vertical deflection** is driven by the receiver output. The result is a **series of vertical spikes** along the horizontal line — each spike representing one tissue interface, with its position indicating depth and its height indicating echo strength.

**Working:** The pulser excites the transducer → ultrasound pulse travels into body → echoes return from tissue interfaces at different depths → receiver amplifies and applies TGC → vertical amplifier drives CRT Y-deflection → horizontal ramp provides the time base → spikes appear on the CRT at positions proportional to depth.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $c = 1540$ m/s, $f_0 = 5$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $t = 39 \ \mu s = 39 \times 10^{-6}$ s, $n = 2$ cycles.

**(i) Depth of the interface**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \cdot 39 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{60060 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 30030 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m} = 0.03003 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 3.0 \ \text{cm}}
$$

**(ii) Wavelength in tissue**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 308 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 0.308 \ \text{mm}}
$$

**(iii) Axial resolution for $n = 2$**

$$
\Delta d = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0} = \frac{2 \cdot 1540}{2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 308 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta d = 0.308 \ \text{mm}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **operating principle of a B-Mode ultrasound scanner**. Discuss how the 2-D grayscale image is constructed from multiple A-Mode lines. State any two clinical applications of B-Mode imaging.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **M-Mode ultrasound scanning** with a suitable block diagram. How is the time-motion display generated? State its clinical applications in cardiology.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — B-Mode Scanner Operation

B-Mode (Brightness Mode) is a **2-D grayscale imaging technique** that produces a real-time cross-sectional image of tissue anatomy. Its block diagram consists of:

1. **Sequencer / Scan Controller:** Coordinates the firing of multiple scan lines (typically 64–256 lines per frame) across the imaging plane, either by mechanical sweeping of the transducer or by electronic steering using a phased-array probe.

2. **Beamformer:** For each line, it properly times the excitation of multiple transducer elements to **steer and focus** the beam at a chosen angle and depth. The receive beamformer also dynamically focuses the returning echoes.

3. **Transducer Array (Linear / Convex / Phased):** Emits and receives ultrasound for each line in sequence.

4. **TGC Amplifier:** Compensates for depth-dependent attenuation so that echoes from deep structures appear with similar brightness to those from shallow structures.

5. **Envelope Detector and Log Compression:** The bipolar RF echo is rectified to obtain its envelope (positive amplitude only), then logarithmically compressed (~40 dB dynamic range) to fit into the limited grayscale of the display (typically 8-bit = 256 gray levels).

6. **Digital Scan Converter (DSC):** A 2-D memory array that stores echo amplitudes at their correct (X, Y) spatial positions as they arrive line-by-line, then reads them out as a standard video raster for the monitor.

7. **Monitor:** Displays the 2-D grayscale image in real time at a frame rate of 15–60 frames/second.

**Image construction:** Each scan line is essentially an A-Mode acquisition. By stacking brightness-modulated A-Mode lines side-by-side in the correct spatial positions, a 2-D image is built up. The **brightness of each pixel** is proportional to the **echo strength** at that point.

**Clinical applications of B-Mode:**
1. **Obstetric imaging** — pregnancy monitoring, fetal biometry, anomaly screening.
2. **Abdominal imaging** — liver, kidneys, gallbladder, spleen assessment.

*(Other valid applications: vascular, MSK, cardiac 2D echo, emergency imaging, etc.)*

#### Part (b) — M-Mode Scanner Operation

M-Mode (Motion Mode) is a **1-D time-motion display** in which a single B-Mode line is repeatedly acquired and swept across the screen over time. Its block diagram is similar to a B-Mode scanner, but with the following key differences:

- The transducer is **held stationary** over the structure of interest.
- A **single A-Mode line** is repeatedly fired at a fixed PRF.
- The echoes are brightness-modulated (as in B-Mode).
- The **horizontal time-base is slow** (e.g., 1–5 seconds per sweep), so that successive echo lines are placed next to each other on the display, with the **vertical axis representing depth** and the **horizontal axis representing elapsed time**.

**Time-motion display generation:** As the scan line is repeated, the position of each echo along the depth axis changes according to the **motion of the reflecting interface**. Over time, a moving structure traces a **wavy line** across the display — the shape of this line directly represents the **displacement of the structure as a function of time**. The **slope of the line at any instant** gives the **velocity** of the structure.

**Clinical applications in cardiology:**
1. **Mitral valve motion analysis** — visualizing the rapid opening and closing of mitral valve leaflets during the cardiac cycle.
2. **Ventricular wall motion** — measurement of left ventricular internal dimensions, wall thickness, and fractional shortening during systole and diastole.
3. **Fetal heart rate measurement** — by observing the motion of fetal heart walls/valves.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in A/B/M-Mode questions — flagged by KTU examiners:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the depth equation.** Writing $d = c \cdot t$ instead of $d = c \cdot t / 2$ is the **#1 mistake** and costs a full 2–3 marks in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing A-Mode with B-Mode.** Many students incorrectly state that A-Mode produces a 2-D image. A-Mode is **strictly 1-D** (single line). B-Mode is the 2-D mode.
> 3. **Confusing M-Mode axes.** Students frequently say M-Mode is "M for Modulation" or assign the wrong axes. The **X-axis is TIME** and the **Y-axis is DEPTH**; the brightness is the echo intensity.
> 4. **Using one-way time instead of round-trip time** in numerical problems. The transducer measures **echo arrival time**, which is round-trip.
> 5. **Mixing up velocity in air (330 m/s) with velocity in soft tissue (1540 m/s).** Always use **1540 m/s** unless specifically told otherwise.
> 6. **Omitting the units** in the final answer. KTU examiners **strictly deduct marks** for missing units in numerical answers.
> 7. **In M-Mode, students often say "B-Mode + time"** — this is loosely correct, but for full marks state that M-Mode uses a **single stationary scan line** swept slowly in time.
> 8. **Stating that B-Mode is for motion analysis** — that is M-Mode's role. B-Mode is for **anatomical visualization**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — A, B, and M Modes of Scanning:**

- [x] **All three modes share the same physical basis** — the **pulse-echo principle** using piezoelectric transducers.
- [x] **A-Mode = Amplitude vs Depth** (1-D spike display, used for biometry and echoencephalography).
- [x] **B-Mode = Brightness modulated 2-D image** (workhorse of modern clinical ultrasound, used in obstetrics, abdominal, cardiac, vascular, MSK).
- [x] **M-Mode = Brightness vs Depth vs Time** (1-D time-motion strip, used for cardiac valve/wall motion, fetal heart rate).
- [x] **Depth equation:** $d = c \cdot t / 2$ with $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- [x] **Time per cm of depth:** $\approx 13 \ \mu s$ (round-trip).
- [x] **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c / f_0$.
- [x] **Axial resolution:** $\Delta d = n \cdot c / (2 \cdot f_0)$; improves with **higher frequency**.
- [x] **Maximum unambiguous depth:** $D_{max} = c / (2 \cdot PRF)$.
- [x] **Reflection coefficient (intensity):** $R = \left( (Z_2 - Z_1) / (Z_2 + Z_1) \right)^2$.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho \cdot c$ (Rayl).
- [x] **Standard medical convention:** velocity in soft tissue $= 1540$ m/s (memorize this constant).
- [x] **B-Mode is built by stacking many A-Mode lines** — the "A-line is the atom of B-Mode".
- [x] **M-Mode uses a single A-line swept over time** — high temporal resolution makes it ideal for fast motion.
- [x] **Always include units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners penalize missing units.
- [x] **Always include the factor of 2** in depth and PRF formulas.
- [x] **Higher frequency = better resolution but lower penetration** (due to frequency-dependent attenuation).
- [x] **Real-time B-Mode frame rate** $f_f = PRF / N_{lines}$; higher frame rate requires fewer lines or shallower imaging.
- [x] **Clinical flagship uses:** B-Mode for **anatomy**; M-Mode for **motion**; A-Mode for **precise distance measurement** (mainly historical and ophthalmologic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation - Axial and Lateral Resolutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT502 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation - Axial and Lateral Resolutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In diagnostic ultrasound imaging, the ability of the system to distinctly display two closely situated reflectors as separate structures is quantified by the term **Resolution**. Resolution determines the diagnostic quality of a sonogram and is the single most important parameter governing clinical image interpretability. For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this concept is classified under Module 1 (Ultrasound Instrumentation) and is most frequently tested as either a Part A (3 marks) conceptual question or as a 14-mark Part B derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Strict Verbatim):**
> *Resolution is the ability of an imaging system to distinguish between two closely spaced reflectors. It is a directional property and is sub-divided into **Axial Resolution** (along the beam axis) and **Lateral Resolution** (perpendicular to the beam axis).*

The two principal types of resolution are:

**1. Axial Resolution (Longitudinal / Radial / Depth Resolution)**
Axial resolution is the minimum separation distance between two reflectors lying **along the propagation axis** of the ultrasound beam (i.e., one behind the other in depth) that the system can display as two distinct echoes.

**2. Lateral Resolution (Azimuthal / Transverse Resolution)**
Lateral resolution is the minimum separation distance between two reflectors lying **perpendicular to the beam axis** (i.e., side-by-side at the same depth) that the system can display as two distinct echoes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool with a flashlight (the ultrasound beam) in a foggy environment (the human body).
- **Axial Resolution** is like asking: *“How close can two submarines be, one directly behind the other along the beam, before their shadows blur into one?”* It depends on how **briefly** you flash the light. A very short, sharp flash (short pulse) will hit the front submarine and return before hitting the rear one, producing two distinct shadows.
- **Lateral Resolution** is like asking: *“How close can two submarines be, side-by-side across the beam, before their shadows merge?”* It depends on how **narrow** the flashlight beam is. A tightly focused, pencil-thin beam will keep the two side-by-side shadows separate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU Viva/Exam):**
> - **Typical clinical frequency range:** **2 MHz – 15 MHz**
> - **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** **c = 1540 m/s** (Standard assumption in all KTU numericals)
> - **Wavelength (λ) in soft tissue at 5 MHz:** **λ = 0.308 mm**
> - **Good clinical axial resolution:** **~ 0.5 mm to 1 mm**
> - **Good clinical lateral resolution:** **~ 1 mm to 2 mm** (highly depth-dependent)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Axial Resolution — The 'Why' and 'How'

**The Physics Behind It:** A clinical ultrasound transducer is not a continuous wave (CW) source; it is excited by a brief, damped electrical pulse (typically 2–3 cycles of the central frequency). This results in a **Spatial Pulse Length (SPL)** — the physical length of the ultrasound wave packet in tissue.

**Why SPL/2?** To resolve two reflectors (A and B) lying along the beam at depths $z_A < z_B$:
1. The **trailing edge** of the echo from reflector A must return to the transducer *before* the **leading edge** of the echo from reflector B arrives back at the transducer.
2. The pulse must therefore make the round trip (A→B→A) within one SPL.
3. Mathematically, this forces the minimum resolvable distance $d = SPL / 2$.

**Factors Improving Axial Resolution:**
- **Higher frequency** → Shorter wavelength (λ) → Shorter SPL → Better axial resolution. *Trade-off: penetration depth reduces.*
- **Greater damping** of the transducer → Fewer cycles per pulse (n ↓) → Shorter SPL → Better axial resolution.

### 2.2 Lateral Resolution — The 'Why' and 'How'

**The Physics Behind It:** An ultrasound beam is not infinitely thin; it diverges due to diffraction. The beam has a finite **width** at every depth. Two reflectors located side-by-side within the beam width will be insonified simultaneously and their echoes will merge.

**Why Beam Width Matters:** If the beam is wide at a particular depth, it "sees" both reflectors at the same instant. The echoes overlap in time and are reconstructed as a single bright blob. To resolve them laterally, the reflectors must be separated by a distance greater than the **beam width** at that depth.

**Factors Improving Lateral Resolution:**
- **Higher frequency** → Shorter wavelength (λ) → Narrower beam in the far field.
- **Larger transducer diameter (D)** → Narrower beam (beam width ∝ λ/D).
- **Acoustic lens / Electronic focusing** → Concentrates the beam at the focal point.
- **Operating at the focal zone** → Beam is at its narrowest here.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical KTU Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | mm | c = 1540 m/s in soft tissue |
| 2 | Spatial Pulse Length | $SPL = n \cdot \lambda$ | mm | n = number of cycles in pulse (2–3) |
| 3 | **Axial Resolution** | $R_{axial} = SPL / 2 = (n \cdot \lambda) / 2$ | mm | **Independent of depth** (huge KTU favorite) |
| 4 | Beam Width (Far Field) | $BW = 1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot z / D$ | mm | z = distance from transducer, D = diameter |
| 5 | Beam Width at Focus | $BW_{focal} = 1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot f / D$ | mm | f = focal length |
| 6 | **Lateral Resolution** | $R_{lateral} \approx BW$ | mm | **Strongly depth-dependent** |
| 7 | Depth of Field (Focal Zone) | $DOF = 7.1 \cdot \lambda \cdot f^2 / D^2$ | mm | Range over which beam is narrow |
| 8 | Near Field Length (Fresnel) | $N = D^2 / (4 \cdot \lambda)$ | mm | Where beam is roughly collimated |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Many students incorrectly write $R_{axial} = SPL$. The correct formula is **SPL/2** because the pulse makes a round trip. This single slash difference costs 1 mark in Part A.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern clinical practice, these two resolutions are the deciding factors for **probe selection**. A cardiologist performing transthoracic echo uses a **5 MHz phased array** because it offers a good balance of resolution and penetration (~15 cm). An ophthalmologist uses a **10–15 MHz linear probe** because the eye is shallow (~3 cm) and demands ultra-high axial resolution to visualize the retina. The radiologist performing an abdominal scan chooses a **3.5 MHz curvilinear probe** to penetrate deep tissue at the cost of coarser resolution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Axial Resolution

**Statement to Prove:** $R_{axial} = \dfrac{SPL}{2} = \dfrac{n \cdot \lambda}{2}$

**Step 1 — Setup the geometry.**
Consider two point reflectors $P_1$ and $P_2$ lying along the central axis of the ultrasound beam at depths $z_1$ and $z_2$ respectively, with $z_2 > z_1$. The separation distance is $d = z_2 - z_1$. We are required to find the minimum $d$ for which the echoes appear as two distinct lines on the display.

**Step 2 — Define the emitted pulse.**
The transducer emits a wave packet of length $SPL = n \cdot \lambda$ at time $t = 0$. The leading edge travels at velocity $c$, and the trailing edge lags behind by exactly one spatial pulse length.

**Step 3 — Time for leading edge to reach $P_1$ and $P_2$.**
The leading edge of the pulse reaches $P_1$ at time $t_{1,lead} = z_1 / c$ and reaches $P_2$ at time $t_{2,lead} = z_2 / c$.

**Step 4 — Time for trailing edge to reach $P_1$.**
The trailing edge of the pulse reaches $P_1$ at time:
$$t_{1,trail} = t_{1,lead} + \frac{SPL}{c} = \frac{z_1}{c} + \frac{SPL}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Time for the leading edge of the echo from $P_1$ to return to the transducer.**
The echo from $P_1$ starts at $t_{1,lead}$ and travels back. The leading edge of this echo reaches the transducer at:
$$t_{echo1,lead} = 2 \cdot \frac{z_1}{c}$$

**Step 6 — Time for the leading edge of the echo from $P_2$ to return to the transducer.**
Similarly, the leading edge of the echo from $P_2$ arrives at:
$$t_{echo2,lead} = 2 \cdot \frac{z_2}{c} = 2 \cdot \frac{z_1 + d}{c}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the resolvability condition.**
For the two echoes to be **distinct**, the leading edge of the echo from $P_2$ must arrive *after* the trailing edge of the echo from $P_1$ has already returned. Mathematically:

$$t_{echo1,trail} \leq t_{echo2,lead}$$

The trailing edge of the echo from $P_1$ returns at $t_{echo1,trail} = t_{echo1,lead} + SPL/c = 2z_1/c + SPL/c$.

Substituting:

$$\frac{2 \cdot z_1}{c} + \frac{SPL}{c} \leq 2 \cdot \frac{(z_1 + d)}{c}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify the inequality.**
Multiplying through by $c$:
$$2 \cdot z_1 + SPL \leq 2 \cdot z_1 + 2 \cdot d$$

The $2 \cdot z_1$ terms cancel on both sides, leaving:
$$SPL \leq 2 \cdot d$$

**Step 9 — Solve for the minimum resolvable distance.**
The minimum separation for the two echoes to be just distinguishable is therefore:
$$d_{min} = \frac{SPL}{2}$$

Since $SPL = n \cdot \lambda$, the final boxed result is:
$$\boxed{R_{axial} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda}{2} = \frac{SPL}{2}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A diagnostic ultrasound probe operates at 6 MHz with 2 cycles per pulse. Calculate the axial resolution in soft tissue. Assume $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Calculate wavelength.**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{6 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 2.5667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.2567 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Spatial Pulse Length.**
$$SPL = n \cdot \lambda = 2 \times 0.2567 \text{ mm} = 0.5133 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply axial resolution formula.**
$$R_{axial} = \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{0.5133}{2} = 0.2567 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** $R_{axial} \approx 0.257$ mm (i.e., structures deeper than 0.257 mm apart along the beam axis can be resolved).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Property | Axial Resolution | Lateral Resolution |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Direction of measurement | Along beam axis (depth) | Perpendicular to beam (sideways) |
| Depends on | Spatial Pulse Length (SPL) | Beam Width (BW) |
| Governing parameter | $\lambda$ and number of cycles $n$ | $\lambda$, transducer diameter $D$, depth $z$ |
| Frequency dependence | $R_{axial} \propto 1/f$ | $R_{lateral} \propto 1/f$ in far field |
| Depth dependence | **Independent of depth** (uniform) | **Strongly depth-dependent** (worst in far field) |
| Best region | Anywhere in the image | At the focal point of the transducer |
| Typical clinical value | 0.5 – 1 mm | 1 – 2 mm |
| Engineering lever to improve | Increase damping, raise frequency | Focus beam, increase aperture, raise frequency |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Resolution Determination

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how the transducer's physical parameters and the beam geometry interactively determine the two resolutions. This is the recommended KTU Module 1 instrumentation architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pulser Receiver Circuit] --> B[Transducer Element Piezoelectric Crystal]
    B --> C[Excitation Pulse 2 to 3 cycles]
    C --> D[Spatial Pulse Length SPL = n times lambda]
    D --> E{Axial Resolution Test SPL by 2}
    C --> F[Acoustic Beam Propagation Tissue Medium]
    F --> G[Near Field Fresnel Zone D squared by 4 lambda]
    F --> H[Focal Point Narrowest Beam Width]
    F --> I[Far Field Fraunhofer Zone Beam Diverges]
    H --> J{Beam Width BW = 1.22 times lambda times z by D}
    J --> K{Lateral Resolution Test approx Beam Width}
    G --> K
    I --> K
    L[Depth z and Frequency f] --> J
    L --> E
    M[Display Unit B Mode Scan Converter] --> N[Final 2D Ultrasound Image]
    E --> N
    K --> N
```

### 4.2 Geometric Comparison — Axial vs. Lateral Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXIAL_DIR [ALONG BEAM AXIS DEPTH DIRECTION]
        A1[Reflector 1] -->|distance d ax| A2[Reflector 2]
    end
    subgraph LATERAL_DIR [PERPENDICULAR TO BEAM SIDEWAYS DIRECTION]
        L1[Reflector A] -->|distance d lat| L2[Reflector B]
    end
    B((Ultrasound Beam)) -.insoniates.-> A1
    B -.insoniates.-> A2
    B -.insoniates.-> L1
    B -.insoniates.-> L2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams during revision:**
> - The **first flowchart** shows the entire signal chain: Pulser → Transducer → Pulse → SPL → **Axial Resolution** and Beam → Near/Focal/Far field → **Beam Width → Lateral Resolution** → Final Image.
> - The **second diagram** shows that Axial resolution deals with reflectors stacked along the beam (one after the other), whereas Lateral resolution deals with reflectors lying side-by-side across the beam.

### 4.3 Schematic of the Transducer Pulse and SPL

```
   Transducer Face (z = 0)
   |
   |  |---| |---| |---|       (3 cycles, n = 3)
   |  |   | |   | |   |
   |  |---| |---| |---|  ---->  Direction of Propagation
   |  |---|---|---|---|--- SPL
   |
   v
   Reflector P1 (depth z1)
   v
   Reflector P2 (depth z2 = z1 + SPL/2)
```

The two reflectors $P_1$ and $P_2$ are separated by exactly $SPL/2$, which is the theoretical minimum distance for the echoes to be resolved as two separate A-line spikes on the oscilloscope.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define axial resolution in ultrasound imaging. State the formula for axial resolution and explain the role of spatial pulse length.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Axial resolution is defined as the minimum distance between two reflectors lying along the direction of ultrasound beam propagation that can be displayed as separate echoes.
$$R_{axial} = \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda}{2}$$
where $SPL = n \cdot \lambda$ is the spatial pulse length, $n$ is the number of cycles per pulse, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. A shorter SPL produces a shorter pulse, which improves (reduces) the axial resolution, allowing finer detail to be resolved along the depth direction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between axial resolution and lateral resolution with respect to ultrasound beam geometry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Axial Resolution | Lateral Resolution |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Direction | Along beam axis (depth) | Perpendicular to beam (sideways) |
| Depends on | Spatial pulse length | Beam width |
| Depth variation | Independent of depth | Varies with depth (best at focus) |
| Formula | $n \cdot \lambda / 2$ | $\approx 1.22 \lambda z / D$ |
Axial resolution measures separation along the beam, governed by pulse length. Lateral resolution measures side-by-side separation, governed by beam width.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for axial resolution in ultrasound imaging in terms of spatial pulse length. Briefly discuss the factors that influence axial resolution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define lateral resolution. Derive the expression for far-field beam width of a circular piston transducer and explain how it governs lateral resolution. Discuss the role of transducer focusing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Axial Resolution (7 marks):**

1. The transducer emits a wave packet of spatial pulse length $SPL = n \cdot \lambda$ at $t = 0$. *(1 mark)*
2. Two point reflectors $P_1$ and $P_2$ lie along the beam axis at depths $z_1$ and $z_2$ with separation $d = z_2 - z_1$. *(1 mark)*
3. The leading edge of the pulse reaches $P_1$ at $t = z_1/c$ and $P_2$ at $t = z_2/c$. The leading edge of the echo from $P_1$ returns to the transducer at $t = 2z_1/c$. The leading edge of the echo from $P_2$ returns at $t = 2z_2/c$. *(2 marks)*
4. For the two echoes to be distinguishable, the trailing edge of the echo from $P_1$ must return before the leading edge of the echo from $P_2$ arrives. This gives $SPL/c \leq 2d/c$, hence $d \geq SPL/2$. *(2 marks)*
5. Therefore, $R_{axial} = SPL/2 = (n \cdot \lambda)/2$. *(1 mark)*

**Factors influencing axial resolution (additional explanation):**
- Higher frequency reduces $\lambda$, improving resolution but reducing penetration.
- Damping the transducer reduces $n$, shortening SPL and improving resolution.

**(b) Lateral Resolution Derivation (7 marks):**

1. The lateral resolution is governed by the beam width, which is determined by diffraction at the transducer aperture. *(1 mark)*
2. For a circular piston transducer of diameter $D$ operating in a medium of wavelength $\lambda$, the angular spread in the far field (Fraunhofer region) is $\sin\theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$. *(2 marks)*
3. At a depth $z$ along the axis, the half-amplitude beam width is given by $BW = 1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot z / D$. *(2 marks)*
4. The lateral resolution is approximately equal to the beam width: $R_{lateral} \approx 1.22 \lambda z / D$. *(1 mark)*
5. **Role of focusing:** An acoustic lens or electronic phasing focuses the beam at a chosen depth $z = f$. At the focal point, the beam attains its minimum width $BW_{focal} = 1.22 \lambda f / D$, giving the best lateral resolution. Outside the focal zone (depth of field $DOF = 7.1 \lambda f^2 / D^2$), the beam diverges and lateral resolution degrades. *(1 mark)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** A 5 MHz ultrasound transducer with 3 cycles per pulse is used to image the abdomen. Calculate the axial resolution. Given that the diameter of the transducer is 20 mm, also calculate the lateral resolution at a depth of 80 mm. Assume the speed of sound in soft tissue is 1540 m/s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radiologist is comparing a 3.5 MHz curvilinear probe and a 7.5 MHz linear probe for an application. Discuss the trade-offs in axial and lateral resolution, penetration depth, and clinical suitability. Justify your selection for (i) abdominal imaging and (ii) vascular/imaging of superficial structures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 5$ MHz = $5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $n = 3$, $c = 1540$ m/s, $D = 20$ mm, $z = 80$ mm.

**Step 1 — Calculate wavelength:** *(1 mark)*
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.308 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate axial resolution:** *(2 marks)*
$$R_{axial} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda}{2} = \frac{3 \times 0.308}{2} = \frac{0.924}{2} = 0.462 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate beam width (lateral resolution) at 80 mm depth:** *(2 marks)*
$$R_{lateral} = \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot z}{D} = \frac{1.22 \times 0.308 \times 80}{20}$$
$$R_{lateral} = \frac{30.06}{20} = 1.503 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** *(2 marks)*
- Axial resolution = **0.462 mm** (excellent depth discrimination)
- Lateral resolution at 80 mm = **1.503 mm** (roughly 3× coarser than axial — typical clinical finding)

**(b) Probe Comparison Discussion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Frequency and wavelength relationship:** *(2 marks)*
For 3.5 MHz: $\lambda_{low} = 1540 / 3.5 \times 10^6 = 0.44$ mm.
For 7.5 MHz: $\lambda_{high} = 1540 / 7.5 \times 10^6 = 0.205$ mm.
Higher frequency halves the wavelength, which improves both axial and lateral resolution proportionally.

**Step 2 — Penetration trade-off:** *(2 marks)*
Attenuation in soft tissue is ~0.5 dB/cm/MHz. The 7.5 MHz probe suffers ~7.5× higher attenuation per unit depth, so penetration drops to ~5–6 cm. The 3.5 MHz probe penetrates 15–20 cm. Penetration ∝ 1/f.

**Step 3 — Clinical Selection:** *(3 marks)*
- **(i) Abdominal imaging (e.g., liver, kidney):** Select the **3.5 MHz curvilinear probe** because deep penetration (15–20 cm) is essential; the coarser resolution is clinically acceptable for gross organ assessment. *(1.5 marks)*
- **(ii) Vascular / superficial structures (e.g., carotid artery, thyroid):** Select the **7.5 MHz linear probe** because shallow depth (1–4 cm) demands fine lateral and axial resolution to assess vessel wall, plaque morphology, and intima-media thickness. *(1.5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Axial Formula:** Writing $R_{axial} = SPL$ instead of $SPL/2$ → **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.22 factor:** The Fraunhofer diffraction constant is 1.22, not 1.0. Missing this → **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in numericals:** Mixing cm and mm in the beam width formula. Standardize all lengths in **mm** to avoid 10× errors.
> 4. **Confusing axial with lateral:** A common mistake is writing "$R_{lateral} = n\lambda/2$" — this is axial. Lateral resolution uses **beam width**, not pulse length.
> 5. **Forgetting $c = 1540$ m/s in numericals:** If not given in the question, you MUST state it as a standard assumption to get full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⏵ **Axial resolution** = ability to distinguish reflectors **along the beam** (depth direction). Formula: $R_{axial} = n\lambda/2$. **Independent of depth.**
- ⏵ **Lateral resolution** = ability to distinguish reflectors **perpendicular to the beam** (sideways). Formula: $R_{lateral} \approx 1.22 \lambda z / D$. **Strongly depth-dependent.**
- ⏵ **Spatial Pulse Length (SPL)** is the physical length of the emitted ultrasound wave packet: $SPL = n \times \lambda$. Shorter SPL → better axial resolution.
- ⏵ **Beam width** governs lateral resolution. At the **focal point**, beam is narrowest → best lateral resolution.
- ⏵ **Frequency trade-off:** Higher $f$ → shorter $\lambda$ → better resolution BUT less penetration. Standard assumption: $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- ⏵ **Engineering levers** to improve resolution: (1) increase frequency, (2) increase transducer damping (reduce $n$), (3) increase transducer diameter $D$, (4) use acoustic lens or electronic focusing, (5) operate at the focal zone.
- ⏵ **Always include the factor 1.22** in far-field beam width expressions (Fraunhofer diffraction for a circular aperture).
- ⏵ **Memorize:** Axial uses pulse length; Lateral uses beam width. This is the single most important conceptual distinction for KTU Module 1.
- ⏵ **Clinical application rule of thumb:** Deep structures → low frequency; Superficial structures → high frequency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duplex Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT502 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Duplex Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Duplex Scanning** (also termed **Duplex Ultrasonography** or **Duplex Ultrasound Imaging**) is a non-invasive, real-time, dual-modality diagnostic ultrasound technique that simultaneously combines two fundamental ultrasound modalities into a single, integrated examination:

1. **B-Mode (Brightness-Mode) Ultrasonography** – Provides a two-dimensional, grayscale, anatomical cross-sectional image of tissues and vessel walls.
2. **Doppler Ultrasound (Spectral and/or Color Flow)** – Provides hemodynamic information regarding blood flow velocity, direction, and turbulence within the imaged vascular segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Duplex scanning is the synchronous combination of a real-time B-mode gray-scale image with a Doppler flow detector (pulsed-wave spectral Doppler and/or color flow Doppler) within a single transducer assembly, enabling the operator to interrogate blood flow at any user-selected location (sample volume) within the visualized anatomical field.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Camera + Speedometer" Model

Imagine you are standing beside a busy highway. You have **two devices**:
- A **CCTV camera** that shows you *where* the cars are and *what the road looks like* (this is your **B-mode** image).
- A **radar speed gun** pointed at a specific car that tells you *how fast* that car is moving and in *which direction* (this is your **Doppler** component).

A duplex scanner is exactly this combination — but miniaturized into a single ultrasound probe. The **camera (B-mode)** lets the sonographer visually identify a blood vessel, and the **radar (Doppler)** measures the velocity of blood flowing inside it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The word *duplex* literally means *two-fold* or *double*. In ultrasound, it refers to the dual functionality — anatomical imaging **plus** flow measurement — performed simultaneously by the same transducer.

### 1.3 Historical Context & Evolution

| Generation | Modality | Capability |
|:-----------|:---------|:-----------|
| 1st | Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler only | No image; blind Doppler |
| 2nd | Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler + B-mode | First true duplex |
| 3rd | Color Flow Doppler + B-mode | Real-time color map of flow |
| 4th | Power Doppler + Color + B-mode | Sensitive to slow flow |
| 5th | Spectral + Color + B-mode + 3D/4D | Modern clinical standard |

### 1.4 Standard Operational Metrics in Duplex Scanning

The clinical utility of duplex scanning relies on several standard hemodynamic metrics that must be **highlighted in bold** for KTU reference:

- **Peak Systolic Velocity (PSV)** — measured in **cm/s** or **m/s**
- **End Diastolic Velocity (EDV)** — measured in **cm/s** or **m/s**
- **Resistive Index (RI)** — dimensionless ratio (typically 0.6 – 0.9 normal)
- **Pulsatility Index (PI)** — dimensionless ratio (Gosling/King formula)
- **Velocity Ratio (ICA/CCA ratio)** — dimensionless, used in carotid studies

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Duplex Scanning

#### Pillar 1 — B-Mode Imaging (Anatomical Foundation)
- Operates on **pulse-echo principle**: a short burst (pulse) of high-frequency ultrasound (typically **2 – 15 MHz**) is transmitted into tissue.
- Echoes returning from acoustic impedance mismatches are converted into gray-scale pixel intensities.
- Provides spatial information: **axial**, **lateral**, and **elevational** resolution.
- Establishes the **anatomical roadmap** — the sonographer visually locates the vessel of interest.

#### Pillar 2 — Doppler Ultrasound (Hemodynamic Foundation)
- Based on the **Doppler Effect**: the frequency shift between transmitted and reflected ultrasound from moving red blood cells (RBCs).
- Provides **temporal** (velocity over time) information — usually displayed as a **spectral waveform** (velocity vs. time) or a **color map** overlaid on the B-mode image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Operational Rule:** Doppler interrogation is only meaningful *after* the B-mode image has localized the vessel. The "blind" placement of a Doppler sample volume without anatomical guidance is **not duplex scanning** — it is simple Doppler ultrasonography.

### 2.2 Modes of Doppler Used in Duplex Scanners

| Mode | Full Form | Function | Limitation |
|:-----|:----------|:---------|:-----------|
| **PW** | Pulsed-Wave Doppler | Range-gated; site-specific velocity measurement | Limited by **Nyquist limit** (aliasing at high velocities) |
| **CW** | Continuous-Wave Doppler | Measures very high velocities without aliasing | **No range resolution** — samples entire beam path |
| **CFD** | Color Flow Doppler | Real-time color overlay of flow direction & velocity | Subject to aliasing; angle-dependent |
| **PD** | Power Doppler | Displays amplitude (not velocity) of Doppler signal | No directional or velocity information; very sensitive to slow flow |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mandate:** All formulas below are derivable from first principles and are **must-know** for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions. Memorize the symbols, units, and clinical limits.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Standard Unit | Typical Clinical Range / Note |
|:------:|:---------|:---------------------|:-------------:|:------------------------------|
| $f_d$ | Doppler frequency shift | $f_d = f_r - f_0$ | Hz | Audible range 20 Hz – 20 kHz when demodulated |
| $f_0$ | Transmitted (incident) frequency | Given by transducer | MHz | Vascular: 2 – 10 MHz |
| $f_r$ | Received (reflected) frequency | $f_r = f_0 + f_d$ | MHz | — |
| $v$ | Blood flow velocity (along beam axis) | $v = \dfrac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Normal arterial: 0.2 – 1.5 m/s |
| $c$ | Speed of ultrasound in soft tissue | — | m/s | **1540 m/s** (standard assumed value) |
| $\theta$ | Doppler angle (beam-to-flow) | — | degrees | **≤ 60°** clinically; optimal **30° – 60°** |
| PSV | Peak Systolic Velocity | Read from spectral peak | cm/s | Carotid: < 125 cm/s (normal) |
| EDV | End Diastolic Velocity | Read from spectral trough | cm/s | Carotid: < 40 cm/s (normal) |
| RI | Resistive Index | $RI = \dfrac{PSV - EDV}{PSV}$ | dimensionless | **0.6 – 0.9** normal; > 0.8 abnormal in renal |
| PI | Pulsatility Index | $PI = \dfrac{PSV - EDV}{V_{mean}}$ | dimensionless | Higher in distal, high-resistance beds |
| ICA/CCA | Carotid velocity ratio | $\dfrac{PSV_{ICA}}{PSV_{CCA}}$ | dimensionless | < 2.0 normal; > 4.0 indicates ≥ 70% stenosis |
| $f_{Nq}$ | Nyquist frequency (PW Doppler) | $f_{Nq} = \dfrac{PRF}{2}$ | Hz | $PRF$ = Pulse Repetition Frequency |
| $v_{max,\,unaliased}$ | Maximum unaliased velocity | $v_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | m/s | Determines aliasing threshold |
| $d_{max}$ | Maximum sample volume depth | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | m | Range-gating depth limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** The pipe (absolute value) is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX, so for example $\vert f_d \vert$ is the unsigned magnitude of the Doppler shift.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Duplex scanning is the **clinical workhorse** in:
- **Vascular surgery** — Carotid stenosis grading, peripheral arterial disease (PAD) assessment, deep vein thrombosis (DVT) diagnosis.
- **Nephrology** — Renal artery stenosis evaluation via RI measurements.
- **Obstetrics** — Umbilical artery and middle cerebral artery Doppler for fetal well-being.
- **Cardiology** — Echocardiography (cardiac duplex) for valve and shunt assessment.
- **Transplant surgery** — Monitoring vascular patency of transplanted organs.

### 2.5 Physical & System Constraints

- **Angle of insonation ($\theta$)**: Errors in $\theta$ are magnified in velocity because $v \propto 1/\cos\theta$. At $\theta = 90°$, $\cos\theta = 0$ and $v$ becomes **infinite** (mathematically undefined) — meaning **no Doppler shift is detected when the beam is perpendicular to flow**. This is why the beam is always steered to $\theta \leq 60°$.
- **Aliasing**: Occurs in PW Doppler when $f_d > f_{Nq}$. Solved by increasing PRF, lowering $f_0$, or using CW Doppler.
- **Sample volume (SV)**: User-defined region (1 – 15 mm axial length) at a chosen depth where Doppler is sampled.
- **Wall filter (high-pass filter)**: Removes low-frequency signals from vessel wall motion (typically 50 – 200 Hz cutoff).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Doppler Velocity Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU examinations on duplex scanning. It connects the Doppler shift to actual blood flow velocity.

---

#### **Derivation: Doppler Velocity Equation**

**Step 1 — Define the system geometry.**

Let:
- An ultrasound transducer be stationary, emitting waves at frequency $f_0$.
- A red blood cell (RBC) act as a moving target (the "reflector") moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ to the ultrasound beam axis.

**Step 2 — First Doppler shift (transmitter → moving RBC).**

Because the RBC is moving *away from* (or *toward*) the transducer, the wavefronts perceived by the RBC are compressed or stretched. The frequency of ultrasound *as seen by the moving RBC* is:

$$
f' = \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \cdot f_0
$$

> **Logic:** If the RBC moves **toward** the transducer, the relative speed of approach is $c + v\cos\theta$, so the apparent frequency $f'$ is *higher* than $f_0$. If the RBC moves **away**, $f'$ is *lower*.

**Step 3 — Second Doppler shift (moving RBC → stationary transducer).**

The RBC *re-radiates* (scatters) ultrasound at frequency $f'$ in its own reference frame. From the stationary transducer's point of view, this re-radiated wave is also Doppler-shifted because the RBC is moving. The received frequency $f_r$ is:

$$
f_r = \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \cdot f'
$$

> **Logic:** The denominator $(c - v\cos\theta)$ represents the rate at which successive wave crests reach the stationary receiver.

**Step 4 — Combine the two shifts.**

Substitute the expression for $f'$ from Step 2 into Step 3:

$$
f_r = \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \cdot \left[ \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \cdot f_0 \right]
$$

$$
f_r = \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} \cdot f_0
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Doppler frequency shift $f_d$.**

By definition:

$$
f_d = f_r - f_0
$$

Subtracting and simplifying:

$$
f_d = f_0 \left[ \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} - 1 \right] = f_0 \left[ \frac{c + v\cos\theta - (c - v\cos\theta)}{c - v\cos\theta} \right]
$$

$$
f_d = f_0 \cdot \frac{2 v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the clinical approximation.**

Since $v \ll c$ (blood velocity is on the order of 0.1 – 1.5 m/s, while $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue), the term $v\cos\theta$ in the denominator is **negligible** compared to $c$. Thus:

$$
c - v\cos\theta \approx c
$$

This yields the **classical Doppler equation**:

$$
f_d \approx \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for velocity $v$ (the form used in duplex scanning).**

Rearranging the classical Doppler equation to express velocity in terms of the measurable shift $f_d$:

$$
\boxed{\, v = \frac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta} \,}
$$

> **This is the master equation of duplex scanning.** Every clinical velocity reading on a duplex scanner is computed from this expression in real time.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Problem-Style)

**Problem:** A 5 MHz transducer ($\,f_0 = 5 \times 10^6\,$ Hz) is used in a duplex scanner. The Doppler angle is set to $\theta = 60°$. The measured Doppler shift is $f_d = 2000\,$ Hz. Assume $c = 1540\,$ m/s. Calculate the blood flow velocity $v$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — List the knowns.**
- $c = 1540\,$ m/s
- $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6\,$ Hz
- $f_d = 2000\,$ Hz
- $\theta = 60° \Rightarrow \cos 60° = 0.5$

**Step 2 — Apply the velocity equation.**

$$
v = \frac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
v = \frac{1540 \times 2000}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 0.5}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately.**

$$
\text{Numerator: } 1540 \times 2000 = 3{,}080{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Denominator: } 2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5 = 5 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$
v = \frac{3{,}080{,}000}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.616\, \text{m/s} = 61.6\, \text{cm/s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is a typical **Peak Systolic Velocity (PSV)** in a moderately diseased carotid artery (normal is < 125 cm/s but with spectral broadening suggestive of early plaque).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Doppler Modes

| Parameter | Pulsed-Wave (PW) Doppler | Continuous-Wave (CW) Doppler |
|:----------|:-------------------------|:------------------------------|
| Transmission | Pulses (one crystal alternates Tx/Rx) | Continuous (separate Tx and Rx crystals) |
| Range resolution | **Yes** (range-gated sample volume) | **No** (samples entire beam path) |
| Aliasing | **Yes** (limited by Nyquist/PRF) | **No** (no PRF) |
| Maximum measurable velocity | Limited | **Very high** (e.g., aortic stenosis > 5 m/s) |
| Typical clinical use in duplex | Routine vessel interrogation | High-velocity jets, valvular stenosis |
| Limitation in duplex | Aliasing at stenoses | No localization — combines with PW for site-specific data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Modern duplex scanners often use **triplex mode** — simultaneous B-mode + Color Flow + Spectral Doppler — which is technically a "triplex" not a "duplex," though clinically the term duplex is loosely retained.

### 3.4 Resistive Index (RI) — Derivation from Spectral Waveform

The Resistive Index (Pourcelot, 1974) is empirically derived from the spectral envelope:

**Step 1 — Identify the peak systolic velocity (PSV)** from the spectral waveform — this is the highest point of the cardiac-cycle envelope.

**Step 2 — Identify the end-diastolic velocity (EDV)** — the velocity at the end of the cardiac cycle, just before the next systole.

**Step 3 — Apply the definition:**

$$
RI = \frac{PSV - EDV}{PSV}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
- $RI \to 1$ implies EDV $\to 0$ — characteristic of **high-resistance vascular beds** (e.g., external carotid artery, resting limb arteries, renal interlobar arteries with pathology).
- $RI \to 0$ implies EDV $\to$ PSV — characteristic of **low-resistance beds** (e.g., internal carotid artery, hepatic artery, renal artery in normal kidney).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Duplex Scanner

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TxChain["Transmit Chain"]
        PULSER["Pulser / Beamformer"]
        TXCRYSTAL["Piezoelectric Crystal — Transmit"]
    end
    subgraph Patient["Patient Tissue / Blood Vessel"]
        TISSUE["Soft Tissue (Anatomical Reflectors)"]
        RBC["Red Blood Cells (Moving Reflectors)"]
    end
    subgraph RxChain["Receive Chain"]
        RXCRYSTAL["Piezoelectric Crystal — Receive"]
        TGC["Time Gain Compensation (TGC) Amplifier"]
        SPLIT{"Signal Splitter"}
        BMODECHAIN["B-Mode Processor — Amplitude → Grayscale Pixel"]
        DOPPLERCHAIN["Doppler Processor — Frequency Shift Extraction"]
    end
    subgraph Output["Display"]
        BMON["Grayscale B-Mode Image — Anatomy"]
        DOPON["Spectral / Color Flow Display — Hemodynamics"]
        COMBINED["Combined Duplex Display (Anatomy + Spectrum)"]
    end
    PULSER --> TXCRYSTAL
    TXCRYSTAL --> TISSUE
    TXCRYSTAL --> RBC
    TISSUE --> RXCRYSTAL
    RBC --> RXCRYSTAL
    RXCRYSTAL --> TGC
    TGC --> SPLIT
    SPLIT --> BMODECHAIN
    SPLIT --> DOPPLERCHAIN
    BMODECHAIN --> BMON
    DOPPLERCHAIN --> DOPON
    BMON --> COMBINED
    DOPON --> COMBINED
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The same transducer crystal (in duplex, the same array) handles both transmission and reception. The received echo is split into two parallel processing paths — one for B-mode anatomy, one for Doppler flow. The final display **superimposes** both modalities.

### 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Doppler Angle Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TRANSDUCER["Ultrasound Transducer"] -->|"Beam axis"| VESSEL["Blood Vessel Wall"]
    VESSEL -->|"Flow direction vector v"| VELOCITY["RBC Flow Direction"]
    TRANSDUCER -.->|"Angle θ (between beam and flow)"| VELOCITY
    note1["θ = 0° : Beam parallel to flow → maximum shift"]
    note2["θ = 90° : Beam perpendicular to flow → zero shift (cos 90° = 0)"]
    note3["Clinical optimum: 30° ≤ θ ≤ 60°"]
    TRANSDUCER -.- note1
    TRANSDUCER -.- note2
    TRANSDUCER -.- note3
```

### 4.3 Duplex Display Layout (Standard Clinical Format)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DisplayScreen["Duplex Scanner Display Screen"]
        A["Top-Left Quadrant: B-Mode Grayscale Image\n(Anatomical cross-section with color flow overlay)"]
        B["Top-Right Quadrant: Color Flow Map\n(Red = flow toward transducer, Blue = away)"]
        C["Bottom Quadrant: Spectral Doppler Waveform\n(Vertical: velocity in cm/s; Horizontal: time in seconds)"]
    end
    A --> D["Operator places Sample Volume (SV) on the B-mode image at the point of interest"]
    D --> C
    B --> D
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Doppler Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECHOS["Received RF Echoes"] --> DEMOD["Demodulation (Quadrature Detection)"]
    DEMOD --> WALLFILTER["Wall Filter (High-Pass)\nRemoves vessel wall motion clutter"]
    WALLFILTER --> FFT["Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)\nFrequency → Velocity Spectrum"]
    FFT --> DISPLAY["Spectral Display\n(Velocity vs. Time)"]
    FFT --> COLORMAP["Color Map Processor\n(Mean velocity + variance)"]
    COLORMAP --> OVERLAY["Color Overlay on B-Mode Image"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)** is the heart of spectral Doppler. It decomposes the complex Doppler-shifted signal into its constituent frequencies, which are then mapped to velocities using the Doppler equation. This is what produces the characteristic waveform shape clinicians analyze.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCEBT502]**
**Define Duplex Scanning. List its two main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Duplex scanning is a non-invasive ultrasound imaging technique that combines real-time B-mode (brightness-mode) grayscale anatomical imaging with Doppler ultrasound (spectral and/or color flow) to simultaneously visualize tissue structure and assess blood flow hemodynamics within a single integrated examination.

**Two Main Components (2 marks):**
1. **B-mode Ultrasonography** — Provides 2D grayscale anatomical cross-sectional images of tissues and vessel walls.
2. **Doppler Ultrasonography** — Provides blood flow information (velocity, direction, character) using either Pulsed-Wave (PW) spectral Doppler, Continuous-Wave (CW) Doppler, or Color Flow Doppler mapping.

> **Marking Note:** Full 3 marks require both the definition *and* both components with one-line functions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCEBT502]**
**State the Doppler equation and explain the significance of the angle $\theta$ in duplex scanning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Doppler Equation (1 mark):**

$$
v = \frac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

where $v$ = blood flow velocity, $c$ = speed of sound in tissue (1540 m/s), $f_d$ = Doppler shift frequency, $f_0$ = transmitted frequency, $\theta$ = angle between ultrasound beam and blood flow direction.

**Significance of $\theta$ (2 marks):**
- The velocity $v$ is **inversely proportional to $\cos\theta$**, meaning the angle directly affects the computed velocity.
- When $\theta = 0°$ (beam parallel to flow): $\cos\theta = 1$ → maximum measurable Doppler shift.
- When $\theta = 90°$ (beam perpendicular to flow): $\cos\theta = 0$ → velocity becomes mathematically undefined; **no Doppler shift is detected**.
- **Clinical practice:** The beam is always steered so that $\theta \leq 60°$, with the **optimal range of 30° to 60°**, to minimize velocity errors caused by small misalignments.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCEBT502, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a duplex ultrasound scanner. Describe how B-mode and Doppler information are acquired and displayed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Doppler velocity equation $v = \dfrac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture of a Duplex Scanner (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TxBlock["Transmit Subsystem"]
        P["Pulser / Beamformer"] --> CRX["Piezoelectric Array (Transmit Mode)"]
    end
    subgraph PatBlock["Patient"]
        CRX -->|US pulses| TIS["Tissue & RBCs"]
    end
    subgraph RxBlock["Receive & Processing Subsystem"]
        TIS -->|Echoes| CRX2["Piezoelectric Array (Receive Mode)"]
        CRX2 --> AMP["TGC Amplifier"]
        AMP --> SPLIT{"Signal Splitter"}
        SPLIT --> BP["B-Mode Processor → Grayscale Image"]
        SPLIT --> DP["Doppler Processor → Spectral / Color Map"]
        BP --> DISP["Integrated Display"]
        DP --> DISP
    end
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Transmission:** The pulser excites the piezoelectric crystal array, generating short bursts (pulses) of ultrasound at the resonant frequency $f_0$ (typically 2 – 10 MHz for vascular work). **(1 mark)**
2. **Interaction with Tissue:** The ultrasound beam propagates through soft tissue. A small fraction is backscattered from **stationary anatomical structures** (vessel walls, organ boundaries) and from **moving red blood cells**. **(1 mark)**
3. **Reception:** The same array receives returning echoes. The received RF signal contains both anatomical (high-amplitude, stationary) and Doppler (frequency-shifted, moving) information. **(1 mark)**
4. **Signal Splitting & Dual Processing:** The amplified echo is split into two parallel processing chains:
   - **B-mode path:** Amplitude envelope is detected and mapped to grayscale pixel intensity → 2D anatomical image.
   - **Doppler path:** Quadrature demodulation extracts frequency shift → FFT produces velocity spectrum → displayed as time-velocity waveform and/or color overlay. **(1 mark)**
5. **Display:** The B-mode image and Doppler spectrum (or color map) are displayed simultaneously — usually with the B-mode image on top and the spectral tracing at the bottom, with a sample volume marker visible on the B-mode image. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Derivation of the Doppler Equation (7 marks):**

**Setup (1 mark):** Let a stationary transducer transmit at $f_0$ toward an RBC moving at velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam. Assume $v \ll c$ (clinical condition).

**First Doppler shift — Transducer to RBC (2 marks):**
The frequency of ultrasound perceived by the moving RBC is:

$$
f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c} \right)
$$

**Second Doppler shift — RBC to Transducer (2 marks):**
The RBC re-radiates at $f'$ in its own frame; the stationary transducer receives:

$$
f_r = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right) = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)
$$

**Define the Doppler shift (1 mark):**

$$
f_d = f_r - f_0 = f_0 \left[ \frac{c + v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta} - 1 \right] = f_0 \cdot \frac{2 v\cos\theta}{c - v\cos\theta}
$$

**Apply the clinical approximation $v \ll c$ (1 mark):**
Since $v \ll c$, the denominator $c - v\cos\theta \approx c$:

$$
f_d \approx \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}
$$

**Solve for velocity (the final form) (mark embedded in the box):**

$$
\boxed{\, v = \frac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta} \,}
$$

> **Assumption stated (implicit in approximation):** Blood velocity $v$ is negligible compared to the speed of sound $c$ in soft tissue.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCEBT502, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the clinical significance and measurement of **Peak Systolic Velocity (PSV)**, **End Diastolic Velocity (EDV)**, and **Resistive Index (RI)** in duplex scanning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A duplex scanner operates at $f_0 = 7.5$ MHz with the beam steered at $\theta = 45°$ to the direction of blood flow. If the measured Doppler shift is $f_d = 3500$ Hz, calculate:
   (i) The blood flow velocity $v$. **(4 marks)**
   (ii) Whether the velocity falls within the normal range for the common carotid artery. **(3 marks)**
   
   (Given: $c = 1540$ m/s; normal CCA PSV = 50 – 100 cm/s.)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Clinical Significance of PSV, EDV, and RI (7 marks):**

**PSV — Peak Systolic Velocity (2 marks):**
- The maximum velocity reached during the systolic phase of the cardiac cycle.
- Read directly from the peak of the spectral waveform envelope.
- **Clinical use:** Principal criterion for grading **stenosis** (e.g., in carotid arteries, PSV > 125 cm/s suggests ≥ 50% stenosis; PSV > 230 cm/s suggests ≥ 70% stenosis).
- Increases in stenosis due to **continuity equation** (narrower lumen → higher velocity to maintain flow).

**EDV — End Diastolic Velocity (2 marks):**
- The velocity at the end of diastole, just before the next systole.
- Read at the trailing edge of the spectral waveform.
- **Clinical use:** More reliable than PSV alone for **high-grade stenosis** (e.g., ICA EDV > 100 cm/s indicates ≥ 70% stenosis). Reflects the residual flow at low-pressure phase.

**RI — Resistive Index (3 marks):**

$$
RI = \frac{PSV - EDV}{PSV}
$$

- **Dimensionless**, ranging from 0 to 1.
- **High RI (> 0.8):** Suggests high downstream resistance — e.g., renal artery stenosis, external carotid, resting limb vessels, or distal obstruction.
- **Low RI (< 0.6):** Suggests low-resistance bed — e.g., internal carotid, hepatic, or normal renal parenchymal arteries.
- **Clinical use:** Routine in **renal duplex** to detect allograft rejection, renal artery stenosis, and obstructive uropathy.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Velocity calculation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify knowns (1 mark):**
- $c = 1540$ m/s
- $f_0 = 7.5 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $f_d = 3500$ Hz
- $\theta = 45° \Rightarrow \cos 45° = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$

**Step 2 — Apply the Doppler velocity equation (1 mark):**

$$
v = \frac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$
v = \frac{1540 \times 3500}{2 \times 7.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate (1 mark):**

$$
v = \frac{5{,}390{,}000}{10{,}606{,}500} \approx 0.508\, \text{m/s} = 50.8\, \text{cm/s}
$$

**(ii) Clinical interpretation (3 marks):**

The normal common carotid artery (CCA) PSV range is **50 – 100 cm/s**.
- Computed PSV = **50.8 cm/s** falls at the **lower end of the normal range**.
- Therefore, the velocity is **within normal limits** for the CCA.
