**Definition (2 marks):** Ultrafast plane-wave imaging is an ultrasound acquisition technique in which a single unfocused wavefront is transmitted across the entire transducer aperture, illuminating the full field of view in one transmit event. This replaces the sequential line-by-line focused transmission of conventional imaging.

**Mathematical Derivation (5 marks):**

For a maximum imaging depth $d$, the maximum PRF is:
$$PRF_{max} = \frac{c}{2d}$$

In conventional focused-beam imaging with $L$ lines per image, one line is acquired per transmit event, so the frame rate is:
$$f_{frame,conv} = \frac{PRF_{max}}{L} = \frac{c}{2dL}$$

In ultrafast plane-wave imaging, the entire image is acquired in one transmit event, so:
$$f_{frame,ultrafast} = PRF_{max} = \frac{c}{2d}$$

The ratio of frame rates is:
$$\frac{f_{frame,ultrafast}}{f_{frame,conv}} = \frac{c/2d}{c/2dL} = L$$

Thus, ultrafast imaging achieves a frame rate that is **$L$ times** higher than conventional imaging, where $L$ is the number of image lines.

**(b) Clinical Applications (7 marks):**

1. **Intraoperative Neurosurgical Guidance (1.5 marks):** fUS provides real-time vascular and functional maps during tumor resection, helping surgeons avoid critical functional areas and identify residual tumor perfusion.

2. **Stroke and Traumatic Brain Injury Monitoring (1.5 marks):** fUS can longitudinally track perfusion deficits and recovery at the bedside, offering a non-ionizing alternative to CT perfusion and a portable alternative to MRI.

3. **Neuroscience Research in Animal Models (1.5 marks):** fUS enables whole-brain functional imaging in awake, behaving rodents at sub-100 μm resolution, allowing chronic studies of brain function that would be impossible with fMRI (which requires anesthesia).

4. **Neonatal Brain Imaging (1.5 marks):** Through the open fontanelle, fUS can image the neonatal brain non-invasively, providing functional hemodynamic data without ionizing radiation or sedation.

5. **Epilepsy Focus Localization (1 mark):** fUS can map ictal and interictal blood flow changes, helping identify the seizure onset zone for surgical planning.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Power Doppler with Color Doppler:** Power Doppler measures **energy** (always positive, angle-independent), while Color Doppler measures **signed velocity** (angle-dependent, aliasable). Do NOT interchange these in answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Doppler shift:** The factor of 2 comes from the **double Doppler shift** (transmit + receive). Omitting it loses marks.
> 3. **Writing $v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_0}$ without $\cos\theta$:** The angle is essential. If you drop it, you are missing a fundamental aspect of Doppler physics.
> 4. **Mixing up frame rate formulas:** Conventional frame rate = $PRF / L$; ultrafast frame rate ≈ $PRF$ (not $PRF \times L$).
> 5. **Stating "fUS measures neural activity directly":** This is FALSE. fUS measures **hemodynamic changes** that are *correlated* with neural activity via neurovascular coupling. It is an **indirect** measure.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Functional Ultrasound (fUS)** is a Doppler-based hemodynamic imaging modality that uses **ultrafast plane-wave imaging** to map tissue perfusion as a surrogate for neural/tissue activity.
- **Ultrafast imaging** achieves frame rates of **5,000–20,000 fps** by transmitting single broad plane waves instead of sequentially focused beams — a speedup factor of $L$ (image lines).
- The **Doppler shift equation** is $\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$, with the factor of 2 arising from the **double Doppler shift** in pulse-echo mode.
- **Power Doppler** computes the temporal energy of clutter-filtered IQ signals and is preferred over Color Doppler in fUS because it is **angle-independent, aliasing-free, and more sensitive to slow microvascular flow**.
- **Clutter filtering** (SVD or polynomial regression) is essential to remove tissue motion (1–10 cm/s) and isolate capillary flow (1–10 mm/s).
- **Neurovascular coupling** is the physiological basis: neural activity → increased CBF/CBV → increased Power Doppler signal.
- **Clinical applications** include intraoperative neurosurgical guidance, stroke/TBI monitoring, neonatal brain imaging, epilepsy focus localization, tumor perfusion assessment, and preclinical neuroscience research.
- **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c = 1540$ m/s (a constant to memorize).
- **Typical fUS frequency range:** 2–10 MHz (lower for transcranial imaging due to skull attenuation).
- **Detectable vessel diameter in fUS:** ≥ 100 μm, with minimum velocity sensitivity of ~1 mm/s.
- **Round-trip time-of-flight** determines the maximum PRF and hence the maximum imaging depth: $d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$.
- **Nyquist velocity limit** for Doppler: $v_{Nyquist} = \frac{PRF \cdot c}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$ — exceeding this causes aliasing, which is why Power Doppler (energy-based) is preferred.
- **Remember the limitations:** fUS is **indirect** (measures hemodynamics, not neural activity), requires **acoustic windows** (skull limits adult transcranial fUS), and is sensitive to **motion artifacts**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Therapeutic Uses of Ultrasound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT502 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Therapeutic Uses of Ultrasound # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Therapeutic Ultrasound** refers to the controlled application of acoustic energy within the frequency range of **20 kHz to 10 MHz** (typically 0.5–3 MHz for physiotherapy and 1–4 MHz for High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound) for clinical purposes other than diagnostic imaging. Unlike diagnostic ultrasound which uses low intensities (typically **< 720 mW/cm² SPTA**) to produce images, therapeutic applications deliberately use higher intensities (**0.5 W/cm² to 10,000 W/cm²**) to induce **thermal, mechanical, and biochemical effects** in tissues. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)** > Therapeutic applications utilize the *non-linear* and *high-energy* interactions of ultrasound with biological tissue. The fundamental principle is **controlled energy deposition** in a targeted tissue volume, which is governed by the **Acoustic Intensity (I)**, **Frequency (f)**, **Exposure Duration (t)**, and **Mode of Application** (continuous vs. pulsed). > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction (Diagnostic vs. Therapeutic)** > - **Diagnostic**: Low power, real-time imaging, non-destructive — Goal = *Visualize* tissue. > - **Therapeutic**: High power, controlled energy deposition, often destructive — Goal = *Modify or destroy* tissue, or stimulate a healing process. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine holding a magnifying glass under bright sunlight. When the lens is far from the paper, nothing much happens. But when you carefully adjust the distance, all the sunlight concentrates into a single tiny bright spot — and the paper begins to *smoke and burn*. Ultrasound therapy works on the same principle: - The **ultrasound transducer** is like the "lens" of the magnifying glass. - The **tissue medium** is like the paper. - The **focused acoustic beam** concentrates mechanical vibration energy at a specific point inside the body. - Depending on how much energy is concentrated and for how long, the tissue at the focal point will either **heat up gently** (like warm sunshine → physiotherapy), **heat up severely** (like the burning paper → HIFU tumour ablation), or **break apart mechanically** (like shaking a brittle object until it cracks → lithotripsy for kidney stones). > [!NOTE] > **Why use sound waves for therapy?** > Sound can be tightly **focused** (beam widths on the order of millimetres), can penetrate **deep into the body** without surgery, and its parameters (intensity, frequency, pulse duration) can be precisely controlled in real-time — making it a "**scalpel without a blade**". ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Therapeutic Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Frequency (f) | **0.5 – 4** | MHz | | Intensity (I, SPTA) | **0.5 – 10,000** | W/cm² | | Treatment Duration | **3 – 30** | minutes | | Speed of sound in soft tissue (c) | **1540** | m/s | | Acoustic impedance of soft tissue (Z) | **1.6 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) (Rayl) | | Attenuation coefficient (α) at 1 MHz | **0.5 – 1.0** | dB/cm | | Focal zone (HIFU) | **1 × 1 × 10** | mm (typical) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle All therapeutic applications of ultrasound rest on **three biophysical interaction mechanisms** between the acoustic wave and the tissue: 1. **Thermal Effect** — Absorption of acoustic energy → conversion to heat → temperature rise. 2. **Mechanical (Non-Thermal) Effect** — Acoustic pressure variations, radiation force, microstreaming, and cavitation. 3. **Biochemical / Biological Effect** — Cellular membrane perturbation, altered diffusion, increased enzymatic activity, enhanced drug uptake (sonoporation). The relative dominance of each effect is governed by the **Intensity–Frequency–Time** triplet and the acoustic properties of the target tissue. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Mechanism Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Thermal Mechanism (Hyperthermia & Ablation) - **Step 1 — Absorption**: As the longitudinal pressure wave propagates, molecules oscillate; viscous friction converts part of the mechanical energy into heat. The rate of heat deposition per unit volume is given by the **specific absorption rate (SAR)**. - **Step 2 — Heat Conduction**: Heat diffuses from the heated region to surrounding tissue by thermal conduction (governed by tissue thermal conductivity, **k ≈ 0.5 W/(m·K)** for soft tissue). - **Step 3 — Bio-effect**: - **41 – 45 °C**: Hyperthermia — reversible cellular changes, enhanced drug uptake, increased blood flow. - **45 – 60 °C**: Coagulative necrosis — proteins denature, cells die (used in **HIFU tumour ablation**). - **> 100 °C**: Vaporization, boiling, mechanical disruption (cavitation, mechanical destruction). ### 2.2.2 Mechanical Mechanism (Cavitation & Radiation Force) - **Acoustic Streaming**: The time-averaged momentum transfer from the wave to the fluid generates a steady circulatory flow, enhancing mass transport and drug diffusion. - **Radiation Force**: A unidirectional force acting on absorbers/refractors, given by $F = \frac{W}{c}$ (W = acoustic power, c = speed of sound). Used in *acoustic radiation force impulse (ARFI)* elastography and physiotherapy micromassage. - **Cavitation**: - **Stable Cavitation**: Gas bubbles oscillate non-destructively, producing microstreaming and shear stresses. - **Inertial (Transient) Cavitation**: Bubbles violently collapse → microjets → free radicals, local shock waves, mechanical tissue disruption (used in **lithotripsy** and **histotripsy**). ### 2.2.3 Pulsed vs Continuous Wave Operation - **Continuous Wave (CW)**: Constant output → predominantly thermal effect → used in physiotherapy, hyperthermia. - **Pulsed Wave (PW)**: Short bursts with low duty cycle → minimal heating, dominant mechanical effects → used in LIPUS (Low-Intensity Pulsed Ultrasound) for bone healing and sonoporation. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Formula Reference — Memorise These Equations** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = \frac{P_{avg}}{A}$ | Acoustic Intensity (spatial average) | W/m² | | 2 | $I = \frac{p^{2}}{2 \rho c}$ | Intensity from peak pressure amplitude $p$ | W/m² | | 3 | $I(x) = I_{0} \cdot e^{-2\alpha x}$ | Attenuated intensity at depth $x$ (Nep log) | W/m² | | 4 | $\alpha = \alpha_{0} \cdot f^{n}$ | Attenuation coefficient, n ≈ 1 for soft tissue | dB/(cm·MHz) | | 5 | $\text{Half-power depth} = \frac{\ln 2}{2\alpha} = \frac{3.0}{2\alpha_{dB}}$ | Depth at which intensity is halved | cm | | 6 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Acoustic dose (energy per unit area) | J/cm² | | 7 | $\text{TI} = \frac{W_{p}}{\sqrt{f}}$ | Thermal Index (FDA / IEC 62359) | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{MI} = \frac{p_{r,0.3}}{\sqrt{f}}$ | Mechanical Index | dimensionless | | 9 | $F_{rad} = \frac{I \cdot A}{c} = \frac{W}{c}$ | Acoustic radiation force | N | | 10 | $T(t) - T_{0} = \frac{2 \alpha I}{\rho c_{p}} \cdot t$ | Temperature rise under CW (no cooling) | K | | 11 | $R = \frac{\rho_{2} c_{2} - \rho_{1} c_{1}}{\rho_{2} c_{2} + \rho_{1} c_{1}}$ | Intensity Reflection Coefficient | dimensionless | | 12 | $f_{r} = f_{0} \cdot \frac{c}{c \pm v}$ | Doppler frequency (reference, not always needed) | Hz | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility** > - The **Thermal Index (TI)** and **Mechanical Index (MI)** are mandatory safety readouts on every modern diagnostic and therapeutic scanner (per IEC 62359 / FDA Track 3). They are *real-time predictors* of potential bio-effects. > - The **Half-Power Depth** directly determines the maximum safe treatment depth for a given transducer frequency. > - The **Acoustic Radiation Force** equation underpins *shear wave elastography*, which is a quantitative diagnostic extension of therapeutic ultrasound physics. ## 2.4 Why These Mechanisms Matter — Engineering Perspective | Application | Dominant Mechanism | Typical Parameters | Clinical Goal | |---|---|---|---| | Physiotherapy | Thermal + streaming | 1 MHz, 1–2 W/cm², CW, 5–10 min | Pain relief, tissue healing | | HIFU (tumour ablation) | Thermal (coagulation) | 1–4 MHz, 1000–10000 W/cm² focal, pulsed | Destroy tumour without surgery | | Lithotripsy (ESWL) | Mechanical (cavitation + shock) | ~20–40 kHz equivalent shock, focused | Fragment kidney stones | | Hyperthermia (cancer adjunct) | Thermal (41–45 °C) | 0.5–1.5 MHz, 1–5 W/cm² | Sensitize tumour to radiotherapy/chemo | | LIPUS (bone healing) | Mechanical (streaming) | 1.5 MHz, 30 mW/cm², pulsed (20% duty) | Accelerate fracture repair | | Sonoporation (drug delivery) | Mechanical (cavitation) | 1–3 MHz, microbubbles + US | Open cell membranes for drug/gene uptake | | Histotripsy | Mechanical (inertial cavitation) | 1–2 MHz, high pressure, microsecond pulses | Mechanical liquefaction of tissue | | Phacoemulsification (eye surgery) | Mechanical (cavitation) | ~40 kHz | Emulsify cataract lens | | HIFU (cosmetic fat reduction) | Thermal (apoptosis) | 200 kHz, focused | Selective adipocyte destruction | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Acoustic Intensity from Pressure Amplitude **Goal**: Show that $I = \frac{p^{2}}{2\rho c}$ for a plane progressive wave in a non-attenuating medium. **Starting point — particle velocity of a plane wave**: For a plane harmonic acoustic wave propagating in +x direction: $$p(x,t) = p_{0}\cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 1 — Apply linearised Euler equation** (relates particle velocity $u$ to pressure gradient): $$\rho \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate the pressure equation with respect to $x$**: $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = p_{0} \cdot k \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 3 — Integrate with respect to time** to recover $u(x,t)$: $$u(x,t) = -\frac{p_{0} k}{\rho \omega}\int \sin(\omega t - kx)\, dt = \frac{p_{0} k}{\rho \omega^{2}}\cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 4 — Recognise the wave relation** $c = \frac{\omega}{k}$, hence $\frac{k}{\omega} = \frac{1}{c}$: $$u(x,t) = \frac{p_{0}}{\rho c}\cos(\omega t - kx)$$ This means the **specific acoustic impedance** is $Z = \rho c$ and $p = Z u$ (analogue of Ohm's law $V = IR$). **Step 5 — Write the instantaneous intensity** as $I_{inst} = p \cdot u$: $$I_{inst} = p_{0}\cos(\omega t - kx) \cdot \frac{p_{0}}{\rho c}\cos(\omega t - kx) = \frac{p_{0}^{2}}{\rho c}\cos^{2}(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 6 — Take the time average** over one full cycle. Using $\langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle = \frac{1}{2}$: $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{p_{0}^{2}}{2\rho c} = \frac{p_{rms}^{2}}{\rho c}$$ where $p_{rms} = \frac{p_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}$. **Final Result**: $$I = \frac{p^{2}}{2 \rho c}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **fundamental link** between acoustic pressure (what a hydrophone measures) and intensity (what governs bio-effects). The impedance $Z = \rho c$ of soft tissue is about **1.6 × 10⁶ Rayl**, so a 1 MPa pressure corresponds to roughly **I ≈ 0.3 W/cm²**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Depth of Maximum Thermal Coagulation (HIFU) **Goal**: Determine the depth at which the *in situ* intensity is sufficient to raise focal tissue temperature to 60 °C, given surface intensity $I_0$, frequency $f$, attenuation $\alpha$, and exposure time $t$. **Step 1 — Intensity at depth $x$** (using Nep log form): $$I(x) = I_{0}\, e^{-2\alpha x}$$ **Step 2 — Heat deposition rate per unit volume** (specific absorption rate): $$\text{SAR}(x) = 2\alpha \cdot I(x) = 2\alpha I_{0}\, e^{-2\alpha x}$$ The factor 2 converts Nep to Np/m using the I = I₀e^{-2αx} form with α in Nep/m; if α is in dB/cm, use $2\alpha_{dB} = 4.343 \cdot 2\alpha_{Nep}$. **Step 3 — Temperature rise under short pulse** (negligible perfusion, no conduction): $$\Delta T(x,t) = \frac{\text{SAR}(x)}{\rho\, c_{p}}\cdot t = \frac{2\alpha I_{0}\, e^{-2\alpha x}}{\rho c_{p}} \cdot t$$ where $c_p$ is the specific heat capacity of soft tissue (≈ **3600 J/(kg·K)**). **Step 4 — Threshold condition** for coagulative necrosis ($\Delta T \geq 20$ K starting from 37 °C → 57 °C, or 25 K → 62 °C): $$\frac{2\alpha I_{0}\, e^{-2\alpha x_{f}}}{\rho c_{p}} \cdot t = \Delta T_{thresh}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the focal treatment depth $x_f$**: $$e^{2\alpha x_{f}} = \frac{2\alpha I_{0} t}{\rho c_{p} \Delta T_{thresh}}$$ $$x_{f} = \frac{1}{2\alpha}\ln\left(\frac{2\alpha I_{0} t}{\rho c_{p} \Delta T_{thresh}}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Numerical sanity check** (HIFU prostate, $f$ = 1.5 MHz, $I_0$ = 5000 W/cm², $t$ = 3 s, $\alpha$ = 0.6 dB/cm/MHz → 9 dB/m = 1.04 Np/m, $\rho$ = 1060 kg/m³, $c_p$ = 3600 J/(kg·K), $\Delta T$ = 25 K): $$\text{Numerator inside ln} = \frac{2 \cdot 1.04 \cdot 5\times 10^{6} \cdot 3}{1060 \cdot 3600 \cdot 25} = \frac{3.12 \times 10^{7}}{9.54 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.327$$ Since the argument is **< 1**, the log is negative, meaning $\Delta T < 25$ K — the focal point is **deeper** than the half-power depth. The system would either: - Increase $I_0$ (raise acoustic power), or - Increase $t$ (longer sonication), or - Move focus shallower. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Pattern**: The equation for $x_f$ is the most frequently tested derivation in the *Therapeutic Ultrasound* module. Students are expected to derive the temperature-rise form **and** solve it for a clinical scenario. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cavitation Threshold vs Frequency The **mechanical index (MI)** is the FDA-defined predictor of cavitation risk: $$\text{MI} = \frac{p_{r,0.3}}{\sqrt{f}}$$ where $p_{r,0.3}$ is the derated rarefactional peak pressure in **MPa**, and $f$ is in **MHz**. | $f$ (MHz) | Threshold $p_{r}$ (MPa) for MI = 0.3 | Threshold $p_{r}$ (MPa) for MI = 0.7 | |---|---|---| | 0.5 | 0.21 | 0.49 | | 1.0 | 0.30 | 0.70 | | 1.5 | 0.37 | 0.86 | | 2.0 | 0.42 | 0.99 | | 3.0 | 0.52 | 1.21 | | 5.0 | 0.67 | 1.56 | > [!NOTE] > **Higher frequency → higher pressure is required to initiate inertial cavitation.** This is why **HIFU at 1–2 MHz** can deliver destructive mechanical effects with pressure pulses of ~10–20 MPa (extremely high MI > 5), while diagnostic scanners limit MI ≤ 1.9 to avoid cavitation injury. ## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-style 14-mark style derivation) **Problem**: Calculate the acoustic intensity delivered by a 1 MHz physiotherapy transducer that produces a peak rarefactional pressure of 0.5 MPa in soft tissue. Given $\rho$ = 1060 kg/m³ and $c$ = 1540 m/s. **Step 1 — Identify the relevant equation**: $$I = \frac{p^{2}}{2\rho c}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values** (note: 0.5 MPa = 0.5 × 10⁶ Pa): $$I = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 1060 \times 1540}$$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator**: $$(0.5 \times 10^{6})^{2} = 0.25 \times 10^{12} = 2.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Compute denominator**: $$2 \times 1060 \times 1540 = 3,264,800 \approx 3.265 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{s)}$$ **Step 5 — Divide and convert to W/cm²**: $$I = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{11}}{3.265 \times 10^{6}} = 7.66 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2}$$ Converting: $1 \text{ W/cm}^{2} = 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2}$, so: $$I = \frac{7.66 \times 10^{4}}{10^{4}} = 7.66 \text{ W/cm}^{2}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **THERAPEUTIC level** of intensity — about 10× higher than a typical diagnostic scanner (which operates at ~0.05 W/cm² SPTA). Continuous application would rapidly cause thermal damage; hence therapeutic devices use duty cycles and motion. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Pattern Reference (Module 1, PCEBT502)** > - **Part A (3 marks each)**: Short conceptual / definition / formula questions. ~2–3 such questions per module. > - **Part B (14 marks each)**: ESE Module-Level Choice. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (7 + 7 marks). Internal choice is provided *between* two 14-mark questions. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **Differentiate between thermal and non-thermal (mechanical) bio-effects of therapeutic ultrasound. Give one clinical example of each.** **Model Answer**: | Aspect | Thermal Effect | Non-Thermal (Mechanical) Effect | |---|---|---| | **Cause** | Absorption of acoustic energy converted to heat | Radiation force, streaming, cavitation | | **Frequency Dependence** | Strong (α ∝ fⁿ) | Moderate | | **Time Dependence** | Cumulative (longer t → more heating) | Often transient | | **Dose Metric** | Intensity × time (=Q, J/cm²) | Peak pressure, MI | | **Clinical Example** | **HIFU tumour ablation** (coagulative necrosis at >60 °C) | **LIPUS bone healing** (streaming at low intensity, 30 mW/cm²) | | **Regulatory Predictor** | Thermal Index (TI) | Mechanical Index (MI) | *(3 marks: 1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for example.)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **Define Thermal Index (TI) and Mechanical Index (MI). What is the FDA-recommended upper limit for MI in diagnostic ultrasound?** **Model Answer**: - **Thermal Index (TI)**: $TI = \frac{W_{p}}{\sqrt{f}}$ — a real-time, dimensionless estimate of the maximum temperature rise (in °C) that could occur at the location of interest. $W_p$ is the *derated* acoustic power in mW and $f$ is frequency in MHz. - **Mechanical Index (MI)**: $MI = \frac{p_{r,0.3}}{\sqrt{f}}$ — a dimensionless predictor of the likelihood of inertial (transient) cavitation. $p_{r,0.3}$ is the derated peak rarefactional pressure in MPa and $f$ is in MHz. - **FDA upper limit for diagnostic MI**: **1.9**. *(3 marks: 1 each for TI, MI, and the limit.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **physical mechanism of High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU)** for non-invasive tumour ablation. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the transducer geometry, focal zone dimensions, and the typical temperature–time protocol used. **Model Solution**: 1. **Principle**: HIFU uses a **concave (spherical cap) or phased-array transducer** that emits high-frequency ultrasound (1–4 MHz) which converges at a **geometric focus** typically 8–15 cm deep in tissue. At the focus, the beam is concentrated to a millimetre-scale volume (typical focal zone **1 × 1 × 10 mm**), producing an *in situ* intensity of **1000 – 10 000 W/cm²**. 2. **Energy deposition**: Tissue in the focal zone absorbs acoustic energy at a rate $\text{SAR} = 2\alpha I$. With $\alpha \approx 0.5$ dB/cm at 1 MHz and $I = 5000$ W/cm², the focal SAR is enormous, causing the temperature to climb at **$\approx 30$–$100$ °C/s**. 3. **Thermal protocol**: - **Threshold for coagulative necrosis**: 56–60 °C held for ≥ 1 s. - **Typical clinical sonication**: 3–5 s ON, 10–30 s OFF (to allow cooling of intervening tissue, sparing the skin). - Repeated **adjacent sonications** are stacked to ablate the entire tumour volume. 4. **Why focused geometry?** - Surface intensity is kept **below the skin-damage threshold** (~100 W/cm²). - Pre-focal tissue experiences only mild heating (negligible bio-effect) due to the diverging beam. - Post-focal intensity falls off rapidly — protects deep critical structures. 5. **Diagram requirement**: Show the curved transducer emitting converging wavefronts, marking the focal zone, and a thermal lesion of ellipsoidal shape inside the tumour. *(7 marks: 2 for principle, 2 for thermal protocol, 2 for safety/geometry, 1 for diagram.)* **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **expression for the temperature rise at depth $x$** under continuous-wave ultrasound exposure, neglecting perfusion and thermal conduction. A 2 MHz HIFU system delivers $I_0 = 1000$ W/cm² at the surface. Calculate the temperature rise at $x = 3$ cm after $t = 5$ s, given $\alpha = 0.6$ dB/cm/MHz, $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, and $c_p = 3600$ J/(kg·K). **Model Solution**: **Derivation (3 marks)**: The acoustic intensity at depth $x$ in an attenuating medium is: $$I(x) = I_{0}\, e^{-2\alpha x}$$ where $\alpha$ is in **Nep/m** (1 Np/m = 4.343 dB/m). The volumetric rate of heat deposition (SAR) is: $$\text{SAR} = 2\alpha I(x) = 2\alpha I_{0}\, e^{-2\alpha x}$$ Assuming all absorbed energy remains in the local volume (no perfusion, no conduction), the temperature rise is: $$\Delta T(x,t) = \frac{\text{SAR} \cdot t}{\rho c_{p}} = \frac{2\alpha I_{0} t}{\rho c_{p}}\, e^{-2\alpha x}$$ **Numerical calculation (4 marks)**: - Convert $\alpha$: $\alpha = 0.6 \text{ dB/cm/MHz} \times 2 \text{ MHz} = 1.2 \text{ dB/cm} = 120 \text{ dB/m}$. - Convert to Nep/m: $\alpha = 120 / 8.686 = 13.82 \text{ Np/m}$. - Depth $x = 3$ cm $= 0.03$ m → $2\alpha x = 2 \times 13.82 \times 0.03 = 0.829$. - $e^{-2\alpha x} = e^{-0.829} = 0.4366$. - $I_0 = 1000 \text{ W/cm}^{2} = 10^{7} \text{ W/m}^{2}$. - $\Delta T = \dfrac{2 \times 13.82 \times 10^{7} \times 5}{1060 \times 3600} \times 0.4366$ Numerator: $2 \times 13.82 \times 5 \times 10^{7} = 13.82 \times 10^{8}$ Denominator: $1060 \times 3600 = 3.816 \times 10^{6}$ Ratio: $(13.82 \times 10^{8}) / (3.816 \times 10^{6}) = 362.1$ Multiply by 0.4366 → $\Delta T = 158$ K > [!WARNING] > **This is a clinically *absurd* result** — and that's the *point*. The 158 K rise is **physically impossible** (water would vaporise first). In reality, **boiling/cavitation** will cap the temperature, perfusion cools the tissue, and the duty cycle is reduced. KTU Examiners often pose such numbers to test whether students *apply the assumptions critically*. A perfect answer should add the comment: *"In practice, this temperature is limited by phase change, cavitation, perfusion, and thermal diffusion — actual in-vivo rise is < 30 K."* **Correct clinical interpretation**: Even though the formula gives an unrealistically high value, it confirms that **CW HIFU at 1000 W/cm²** is overwhelmingly capable of coagulative necrosis, and the limiting factor is **safety at the surface and in the near field**, not energy delivery at the focus. --- ### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanism of Extracorporeal Shock Wave Lithotripsy (ESWL)** for the treatment of kidney stones. Compare the acoustic parameters and dominant physical mechanism with that of HIFU. **Model Solution**: 1. **Clinical problem**: Renal calculi (kidney stones, e.g., calcium oxalate) are hard crystalline masses that can obstruct the urinary tract. ESWL non-invasively fragments them into sand-like particles that can be naturally passed. 2. **Waveform**: ESWL uses **highly focused, short-duration, high-pressure shock waves** (~10–20 MPa peak positive, ~5–10 MPa peak negative, pulse duration < 1 µs), typically generated by: - **Electrohydraulic** (spark in water), or - **Electromagnetic** (coil repels a metallic membrane), or - **Piezoelectric** (array of PZT crystals). 3. **Focusing**: An **ellipsoidal reflector** or a **spherical cap array** focuses the shock wave onto the stone (F1) located at the second focus of the reflector. Coupling is via a water-filled cushion to eliminate air-tissue impedance mismatch. 4. **Mechanisms of stone fragmentation**: - **Compressive failure** at the leading surface of the stone from the high positive pressure. - **Tensile (spallation) failure** at the trailing surface, where the rarefactional component exceeds the tensile strength of the stone (~1–10 MPa). - **Inertial cavitation** in the urine adjacent to the stone: bubble collapse produces **microjets and shock waves** that erode the stone surface. - **Acoustic streaming** sweeps away debris and exposes fresh surface. 5. **Comparison with HIFU**: | Feature | ESWL | HIFU | |---|---|---| | Frequency / Waveform | Broadband shock, ~hundreds of kHz spectral centre | Narrowband 1–4 MHz sinusoidal | | Peak Pressure | 10–20 MPa | 5–15 MPa at focus | | Dominant Mechanism | Mechanical (cavitation, spallation) | Thermal (coagulation) | | Pulse Duration | < 1 µs | 0.1–10 s | | Treatment Time | 30–60 min | 1–3 hours | | Imaging Guidance | Fluoroscopy / ultrasound | MRI or ultrasound | | Anesthesia | Sedation / anesthesia | Conscious sedation | *(7 marks: 2 for ESWL mechanism, 2 for stone-fragmentation physics, 2 for comparison, 1 for imaging/anesthesia.)* **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of **Low-Intensity Pulsed Ultrasound (LIPUS)** for accelerated bone fracture healing. State the typical acoustic parameters used and the proposed biological mechanism. **Model Solution**: 1. **Clinical background**: 5–10% of bone fractures fail to unite (non-union). LIPUS is an FDA-approved daily outpatient treatment (20 min/day) that stimulates osteogenesis. 2. **Typical parameters**: - Frequency: **1.5 MHz** - Intensity: **30 mW/cm²** SATA (extremely low — *sub-thermal*) - Mode: **Pulsed** (200 µs ON, 800 µs OFF → 20% duty cycle) - Daily exposure: **20 minutes** 3. **Why pulsed low intensity?** - The intensity is **two orders of magnitude below** the thermal threshold — so the mechanism is **non-thermal**. - Pulsing minimises any residual heating and creates **precisely timed mechanical cues** for cells. 4. **Proposed biological mechanism (mechanotransduction)**: - Acoustic radiation force and microstreaming apply a **gentle, cyclic mechanical strain** (∼ microstrain) on cell membranes of osteoblasts, chondrocytes, and mesenchymal stem cells. - This activates **integrin–cytoskeleton–nucleus signalling pathways** (FAK, MAPK, ERK1/2). - **Upregulation** of bone morphogenetic proteins (BMP-2, BMP-7), cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), prostaglandin E₂, and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). - Result: enhanced **osteoblast proliferation**, **collagen synthesis**, **mineralisation**, and **angiogenesis** at the fracture callus. - LIPUS also modulates **nitric oxide** and **calcium ion flux**, which are second messengers for bone remodelling. 5. **Engineering relevance**: LIPUS is the clinical benchmark showing that ultrasound can produce *therapeutic* effects **without significant heat** — proving the **mechanical / streaming channel** of bio-effect. *(7 marks: 2 for parameters, 3 for biological mechanism, 1 for engineering significance, 1 for clinical context.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in "Therapeutic Ultrasound"** > 1. **Confusing SPTA, SATA, and SPPA intensities** — KTU asks for the *specific* temporal average being used. State it explicitly (SPTA = Spatial-Peak Time-Average; SATA = Spatial-Average Time-Average; SPPA = Spatial-Peak Pulse-Average). > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the attenuation equation** — Students write $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ instead of $I_0 e^{-2\alpha x}$ (Nep log) and lose 1 mark. > 3. **Mixing dB/cm/MHz with dB/cm** — Always convert: $\alpha(f) = \alpha_0 \cdot f^n$ with $f$ in MHz. Then divide by 4.343 to convert dB → Nep. > 4. **Not naming the dominant mechanism** for a given therapy — e.g., calling LIPUS "thermal" loses full credit; it is **non-thermal / streaming**. > 5. **Skipping the diagram** — A 7-mark sub-question with a "neat diagram" instruction gives **1 dedicated mark** for the figure. Always include a labelled block diagram or schematic. > 6. **Failing to state the FDA / IEC threshold** (MI ≤ 1.9, TI ≤ 6) when discussing safety. > 7. **Using "ultrasound diathermy" and "HIFU" interchangeably** — they differ by **3–4 orders of magnitude** in intensity. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Therapeutic Uses of Ultrasound (Module 1)** - **Three bio-effect mechanisms**: Thermal, Mechanical (streaming + radiation force), Cavitation (stable + inertial). - **Diagnostic vs. Therapeutic**: Diagnostic ≤ 720 mW/cm² SPTA (imaging); Therapeutic 0.5 – 10 000 W/cm² (heating or mechanical destruction). - **Key formula 1**: Acoustic intensity $I = \frac{p^{2}}{2\rho c}$; for soft tissue $Z = \rho c \approx 1.6 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl. - **Key formula 2**: Attenuation $I(x) = I_0 e^{-2\alpha x}$ (Nep form). Attenuation in soft tissue $\alpha \approx 0.5$ dB/cm at 1 MHz, scales as $f^{1.0}$. - **Key formula 3**: Temperature rise $\Delta T = \dfrac{2\alpha I_0 t}{\rho c_p} e^{-2\alpha x}$ (no perfusion, no conduction). - **Thermal Index** TI $= W_p / \sqrt{f}$ — predicts °C rise. - **Mechanical Index** MI $= p_{r,0.3}/\sqrt{f}$ — predicts cavitation risk; FDA limit **1.9**. - **HIFU**: 1–4 MHz, focused, 1000–10 000 W/cm² focal intensity, CW or long pulses, 60 °C → coagulative necrosis, used for uterine fibroids, prostate, liver, breast, bone. - **Lithotripsy (ESWL)**: 10–20 MPa shock waves, mechanical fragmentation, dominant cavitation + spallation. - **Hyperthermia**: 41–45 °C, 0.5–1.5 MHz, 1–5 W/cm², adjunct to chemo/radiotherapy. - **LIPUS**: 1.5 MHz, 30 mW/cm², 20% duty cycle — **non-thermal**, mechanotransduction, fracture healing. - **Sonoporation**: 1–3 MHz with microbubble contrast agents — reversible cell-membrane pores for drug/gene delivery. - **Histotripsy**: Microsecond pulses at high pressure → inertial cavitation cloud → mechanical tissue liquefaction. - **Phacoemulsification**: ~40 kHz, mechanical fragmentation of cataract lens. - **Half-power depth** = $\dfrac{3.0}{2\alpha_{dB/\text{cm}}}$ cm; choose $f$ so that target depth ≤ 1–1.5 × half-power depth. - **Duty cycle** controls the **thermal dose**; CW = max heating, PW = mechanical-dominant. - **Real-time guidance** for HIFU uses either **MR thermometry** (proton-resonance frequency shift method) or **diagnostic ultrasound elastography**.

topics.title: Principle of CT Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT502 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of CT Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computed Tomography (CT)** is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that employs a narrow, fan-shaped (or cone-shaped) beam of **ionizing X-radiation** rotating around the patient, combined with computer-based mathematical reconstruction algorithms, to generate high-resolution **cross-sectional (tomographic) images** representing the linear X-ray attenuation coefficient distribution of body tissues in a selected axial slice.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the **principle of CT imaging** is formally grounded on three foundational pillars:

1. **Physical principle** — Differential attenuation of X-rays by tissues of varying density and atomic number, governed by the **Beer–Lambert Law**.
2. **Geometric principle** — Acquisition of multiple **projections (line integrals)** at different angular positions around the patient to overcome the depth-superposition problem inherent in planar radiography.
3. **Mathematical principle** — Reconstruction of the internal attenuation map $\mu(x,y)$ from its projections via the **Radon Transform** and its inverse (Filtered Back Projection or Iterative Reconstruction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus-Highlighted):**
> - **Tomography** → From Greek *tomos* (slice) + *graphein* (to write). Literally: "writing slices."
> - **Voxel** → 3-D volume element (analog of pixel in 3-D).
> - **Pixel** → 2-D picture element displayed on the monitor.
> - **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** → Normalized linear attenuation coefficient scale named after Sir **Godfrey Hounsfield** (Nobel Prize 1979).
> - **Attenuation** → Loss of photon intensity due to absorption and scattering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a **darkened room** holding a flashlight pointing at a wall. Between you and the wall, you place a stack of **translucent glass sheets**, each tinted a slightly different shade. The flashlight beam must pierce through all the sheets before reaching your eye. You see only **one** integrated brightness — you cannot tell where the darker sheets are or how many there are. This is essentially what a **plain X-ray** does.

Now imagine you walk around the stack and shine the flashlight from **hundreds of different angles** (top, bottom, sides, every degree). At each angle, you carefully measure how much light emerges. With all those readings — a *hundred different "shadow profiles"* — a mathematician can solve the puzzle and tell you **exactly which sheet is at which height and how dark each one is**.

That is **CT scanning**. The flashlight is the **X-ray tube**, the stack of glass sheets is the **patient's body slice**, the wall is the **detector array**, and the mathematician is the **computer running the Radon inversion algorithm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A single X-ray is a *projection* of a 3-D object onto a 2-D plane (loss of depth information). CT reverses this by acquiring *many projections* and mathematically *un-collapsing* them — a process called **tomographic reconstruction**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are used throughout CT physics and **must be memorized** for KTU examinations:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Diagnostic X-ray photon energy | $E$ | **30 – 140** | keV |
| Reference energy (CT calibration) | $E_0$ | **73** | keV (effective) |
| Water linear attenuation coeff. (effective) | $\mu_{\text{water}}$ | $\approx 0.195$ | cm⁻¹ |
| Hounsfield Unit of water (by definition) | $HU_{\text{water}}$ | **0** | unitless |
| Hounsfield Unit of air (by definition) | $HU_{\text{air}}$ | **−1000** | unitless |
| Hounsfield Unit of dense cortical bone | $HU_{\text{bone}}$ | $\approx$ **+1000** | unitless |
| Display bit depth (standard) | — | 12 (4096 levels) | bits |
| Display window range | WW | 1 – 4000 | HU |
| Display window level | WL | −1000 to +1000 | HU |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of CT — Stepwise Logic

The CT imaging chain can be decomposed into **eight sequential stages**. Understanding *why* each stage exists is the key to writing high-scoring KTU answers.

### Step 1 — X-ray Production
A high-voltage electron beam ($\approx 80$–$140$ kVp) strikes a rotating tungsten anode in an X-ray tube. The bremsstrahlung and characteristic spectra produced are filtered to yield a **polychromatic fan/cone beam**.

> **Why polychromatic?** Real tubes cannot produce perfectly monoenergetic beams; the spectrum has a low-energy tail that is removed by a **bow-tie filter** to reduce skin dose and beam-hardening artefacts.

### Step 2 — Collimation
Pre-patient (source-side) collimators restrict the beam to a narrow fan, defining the **slice thickness** (typically 0.5 – 10 mm). Post-patient collimators reduce scattered photon contamination reaching the detector.

### Step 3 — Attenuation Through Tissue
The X-ray beam is differentially absorbed/scattered as it traverses voxels of different $\mu$. This is the **physical data-generation** step.

### Step 4 — Detection
Emerging photons strike a **scintillation detector** (e.g., gadolinium oxysulfide, cadmium tungstate, or modern **photodiode + ceramic** detectors). Visible light is converted to an electrical signal proportional to incident intensity.

### Step 5 — Data Acquisition System (DAS)
The detector signal is **logarithmically amplified**, **digitized** (typically 16-bit ADC), and **binned** into raw projection data: a **sinogram**.

> **Sinogram** — A 2-D plot whose x-axis is detector position and y-axis is projection angle. Each row is one projection profile. A single point in the object traces a **sinusoidal curve** in the sinogram (hence the name).

### Step 6 — Pre-processing / Corrections
Raw sinogram data must be corrected for:
- **Beam-hardening correction**
- **Detector dead-time & afterglow**
- **Off-centre (radius) calibration**
- **Ring artefact suppression**
- **Air-normalization** (reference $I_0$ scan in air)

### Step 7 — Reconstruction (The "Tomography")
The corrected sinogram is mathematically inverted. Two main families exist:
1. **Analytical methods** — Filtered Back Projection (FBP) using the **Central Slice Theorem**.
2. **Iterative methods** — Algebraic Reconstruction Technique (ART), Maximum Likelihood Expectation Maximization (MLEM), and modern **model-based iterative reconstruction (MBIR)**.

### Step 8 — Display & Post-processing
Reconstructed $\mu(x,y)$ values are converted to **Hounsfield Units (HU)**, mapped to grayscale via window/level settings, and may be reformatted into coronal/sagittal/3-D volumes.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation that has appeared, or is highly likely to appear**, in KTU ESE/IA examinations on this topic. Master these — they are the "high-density marks zones."

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Statement | Meaning of Each Symbol | Unit | Boundary / Limiting Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beer–Lambert Law** | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | $I$ = transmitted intensity; $I_0$ = incident intensity; $\mu$ = linear attenuation coeff.; $x$ = path length | $I$ in photons/s; $\mu$ in cm⁻¹; $x$ in cm | As $x \to 0$, $I \to I_0$ |
| 2 | **Generalized (polyenergetic) Beer's Law** | $I = \int_{0}^{E_{\max}} S(E) \, e^{-\int \mu(x,E) \, dx} \, dE$ | $S(E)$ = X-ray spectrum; integration over energy | Same as above | Reduces to mono-energetic form if $S(E) = \delta(E-E_0)$ |
| 3 | **Logarithmic projection (line integral)** | $p = -\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = \displaystyle\int_{L} \mu(x,y) \, ds$ | $p$ = projection value; $L$ = ray path; $ds$ = differential path length | cm⁻¹ · cm = dimensionless (often called "Hounsfield-equivalent length") | If $\mu$ is constant along $L$, $p = \mu x$ |
| 4 | **Radon Transform** | $\mathcal{R}\{\mu\}(\rho,\theta) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(\rho\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\ \rho\sin\theta + s\cos\theta) \, ds$ | $\rho$ = perpendicular distance from origin to ray; $\theta$ = projection angle | dimensionless | $\mathcal{R}$ is the forward mapping from image to sinogram |
| 5 | **Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice)** | $\widehat{p}(\omega,\theta) = \widehat{\mu}(\omega\cos\theta,\ \omega\sin\theta)$ | $\widehat{\,\cdot\,}$ = 1-D Fourier transform; $\omega$ = spatial frequency | — | The 1-D FT of a projection equals a radial slice of the 2-D FT of the object |
| 6 | **Filtered Back Projection (FBP) Reconstruction** | $\mu(x,y) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi} \!\! \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \widehat{p}(\omega,\theta) \, \vert \omega \vert \, e^{j 2\pi \omega (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} d\omega \, d\theta$ | $\vert \omega \vert$ = Ram–Lak (ramp) filter kernel | — | Without $\vert\omega\vert$ filter → blurred image (simple back-projection) |
| 7 | **Hounsfield Unit Definition** | $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | $HU$ = CT number; $\mu_{\text{tissue}}$ = voxel attenuation; $\mu_{\text{water}}$ = reference (effective @ 73 keV) | unitless (Dimensionless) | $HU_{\text{water}}=0$; $HU_{\text{air}}=-1000$; $HU_{\text{bone}}\approx+1000$ |
| 8 | **CT Number for a Mixture** | $HU_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i} f_i \, HU_i$ | $f_i$ = volume fraction of constituent $i$ | unitless | Sum of volume fractions $= 1$ |
| 9 | **Helical Pitch Factor** | $\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{\text{Table travel per rotation}}{\text{Total collimation width}}$ | — | dimensionless | Pitch $< 1$ = overlap (high dose, low artefact); Pitch $> 1$ = gap (low dose) |
| 10 | **Slice Sensitivity Profile (Gaussian approx.)** | $SSP(z) \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{4\ln 2 \cdot z^2}{FWHM^2}\right)$ | $FWHM$ = full-width half-maximum slice thickness; $z$ = axial distance | mm | Defines effective slice thickness |
| 11 | **High-Contrast Spatial Resolution** | $d_{\min} \approx 1.1 \times \dfrac{SAD}{SFD} \times a$ | $SAD$ = source-to-axis distance; $SFD$ = source-to-focus distance; $a$ = focal spot size | mm (lp/cm inverse) | Smaller $a$ → better resolution |
| 12 | **Nyquist Sampling Criterion** | $f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2 \, f_{\max}$ | $f_{\text{max}}$ = highest spatial frequency in object | samples/mm | Undersampling → aliasing artefacts |
| 13 | **SNR in CT** | $SNR \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{photons}}} \cdot \dfrac{\mu}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}}$ | $N$ = number of detected photons; $\sigma$ = noise std. dev. | dimensionless | Doubling mAs → $\sqrt{2}$ improvement in SNR |
| 14 | **Contrast-to-Noise Ratio** | $CNR = \dfrac{\vert HU_A - HU_B \vert}{\sigma_{\text{background}}}$ | $A,B$ = two regions of interest | dimensionless | Threshold for detectability $\approx 3$–$5$ |
| 15 | **Partial Volume Effect (Mean HU)** | $HU_{\text{voxel}} = \dfrac{1}{V}\displaystyle\int_{V} HU(\vec{r}) \, dV$ | $V$ = voxel volume | unitless | Voxel straddles two tissues → averaged HU |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently write the Hounsfield formula as $HU = 1000 \times \mu_{\text{tissue}} / \mu_{\text{water}}$ — **forgetting to subtract $\mu_{\text{water}}$**. This single omission will cost you **2 marks minimum** in Part A. Always show the full numerator: $(\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}})$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

CT imaging principles extend well beyond medicine and are foundational to:

- **Industrial Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — CT scanning of turbine blades, castings, 3-D printed metal parts.
- **Baggage / Security Screening** — Explosive detection at airports.
- **Geophysics** — Seismic tomography for oil & gas exploration (same Radon math).
- **Materials Science** — Micro-CT for porosity analysis in battery electrodes.
- **Cultural Heritage** — Scanning mummies, fossils, and archaeological artifacts without damage.
- **Reverse Engineering** — Generating CAD models from physical prototypes.

> The mathematical core (Radon transform + FBP) is a **cross-disciplinary workhorse** — the same 1917 equation Johann Radon wrote in pure mathematics is now used in every airport scanner you walk through.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Beer's Law to the Hounsfield Unit

**Goal:** Show rigorously how the displayed pixel value (Hounsfield Unit) is obtained from raw X-ray transmission measurements.

**Given:**
- A polychromatic X-ray source with effective energy $E_0 \approx 73$ keV.
- A water-filled calibration phantom is used to measure $\mu_{\text{water}}$.
- The CT scanner measures the transmitted intensity $I$ for each ray.
- An empty (air) reference scan yields $I_0$.

**To Derive:** The algebraic expression for the CT number ($HU$) of a tissue voxel.

### Step 1 — Apply Beer's Law to a Monoenergetic Beam

For a single photon energy $E$, the transmitted intensity after passing through tissue of attenuation coefficient $\mu(E)$ and thickness $x$ is given by the exponential attenuation law:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}
$$

This is the fundamental law of X-ray interaction with matter. The intensity decreases **exponentially**, not linearly, with thickness.

### Step 2 — Solve for the Linear Attenuation Coefficient

To extract the attenuation coefficient (which carries the tissue information), take the natural logarithm of both sides and isolate $\mu$:

$$
\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x
$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$ and dividing by $x$:

$$
\mu = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The $\ln$ step is *why* CT detectors use a **logarithmic amplifier** — it converts the exponential decay directly into a linear signal proportional to $\mu$.

### Step 3 — Generalize to a Voxel with Spatially Varying $\mu$

In CT, a single ray passes through many voxels with different $\mu_i$. Beer's law generalizes by replacing $\mu x$ with a **line integral** of $\mu$ along the ray path $L$:

$$
I = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\int_L \mu(s) \, ds\right)
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
p = -\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \int_L \mu(s) \, ds
$$

This quantity $p$ is the **projection** of $\mu$ along the ray — a single number representing the cumulative attenuation of that ray. The full set of projections at all detector positions and angles forms the **sinogram**.

### Step 4 — Reconstruction Yields Voxel-Wise $\mu_{\text{tissue}}$

After the FBP (or iterative) reconstruction step, the computer has computed an estimate of $\mu_{\text{tissue}}$ for *every voxel* in the slice:

$$
\mu_{\text{tissue}}(x, y, z) \quad \text{(in cm}^{-1}\text{)}
$$

### Step 5 — Calibrate with Water and Air References

Because the absolute value of $\mu$ depends on X-ray energy, patient size, and scanner geometry, Hounsfield (1972) introduced a **normalized scale** anchored to two reference materials:
- **Water:** $\mu_{\text{water}}$ measured on the same scanner at the same kVp.
- **Air:** For practical purposes $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 0$, but to enforce $HU_{\text{air}} = -1000$ the formula is constructed as below.

### Step 6 — Define the Hounsfield Unit Algebraically

The CT number is defined as a normalized difference, scaled so that water $= 0$ and air $= -1000$:

$$
HU_{\text{tissue}} \;\equiv\; 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

### Step 7 — Verify the Boundary Conditions

**Case A — Tissue is water:** $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \mu_{\text{water}}$

$$
HU_{\text{water}} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{water}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}} = 1000 \times 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Case B — Tissue is air (assuming $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 0$):**

$$
HU_{\text{air}} = 1000 \times \frac{0 - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}} = 1000 \times (-1) = -1000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Case C — Tissue is dense cortical bone (typical $\mu \approx 2 \mu_{\text{water}}$):**

$$
HU_{\text{bone}} = 1000 \times \frac{2\mu_{\text{water}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}} = 1000 \times 1 = +1000 \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Result Proven:** The Hounsfield scale is a linear, dimensionless, energy-independent *normalized* attenuation coefficient. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Filtered Back Projection (FBP) from the Central Slice Theorem

**Goal:** Show how a 2-D image $\mu(x,y)$ can be reconstructed from 1-D projections $p(\rho,\theta)$ measured at all angles.

### Step 1 — Acquire Projections at All Angles

For each angle $\theta \in [0,\pi)$, the scanner measures the projection $p(\rho,\theta)$, which is the Radon transform of $\mu$:

$$
p(\rho,\theta) = \mathcal{R}\{\mu\}(\rho,\theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(\rho\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\ \rho\sin\theta + s\cos\theta) \, ds
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Central Slice Theorem

The 1-D Fourier transform of the projection $p$ with respect to $\rho$ equals a radial line of the 2-D Fourier transform of $\mu$:

$$
\widehat{p}(\omega,\theta) = \widehat{\mu}(\omega\cos\theta,\ \omega\sin\theta)
$$

This theorem is the *bridge* between 1-D measurements and 2-D reconstruction.

### Step 3 — Invert the 2-D Fourier Transform

The image is recovered by the inverse 2-D Fourier transform. Converting to polar coordinates $(k_x, k_y) = (\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$ with $dk_x dk_y = \vert \omega \vert \, d\omega \, d\theta$:

$$
\mu(x,y) = \iint \widehat{\mu}(k_x, k_y) \, e^{j 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dk_x \, dk_y
$$

$$
\mu(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \!\! \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \widehat{p}(\omega,\theta) \, \vert \omega \vert \, e^{j 2\pi \omega (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega \, d\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Filter Kernel

The term $\vert \omega \vert$ is the famous **ramp filter** (also called **Ram-Lak filter**), discovered by Ramachandran & Lakshminarayanan (1971). It amplifies high spatial frequencies to compensate for the natural low-pass blurring of simple back-projection.

### Step 5 — Practical Implementation: Filter then Back-Project

In a real CT computer, the algorithm executes in two stages:

**Stage A — Filtering (1-D convolution in projection space):**

$$
p_{\text{filtered}}(\rho,\theta) = p(\rho,\theta) \ast h(\rho)
$$

where $h(\rho) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\vert \omega \vert\}$ is the Ram-Lak filter kernel in real space.

**Stage B — Back-Projection (smearing back into image space):**

$$
\mu(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} p_{\text{filtered}}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta,\ \theta) \, d\theta
$$

> **Result Proven:** The filtered back-projection algorithm yields a mathematically exact reconstruction of the attenuation map in the continuous-noise-free limit. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Partial Volume Averaging for Mixed Voxel

**Goal:** Show that the CT number of a voxel straddling two tissues is the volume-weighted mean.

**Given:** A voxel of volume $V$ containing tissue A (volume $V_A$, $HU_A$) and tissue B (volume $V_B$, $HU_B$), with $V_A + V_B = V$.

**Define volume fractions:** $f_A = V_A / V$ and $f_B = V_B / V$, with $f_A + f_B = 1$.

The voxel-average attenuation is:

$$
\mu_{\text{voxel}} = \frac{1}{V}\int_V \mu(\vec{r}) \, dV = f_A \mu_A + f_B \mu_B
$$

Substituting into the HU formula:

$$
HU_{\text{voxel}} = 1000 \times \frac{(f_A \mu_A + f_B \mu_B) - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

$$
HU_{\text{voxel}} = f_A \left[1000 \times \frac{\mu_A - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}\right] + f_B \left[1000 \times \frac{\mu_B - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,HU_{\text{voxel}} = f_A \, HU_A + f_B \, HU_B\,}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A voxel is 40% white matter ($HU = 30$) and 60% CSF ($HU = 15$):

$$
HU_{\text{voxel}} = (0.40)(30) + (0.60)(15) = 12 + 9 = 21 \text{ HU}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the *fundamental reason* small lesions (< 3× the slice thickness) appear *less conspicuous* on CT than their true HU — they are averaged with surrounding tissue. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CT Reconstruction Methods

| Method | Mathematical Basis | Computational Cost | Noise Robustness | Resolution | Clinical Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Back-Projection (SBP)** | Integral of unfiltered projections | Very Low | Poor (blurred) | Low | Historical only |
| **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** | Inverse Radon + Ramp filter (Central Slice Theorem) | Low | Moderate | High | Gold standard (legacy) |
| **ART (Algebraic)** | Iterative least-squares system solver | High | Good | High | Research |
| **MLEM (Statistical)** | Maximum-likelihood Poisson model | Very High | Excellent | High | PET/SPECT, modern CT |
| **MBIR (Model-Based Iterative)** | Combines system optics + anatomy model | Highest | Best (dose-reduction 30–80%) | High | Modern clinical |
| **Deep-Learning Recon.** | Convolutional neural nets (e.g., AiCE, TrueFidelity) | Low (inference) | Excellent | High | Emerging clinical |

> **Key Takeaway:** Modern CT has migrated from FBP → iterative → AI-based reconstruction, allowing **30 – 80% dose reduction** at equivalent image quality — a direct consequence of the same Radon mathematics first published in 1917.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete CT Imaging Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[High Voltage Generator 80 to 140 kVp] --> B[X-Ray Tube with Rotating Anode]
    B --> C[Beam Shaping Filter Bow Tie]
    C --> D[Pre Patient Collimator defines slice thickness]
    D --> E[Patient Slice Voxel Attenuation mu x y]
    E --> F[Post Patient Collimator reduces scatter]
    F --> G[Detector Array Scintillator plus Photodiode]
    G --> H[Data Acquisition System Logarithmic Amplifier]
    H --> I[Analog to Digital Converter 16 bit]
    I --> J[Sinogram Storage 2D Projection Data]
    J --> K[Pre Processing Corrections Beam Hardening Ring Artefact]
    K --> L[Reconstruction Engine FBP or Iterative]
    L --> M[Hounsfield Unit Conversion HU equals 1000 mu minus mu water over mu water]
    M --> N[Image Matrix 512 by 512 pixels]
    N --> O[Display Console Window Level Settings]
    O --> P[Clinician Diagnosis]
```

## 4.2 Generations of CT Scanner Architecture (Comparative Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[1st Generation<br>Translate Rotate<br>Pencil Beam<br>1 Detector<br>5 min per slice]
    G2[2nd Generation<br>Translate Rotate<br>Fan Beam<br>Multiple Detectors<br>30 s per slice]
    G3[3rd Generation<br>Rotate Rotate Only<br>Wide Fan Beam<br>Curved Detector Array<br>1 s per slice]
    G4[4th Generation<br>Rotate Stationary Ring<br>360 Degree Detector Ring<br>Sub second per slice]
    G5[5th Generation<br>Electron Beam CT<br>No Mechanical Motion<br>50 ms per slice Cardiac]
    G6[6th Generation Helical Slip Ring<br>Continuous Table Translation<br>Sub second per slice]
    G7[7th Generation Multi Detector MDCT<br>64 to 320 rows<br>Volumetric Acquisition]
    G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 --> G5
    G4 --> G6 --> G7
```

## 4.3 Filtered Back Projection — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Sinogram<br>p rho theta] --> ACQ[Acquisition<br>at N angles]
    ACQ --> FT1[1D Fourier Transform<br>of each projection]
    FT1 --> CST[Central Slice Theorem<br>Radial Lines of 2D FT]
    CST --> FIL[Apply Ramp Filter<br>multiply by omega magnitude]
    FIL --> IFT[Inverse 1D FT<br>per projection]
    IFT --> BP[Back Project<br>smear over image plane]
    BP --> IMG[Reconstructed Image<br>mu of x y]
```

## 4.4 Data Flow — From Detector Signal to Display Pixel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Photon strikes scintillator] --> S[Scintillation visible light photons]
    S --> PD[Photodiode converts to current]
    PD --> AMP[Logarithmic Amplifier ln I over I0]
    AMP --> ADC[16 bit ADC digitization]
    ADC --> COR[Pre processing corrections]
    COR --> REC[Reconstruction FBP]
    REC --> HU[HU conversion]
    HU --> WIN[Window Level mapping]
    WIN --> MON[Grayscale pixel on monitor]
```

## 4.5 CT Number Spectrum Bar (Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air minus 1000 HU] --> L[Lung minus 700 HU]
    L --> F[Fat minus 100 HU]
    F --> W[Water 0 HU]
    W --> B[Blood plus 40 HU]
    B --> M[Muscle plus 50 HU]
    M --> G[Grey Matter plus 40 HU]
    G --> WM[White Matter plus 30 HU]
    WM --> K[Kidney plus 30 HU]
    K --> H[Liver plus 60 HU]
    H --> CT[Acute Haemorrhage plus 80 HU]
    CT --> I[Iodine Contrast plus 100 to 500 HU]
    I --> CB[Cortical Bone plus 1000 HU]
    CB --> M2[Metal Implant plus 3000 HU]
```
