<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Hounsfield Unit (CT Number). Calculate the CT number of a tissue whose linear attenuation coefficient is $0.32$ cm⁻¹, given that the linear attenuation coefficient of water at the same effective energy is $0.20$ cm⁻¹. Comment on the type of tissue this value represents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Hounsfield Unit (HU), also called the **CT number**, is a normalized measure of the linear X-ray attenuation coefficient of a tissue, defined as:
>
> $$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
>
> It is a dimensionless quantity, with water defined to have $HU = 0$ and air $HU = -1000$.

**Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$
HU = 1000 \times \frac{0.32 - 0.20}{0.20} = 1000 \times \frac{0.12}{0.20} = 1000 \times 0.6 = +600
$$

**Comment (0.5 marks):** A CT number of $+600$ HU falls in the range of **dense bone or calcified tissue** (cortical bone is typically $+1000$ HU; heavily calcified lesions fall in the $+400$ to $+800$ range).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain Beer's Law as applied to X-ray attenuation. What is the significance of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ in CT imaging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** Beer's Law (also called the Beer–Lambert Law) states that the intensity $I$ of a monoenergetic X-ray beam after passing through a uniform absorber of thickness $x$ and linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ is:
>
> $$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$
>
> where $I_0$ is the incident intensity.

**Explanation (1 mark):** The exponential form means that intensity decreases *non-linearly* with thickness; each additional centimetre of tissue reduces intensity by the same *fraction* (not the same amount). In CT, because the path crosses many tissues, the law is generalized to a line integral:

$$
I = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\int_L \mu(s) \, ds\right)
$$

**Significance of $\mu$ in CT (1 mark):** The linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ (in cm⁻¹) is the *fundamental physical quantity* that CT reconstructs for every voxel. It encodes the local tissue's density and atomic composition. The CT number $HU$ is a normalized form of $\mu$, and all diagnostic information in a CT image is derived from the spatial distribution of $\mu$ values.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** As per the 2024 ESE scheme, each Module carries either **two 14-mark questions with choice** or a **single 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. We provide the sub-part format below, which is the most common pattern.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **3rd generation CT scanner**. Describe the role of the **data acquisition system (DAS)** and the **slip ring** technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **Hounsfield Unit** from first principles using Beer's Law. Show that water maps to 0 HU and air maps to −1000 HU. A voxel contains 70 % grey matter ($HU = 40$) and 30 % CSF ($HU = 15$) — find the mean CT number of the voxel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 3rd Generation CT Scanner (7 marks)**

A **3rd generation (rotate-rotate) CT scanner** is the most widely deployed architecture. Its working principle:

1. **Geometry (1.5 marks):** Both the **X-ray tube** and a **curved detector array** (typically 500–1000 solid-state detectors spanning an arc of $\approx 40$–$50°$) are mounted rigidly on a common rotating **gantry**. The patient lies on a couch that translates through the gantry bore. There is **no translation motion** — only continuous rotation.

2. **Beam (1 mark):** A **wide fan-beam** (typically 30–55° fan angle) covers the entire patient cross-section in a single rotation, eliminating the need for translational motion present in 1st/2nd generation scanners.

3. **Rotation (1 mark):** The tube–detector assembly rotates continuously around the patient at 0.3 – 0.5 s per rotation (sub-second), acquiring 1000+ projections per second.

4. **Role of DAS (1.5 marks):** The **Data Acquisition System** performs:
   - **Logarithmic amplification** of detector signals (converts exponential decay to linear attenuation values).
   - **Integration** over the dwell time.
   - **Analog-to-digital conversion** (16-bit ADC typical).
   - **Multiplexing and transmission** of projection data via slip rings to the reconstruction computer.
   - The DAS enables real-time digitization required for the $\approx 1000$ projections/s data rate.

5. **Slip Ring Technology (1 mark):** **Slip rings** are electromechanical rotary joints that transmit **electrical power and high-speed data** between the stationary frame and the continuously rotating gantry, eliminating the previously used power/data cables that limited rotation to back-and-forth motion. Slip rings enable **continuous (helical/spiral) scanning** and sub-second rotation.

6. **Output (1 mark):** Projection data forms a **sinogram**, which is then reconstructed (FBP / iterative) into a $512 \times 512$ axial image matrix, typically displayed in **Hounsfield Units (HU)**.

**Part (b) — HU Derivation & Partial Volume (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply Beer's Law to a monochromatic X-ray beam through a uniform attenuator:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Generalize to a ray crossing multiple voxels with different $\mu_i$:

$$
I = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\int_L \mu(s) \, ds\right)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define the projection (sinogram value):

$$
p = -\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \int_L \mu(s) \, ds
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** After reconstruction, each voxel has a value $\mu_{\text{tissue}}$. The Hounsfield Unit is defined as:

$$
HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Boundary conditions:
- Water: $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \mu_{\text{water}} \Rightarrow HU = 1000 \times 0 = 0$ ✓
- Air: $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 0 \Rightarrow HU = 1000 \times \frac{0 - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}} = -1000$ ✓

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Partial volume calculation:

For a mixed voxel with volume fractions $f_{\text{GM}} = 0.70$ and $f_{\text{CSF}} = 0.30$:

$$
HU_{\text{voxel}} = f_{\text{GM}} \, HU_{\text{GM}} + f_{\text{CSF}} \, HU_{\text{CSF}}
$$

$$
HU_{\text{voxel}} = (0.70)(40) + (0.30)(15) = 28 + 4.5 = \mathbf{32.5 \ HU}
$$

> **Verification:** $32.5$ lies between $15$ and $40$, as required for a partial-volume average. ✓

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the principle of **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)** reconstruction in CT. State the **Central Slice Theorem** and derive the reconstruction equation. Why is the **ramp filter** necessary? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **pitch** in helical CT. A scanner with a detector collimation of $32 \times 0.6$ mm rotates in $0.4$ s with a table feed of $12.8$ mm/rotation. Calculate: (i) the pitch factor, (ii) the effective slice thickness, and (iii) comment on whether this is an "overlap," "standard," or "gap" acquisition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — FBP Reconstruction (7 marks)**

**1. The Reconstruction Problem (1 mark):** CT must invert the **Radon transform** — given a complete set of projections $p(\rho,\theta)$ at all angles, recover $\mu(x,y)$. Direct analytical inversion is non-trivial; the elegant route uses Fourier space.

**2. Central Slice Theorem (CST) — Statement (1.5 marks):**

> *"The one-dimensional Fourier transform of a parallel-beam projection $p(\rho,\theta)$ at angle $\theta$ is equal to a radial line through the origin of the two-dimensional Fourier transform of the original image $\mu(x,y)$, at the same angle $\theta$."*

Mathematically:

$$
\widehat{p}(\omega,\theta) = \widehat{\mu}(\omega\cos\theta,\ \omega\sin\theta)
$$

**3. Reconstruction Equation — Derivation (2 marks):** Starting from the inverse 2-D FT in Cartesian form:

$$
\mu(x,y) = \iint \widehat{\mu}(k_x,k_y) \, e^{j 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dk_x \, dk_y
$$

Substitute polar coordinates $k_x = \omega\cos\theta$, $k_y = \omega\sin\theta$, with Jacobian $dk_x dk_y = \vert\omega\vert \, d\omega \, d\theta$:

$$
\mu(x,y) = \int_0^\pi \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \widehat{p}(\omega,\theta) \, \vert\omega\vert \, e^{j 2\pi \omega(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, d\omega \, d\theta
$$

**4. Why the Ramp Filter? (1.5 marks):** Simple (unfiltered) back-projection smears each projection back across the image plane. Mathematically, this is equivalent to multiplying $\widehat{\mu}$ by $1/\vert\omega\vert$ in Fourier space, which **attenuates high spatial frequencies** and produces a blurred image with a characteristic $1/r$ star-pattern artefact around high-contrast objects. Multiplying by $\vert\omega\vert$ (the **ramp filter**) exactly compensates this $1/r$ blurring, restoring sharp edges and uniform point-spread response.

**5. Practical Implementation (1 mark):** In the CT computer, FBP is implemented as:
- **Filter** each projection with the 1-D Ram-Lak kernel $h(\rho) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\vert\omega\vert\}$.
- **Back-project** the filtered projection across the image at angle $\theta$.
- **Sum** contributions from all $\theta \in [0, \pi)$.

---

**Part (b) — Helical CT Pitch Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Detector collimation: $32 \times 0.6$ mm $= 19.2$ mm total
- Rotation time: $0.4$ s
- Table feed: $12.8$ mm/rotation

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the pitch factor:

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Table travel per rotation}}{\text{Total collimation width}} = \frac{12.8 \text{ mm}}{32 \times 0.6 \text{ mm}} = \frac{12.8}{19.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Pitch} = 0.667}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Effective slice thickness (assuming thin-beam Gaussian model, with the helical interpolation factor $\approx 1/\sqrt{1 + \text{pitch}^2}$ correction for a single-slice equivalent; for multi-slice CT, SSP broadening is more complex, but the nominal slice thickness is taken as the collimated detector row width, modified by pitch):

For a pitch $< 1$, the effective slice sensitivity profile broadens. Approximate effective slice thickness:

$$
T_{\text{eff}} \approx T_{\text{coll}} \times \sqrt{\frac{1 + \text{pitch}^2}{1}} = 0.6 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.667^2}
$$

$$
T_{\text{eff}} \approx 0.6 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.444} = 0.6 \times \sqrt{1.444} = 0.6 \times 1.202 = 0.721 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Note:** The exact effective SSP in multi-slice helical CT also depends on reconstruction algorithm (e.g., 180° or 360° linear interpolation). The 0.72 mm figure is the *first-order* estimate.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Classification comment:
- Pitch $< 1$ → **Overlap** (over-sampling in z-axis, high dose, smoother 3-D reconstruction).
- Pitch $= 1$ → **Standard** (contiguous sampling).
- Pitch $> 1$ → **Gap** (under-sampling, lower dose, but risk of missing small lesions).

Since **Pitch = 0.667 < 1**, this is an **overlap (over-sampled) acquisition**, characterized by:
- **Higher patient dose** (dose $\propto 1/\text{pitch}$).
- **Better z-axis resolution** and reduced partial-volume artefacts.
- **Smoother multi-planar reformats (MPR)** and 3-D volume renderings.
- Commonly used in **high-resolution chest CT** and **CT angiography** where small z-axis detail is critical.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert detector collimation to total width** — students often write "pitch = 12.8/0.6" — losing 1 mark. Always multiply: $N_{\text{rows}} \times T_{\text{row}}$.
> 2. **Mixing up "pitch" definition** — some textbooks use $\text{Pitch} = \text{feed} / (N \times T)$ (most common); others use $\text{Pitch} = \text{feed} / T$ (single-slice convention). Always state the convention used.
> 3. **Misclassifying the pitch regime** — Pitch = 0.667 is **less than 1**, hence **overlap**, not standard. Students frequently mis-write this.
> 4. **In FBP derivation** — failing to state the Central Slice Theorem explicitly is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **In HU problems** — writing the formula without the $\mu_{\text{water}}$ subtraction in the numerator is a **2-mark killer**.
> 6. **In Beer's Law questions** — using the formula for polychromatic beams without acknowledging the effective-energy approximation loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principle of CT Imaging**

### Must-Know Definitions
- ☐ **CT** — Computed Tomography; cross-sectional X-ray imaging using computer reconstruction.
- ☐ **Tomography** — From Greek *tomos* (slice) + *graphein* (write).
- ☐ **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** — Normalized linear attenuation scale, water = 0, air = −1000.
- ☐ **Pixel** vs **Voxel** — 2-D display element vs 3-D volume element.
- ☐ **Projection** — Line integral of $\mu$ along an X-ray path.
- ☐ **Sinogram** — 2-D plot of detector position vs projection angle.
- ☐ **Radon Transform** — Mathematical forward map from image to sinogram.
- ☐ **FBP** — Filtered Back Projection; analytical inverse Radon method.
- ☐ **Pitch** — Table travel per rotation divided by total collimation width.
- ☐ **Slip Ring** — Rotary electromechanical contact for continuous gantry rotation.

### Must-Memorize Numerical Values
- ☐ $HU_{\text{water}} = 0$, $HU_{\text{air}} = -1000$, $HU_{\text{bone}} \approx +1000$.
- ☐ Effective X-ray energy: $\approx 73$ keV.
- ☐ $\mu_{\text{water}} \approx 0.195$ cm⁻¹ (effective).
- ☐ Standard image matrix: $512 \times 512$ pixels.
- ☐ Display bit depth: **12 bits** (4096 grey levels).
- ☐ Typical clinical slice thickness: 0.5 – 10 mm.
- ☐ Typical kVp: 80 – 140.

### Must-Know Theorems / Principles
- ☐ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
- ☐ **Central Slice Theorem:** 1-D FT of projection = radial line of 2-D FT of image.
- ☐ **FBP equation** (with Ram-Lak $\vert\omega\vert$ filter).
- ☐ **HU formula** with correct numerator $\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}$.
- ☐ **Partial volume:** voxel HU = volume-weighted mean of constituents.

### Must-Know Practical Points
- ☐ FBP is **fast** but noise-sensitive; iterative recon is **slower** but allows dose reduction.
- ☐ Pitch $< 1$ → overlap (high dose, high z-resolution).
- ☐ Pitch $= 1$ → standard.
- ☐ Pitch $> 1$ → gap (low dose, risk of missing small lesions).
- ☐ CNR $> 3$–$5$ needed for lesion detectability.
- ☐ Window width (WW) controls contrast; window level (WL) controls brightness.
- ☐ Modern CT uses **helical (spiral) acquisition with slip rings** as the norm.

### Quick-Fire Practice Values
- ☐ Tissue with $\mu = 0.234$ cm⁻¹ in water ($\mu = 0.195$ cm⁻¹) → $HU = +200$ (typical **kidney** or **grey matter**).
- ☐ Tissue with $\mu = 0.0975$ cm⁻¹ in water → $HU = -500$ (typical **lung**).
- ☐ Liver: $HU \approx +50$ to $+70$.
- ☐ Acute blood: $HU \approx +60$ to $+80$.
- ☐ Fat: $HU \approx -100$.
- ☐ Iodinated contrast (peak): $HU \approx +200$ to $+500$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generations of Scanners, System Instrumentation, X-Ray Tubes for CT Applications, Pencil Beam and Cone Beam Projections, Image Reconstruction Algorithms for Pencil Beam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT502 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generations of Scanners, System Instrumentation, X-Ray Tubes for CT Applications, Pencil Beam and Cone Beam Projections, Image Reconstruction Algorithms for Pencil Beam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Computed Tomography (CT) — Foundational Definition

**Computed Tomography (CT)** is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that uses computer-processed combinations of multiple X-ray projections taken from different angles to produce cross-sectional (tomographic) images of specific areas of a scanned object, allowing the user to see inside the object without cutting it open.

In CT imaging, a narrow beam of X-rays is directed through the patient's body from multiple angular positions. The attenuated X-ray intensities are measured by detectors, and mathematical reconstruction algorithms convert this projection data into a 2D image representing the spatial distribution of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x, y)$ within a thin cross-sectional slice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The basic unit of measurement in CT is the **Hounsfield Unit (HU)**, defined on a linear scale where air = **−1000 HU**, water = **0 HU**, and dense bone = **+1000 HU** or more. This calibrated scale is what differentiates CT from plain radiography.

$$\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

### 1.2 The Generations of CT Scanners — An Evolutionary Concept

The "generations" of CT scanners refer to the chronological evolution of hardware design, specifically how the **X-ray source**, the **detector(s)**, and the **patient** move relative to one another to acquire the angular projection data required for image reconstruction.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse Analogy:**

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a dark field, trying to map out the shape of every tree around you.

- **Generation 1 (Translate–Rotate):** You hold a thin flashlight (pencil beam), sweep it sideways across the trees (translate), then turn a small step (rotate), and repeat. It works, but it is painfully slow.
- **Generation 3 (Rotate–Rotate):** You hold a wide flashlight (fan beam) and simply rotate around yourself. The beam covers everything in one sweep. This is far faster.
- **Generation 6 (Helical/Spiral):** While you keep rotating, the trees themselves are slowly moving past you on a conveyor belt — the data is acquired in a continuous spiral instead of discrete slices.
- **Generation 5 (Electron Beam):** No physical motion at all — a beam of electrons is electronically steered around the patient, like a lighthouse whose mirror spins at the speed of light.

This evolution is driven by one unifying engineering goal: **acquire more projections per second, with higher spatial resolution, lower radiation dose, and minimal motion artifacts.**

### 1.3 X-Ray Tubes for CT — The Workhorse Under Stress

A **CT X-ray tube** is a specialized high-heat-capacity, rotating-anode vacuum tube designed to deliver intense, stable, and rapidly pulsed X-ray beams for thousands of consecutive projections during a single patient scan. Unlike a standard radiographic tube (which may be energized for <1 second), a CT tube must withstand **continuous high-power operation for tens of seconds** to several minutes, dissipating several **Mega Heat Units (MHU)** of thermal energy.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Marathon Runner vs. The Sprinter:**

A regular X-ray tube (used in radiography) is like a sprinter — powerful but only for a few seconds. A CT tube is a marathon runner — it must sustain enormous heat loads for an extended period. This is why CT tubes use large rotating anodes, oil-cooled housings, and sometimes external heat exchangers.

### 1.4 Pencil Beam vs. Cone Beam Projection — Two Geometric Philosophies

- **Pencil Beam Projection:** A narrowly collimated, near-parallel beam of X-rays traverses the object along a single line at a time. Each measurement yields one ray-sum of the attenuation line integral.
- **Cone Beam Projection:** A divergent X-ray beam shaped like a cone (or pyramid) illuminates the entire object volume at once, captured on a 2D detector array in a single exposure.

**Analogy — The Rain Analogy:** A pencil beam is like feeling rain with a single fingertip held out at one position — you only sense one raindrop path at a time. A cone beam is like holding out a flat hand under the rain — every raindrop angle across the entire hand is captured simultaneously. The hand (2D detector) gives a complete "rain map" (2D projection image) in one instant.

### 1.5 Image Reconstruction for Pencil Beam — The Mathematical Reverse Engineering

Image reconstruction is the mathematical process of converting the measured projection data (sinogram) into a cross-sectional image of attenuation coefficients. For pencil beam geometry, the foundational technique is the **Radon Transform** and its inverse — **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)**.

**Analogy — The Shadow Puppet Analogy:** Imagine you shine a light on an unknown object and capture its shadow from many angles. Each shadow is a "projection." If you have shadows from ALL directions and know the math of how shadows form, you can mathematically reverse the process and reconstruct the original 3D shape. That reverse-engineering process is CT reconstruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Outcome Mapping (CO2):** After studying this topic, students will be able to *explain the constructional features and operating principles of various generations of CT scanners, describe the role of X-ray tubes and detector systems, and analyze reconstruction algorithms for parallel beam (pencil beam) projection data.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generations of CT Scanners — Detailed Engineering Analysis

The complete evolutionary table below is the **single most important reference** for KTU exams on this topic.

| Gen | Motion Type | Beam Geometry | Detector Type | No. of Projections per Slice | Time/Slice | Key Innovation |
|-----|------------|---------------|---------------|------------------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1st | Translate–Rotate | Pencil (parallel) | Single detector (NaI scintillation) | 160 × 180 = 28,800 rays | **~4–5 min** | First clinical CT (Hounsfield, 1971, EMI-Scanner) |
| 2nd | Translate–Rotate | Narrow fan (~10°) | 3–30 detectors in a row | 160 × 18 = 2,880 rays | **~20–90 s** | Introduced fan beam to reduce translates |
| 3rd | Rotate–Rotate | Wide fan (~30–55°) | 600–1000+ curved detector array | 1000+ per view | **~1–5 s** | Pure rotation, no translation; ring artifacts possible |
| 4th | Rotate–Stationary (tube rotates, detectors fixed) | Wide fan | 600–4800 stationary ring detectors | Up to 4800 per view | **~1–5 s** | Stationary detector ring; star artifacts possible |
| 5th | Electron Beam (no mechanical motion) | Fan / dual-fan | Fixed arc detectors | Two target rings | **~50–100 ms** (cardiac imaging) | Electron-beam-steered, no moving parts |
| 6th | Helical/Spiral (slip-ring + continuous table motion) | Fan (single or dual) | Curved or flat array | Continuous | Sub-second volumetric | Slip-ring technology enables continuous gantry rotation |
| 7th | Multi-Detector / Cone Beam CT | Cone beam | 2D matrix array (e.g., 320 × 0.5 mm = 16 cm) | Volumetric per rotation | Sub-second isotropic volumes | Multi-row (4, 16, 64, 128, 256, 320-slice) detectors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> - **3rd generation vs. 4th generation:** In 3rd-gen, both tube AND detectors rotate together. In 4th-gen, only the tube rotates; detectors are in a fixed 360° ring around the patient.
> - **5th generation EBCT:** Used specifically for **cardiac imaging** because the 50 ms scan time freezes heart motion.
> - **6th generation:** The defining feature is the **slip ring** — a continuous electrical contact that allows the gantry to rotate indefinitely without cables winding up.

#### 2.1.1 First Generation — Translate–Rotate (Parallel/Pencil Beam)

**Mechanism:**
1. The X-ray tube emits a **pencil beam** (highly collimated to ~1–2 mm wide).
2. The tube–detector assembly translates linearly across the patient, acquiring **N linear measurements** (typically 160 parallel rays per translation).
3. After the translation is complete, the assembly rotates by **1°–2°** (typically 180 angles over 180°).
4. Steps 1–3 are repeated until 180° of angular coverage is obtained.

**Total rays per slice:** $N_{\text{translate}} \times N_{\text{rotate}}$

For a typical 160 × 180 setup: $160 \times 180 = 28{,}800$ ray-sums per slice.

**Why the "180° + fan angle" sufficient?** Because for parallel-beam geometry, opposing projections 180° apart yield **identical data** (reciprocity). So we only need half the angular range.

**Limitation:** Scan time of 4–5 minutes per slice is impractical for clinical use; susceptible to patient motion artifacts.

#### 2.1.2 Second Generation — Translate–Rotate (Narrow Fan Beam)

Same translate–rotate motion as 1st gen, but the pencil beam is widened into a **narrow fan (~5°–10°)** and detected by a small linear array of detectors (3–30 elements). This reduces the number of translates per slice by a factor of 5–10, dramatically reducing scan time to **~20–90 seconds**.

#### 2.1.3 Third Generation — Rotate–Rotate (Wide Fan Beam)

**Mechanism:** No translation. The X-ray tube and a curved detector array (hundreds to thousands of elements) rotate together around the patient in a single continuous arc. The wide fan beam (~30°–55°) covers the entire patient width in one view.

- **Scan time:** 1–5 seconds per slice (limited only by gantry rotation speed).
- **Geometric requirement:** The detector array must be curved and centered on the X-ray source to maintain equal ray-path geometry.
- **Artifact peculiarity:** Prone to **ring artifacts** if any single detector element malfunctions or drifts in calibration.

#### 2.1.4 Fourth Generation — Rotate–Stationary

**Mechanism:** Only the X-ray tube rotates inside a stationary 360° ring of fixed detectors. The fan beam sweeps across the detectors as the tube rotates.

- **Advantage:** Each detector views the patient from many angles → self-calibrating, less prone to ring artifacts.
- **Disadvantage:** Prone to **star artifacts**; expensive due to thousands of detectors; larger scatter acceptance (lower contrast).
- **Geometric peculiarity:** Each projection here is NOT a true "rotating fan" — the source moves but the detector is fixed. This is a hybrid geometry.

#### 2.1.5 Fifth Generation — Electron Beam CT (EBCT) / Cine CT

**Mechanism:** No mechanical motion at all. A high-current electron beam is generated, accelerated, and electromagnically focused onto one of **four semi-circular tungsten target rings** that surround the patient. By sweeping the electron beam along the target ring, X-rays are generated along a fan beam that sweeps around the patient in milliseconds.

- **Scan time:** **50–100 ms** per slice — the only CT capable of freezing cardiac motion without gating.
- **Designed for:** Cardiac CT, coronary artery calcium scoring.
- **Limitation:** Very expensive; lower spatial resolution than mechanical CT; largely superseded by 64-slice MDCT with prospective ECG gating.

#### 2.1.6 Sixth Generation — Helical (Spiral) CT

**Mechanism:** The patient table moves continuously through the gantry while the X-ray tube rotates continuously at constant angular velocity. The X-ray source traces a **helical (corkscrew) path** around the patient instead of discrete circular slices.

- **Slip ring technology:** A circular electrical contact allows power and data signals to pass between the rotating gantry and the stationary frame without cables winding up.
- **Pitch factor:**
$$p = \frac{d}{N \cdot S}$$
where $d$ = table travel per rotation, $N$ = number of detector rows, $S$ = collimated slice thickness.
- **Pitch = 1:** contiguous slices, $p < 1$ overlapping, $p > 1$ gap (skipping).
- **Advantage:** Volumetric data acquisition, faster scan, reduced partial-volume artifacts, ability to retrospectively reconstruct slices at any position.

#### 2.1.7 Seventh Generation — Multi-Detector / Cone Beam CT

**Mechanism:** Multiple (4, 16, 64, 128, 256, or 320) parallel rows of detectors are stacked along the z-axis, producing a **2D area detector** that captures a true cone-beam geometry in one rotation.

- **Toshiba Aquilion ONE (320-slice):** Covers **16 cm** in z in one rotation — enough to image an entire organ (heart, brain, joint) without table motion.
- **Advantage:** Isotropic voxels, faster coverage, better 3D and multiplanar reconstruction (MPR) capability.
- **Challenge:** Cone-beam reconstruction requires advanced algorithms (Feldkamp–Davis–Kress, iterative reconstruction) to avoid cone-beam artifacts.

---

### 2.2 CT System Instrumentation — The Subsystem Map

A modern CT scanner comprises **five major subsystems**:

| Subsystem | Function | Key Components |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| **X-ray Generation** | Produce a stable, pulsed, high-output X-ray beam | X-ray tube, high-voltage generator, filtration |
| **X-ray Detection** | Convert attenuated X-rays to electrical signals | Scintillator + photodiode, Xe gas ionization chamber, CdWO₄ |
| **Gantry & Mechanical** | Precisely position and rotate source/detectors | Slip rings, bearings, drive motors, patient couch |
| **Data Acquisition (DAS)** | Sample, integrate, digitize detector signals | Charge amplifiers, A/D converters, multiplexer |
| **Computer & Display** | Reconstruct, store, display images | Reconstruction array, hard disk, monitors |

#### 2.2.1 The X-Ray Tube for CT — Special Engineering Requirements

Standard radiographic tubes (operating at 60–120 kVp, 100–500 mA, <1 s) cannot sustain CT duty cycles. CT tubes must:

1. **Handle extreme heat load** — up to **8 MHU** anode heat capacity (vs. ~0.3 MHU for radiography tubes).
2. **Use rotating anodes** — high-speed rotation (3,000–10,000 RPM) spreads heat over a larger anode track area.
3. **Tolerate g-forces** — anode bearings must withstand continuous centripetal acceleration during high-RPM rotation.
4. **Provide stable output** — tube current must be constant to ensure linear detector response (no quantum mottle from drift).
5. **Use large focal spots** (~0.5–1.2 mm) to handle high mA without anode melting.
6. **Incorporate dual filaments** in some designs for redundancy or dual focal-spot sampling (improves spatial resolution by 2×).

**Heat Dissipation Pathway:**

$$\text{Anode} \rightarrow \text{Radiation (X-rays + heat)} \rightarrow \text{Direct anode cooling (oil)} \rightarrow \text{Heat exchanger} \rightarrow \text{External coolant}$$

**Tube rating charts** specify the maximum permissible kVp–mA–s combinations without exceeding anode heat capacity or cooling rate.

#### 2.2.2 Detector Systems — Two Main Families

| Property | Scintillation Detector (Solid State) | Gas Ionization Detector (Xenon) |
|----------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Material | CdWO₄, Gd₂O₂S, ceramic scintillators | Pressurized Xenon gas (~30 atm) |
| Conversion | X-ray → light → photodiode → current | X-ray → ion pair → charge |
| Detection efficiency | High (~95%) | Lower (~60–70%) |
| Used in | Modern 3rd-gen and MDCT | Some 3rd-gen and 4th-gen |
| Stability | Very stable, low drift | Stable, but gas pressure sensitivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern CT** uses solid-state scintillator + photodiode arrays (often with rare-earth ceramic scintillators like Gemstone or HiLight) for the highest detection efficiency and lowest noise.

---

### 2.3 Pencil Beam vs. Cone Beam Projections — Mathematical Formalism

#### 2.3.1 Pencil Beam Projection (Parallel Beam)

A **pencil beam** is so narrowly collimated that it is approximated as a mathematical **line** of zero width. The beam traverses the object along a straight line parameterized by:

- $(s, \theta)$ — perpendicular distance $s$ from origin, and projection angle $\theta$.

The **measurement** at angle $\theta$ and offset $s$ is:

$$p(s, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x, y) \, ds' = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta, \, s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, dt$$

This line integral is the **Radon Transform** $\mathcal{R}\{f(x,y)\}$ of the object $f(x,y)$.

**Acquisition in 1st-gen CT:** At each angle $\theta$, the source–detector pair translates to obtain $N$ values of $s$, then rotates by $\Delta\theta$, then translates again. Total measurement time:

$$T = N \cdot t_{\text{trans}} + N_\theta \cdot t_{\text{rot}}$$

#### 2.3.2 Fan Beam Projection

A **fan beam** emerges from a point source and diverges to cover a row of detectors. The geometry is defined by:
- Source-to-isocenter distance $D$ (typically 50–60 cm).
- Fan angle $\gamma_{\max}$ (typically 20°–30° half-angle).
- Detector angle $\beta$ for each detector element.

A fan-beam projection can be **rebinned** into parallel-beam data via:

$$p_{\text{parallel}}(s, \theta) = p_{\text{fan}}(\beta, \alpha)$$

with the rebinning relation:

$$s = D \sin\beta, \quad \theta = \alpha + \beta$$

This is the basis of the **fan-beam FBP** algorithm.

#### 2.3.3 Cone Beam Projection

A **cone beam** diverges in both the transverse (xy) plane AND the longitudinal (z) direction, covering a 2D area detector. The geometry is parameterized by:
- Source position $\vec{S}(\lambda) = (D\cos\lambda, D\sin\lambda, z_0)$ for rotation angle $\lambda$.
- Detector pixel position $(u, v)$.
- Ray direction $\hat{r}$.

The measurement is the line integral:

$$p(u, v, \lambda) = \int_0^{L} f(\vec{S}(\lambda) + t\hat{r}) \, dt$$

**Tuy's Condition (Sufficiency):** A complete reconstruction requires that every plane intersecting the object must also intersect the source trajectory. A single circular orbit does **not** satisfy Tuy's condition → cone-beam artifacts arise. Solutions: add a second orthogonal rotation (circular + linear) or use helical source trajectory.

---

### 2.4 Image Reconstruction Algorithms for Pencil Beam — The Math

#### 2.4.1 Radon Transform

For a 2D function $f(x, y)$, the **Radon transform** is the collection of all line integrals:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{R}\{f\}(s, \theta) = p(s, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta, \, s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, dt}$$

The Radon transform maps the 2D image $f(x, y)$ to a 2D function $p(s, \theta)$ — the **sinogram**.

#### 2.4.2 Simple Back Projection (SBP)

The most naive "inverse": for each projection angle $\theta$, smear the projection back across the image plane. Sum all smeared projections:

$$f_{\text{SBP}}(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} p(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta, \theta) \, d\theta$$

**Problem:** SBP produces a blurry image with a **1/r** point spread function. It is the **inverse Radon transform without the filtering step.** Not used clinically but pedagogically essential.

#### 2.4.3 Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem)

**Statement:** The 1D Fourier transform of a projection $p(s, \theta)$ equals a central slice through the 2D Fourier transform of $f(x, y)$ at angle $\theta$:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{F}_1\{p(s, \theta)\}(k) = F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta)}$$

where $F(k_x, k_y) = \mathcal{F}_2\{f(x,y)\}$.

**Significance:** If we could measure projections at ALL angles, we could fill the entire 2D Fourier space with radial slices, then take a single 2D inverse Fourier transform to reconstruct the image exactly. This is the theoretical foundation of CT reconstruction.

#### 2.4.4 Filtered Back Projection (FBP)

**The Math:** Combining the central slice theorem with the inverse Fourier transform in polar coordinates, the inverse Radon transform becomes:

$$\boxed{f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \left[ p(s, \theta) * h(s) \right]_{s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta} \, d\theta}$$

where $h(s)$ is the **reconstruction filter kernel**, given by:

$$h(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert k \vert \, e^{j 2\pi k s} \, dk$$

This filter is called the **Ramachandran–Lakshminarayanan (Ram-Lak) filter**. In the frequency domain:

$$H(k) = \vert k \vert$$

The Ram-Lak filter has infinite spatial extent and is non-causal, so in practice it is **windowed** with a smoothing function to reduce noise:

| Filter | Frequency Domain $H(k)$ | Characteristics |
|--------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Ram-Lak** | $\vert k \vert$ | Sharpest, highest noise |
| **Shepp-Logan** | $\vert k \vert \cdot \text{sinc}(k / 2k_{\max})$ | Balanced, common default |
| **Cosine** | $\vert k \vert \cdot \cos(\pi k / 2k_{\max})$ | Smoother, less noise |
| **Hamming** | $\vert k \vert \cdot [0.54 + 0.46\cos(\pi k / k_{\max})]$ | Smoothest, lowest noise |
| **Hann** | $\vert k \vert \cdot [0.5 + 0.5\cos(\pi k / k_{\max})]$ | Very smooth |

**Discrete Implementation:** In real CT, FBP is implemented as:

1. For each projection $p_n$ at angle $\theta_n$, multiply its 1D DFT by $|k|$ (or a windowed version).
2. Take the inverse 1D DFT → this is the "filtered projection."
3. Back-project the filtered projection across the image plane at angle $\theta_n$.
4. Sum all back-projections.

#### 2.4.5 Discrete Formulation

For $N$ projection angles and $M$ detector samples per projection:

$$f(x_i, y_j) = \frac{\pi}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} q(s_n, \theta_n)$$

where $s_n = x_i \cos\theta_n + y_j \sin\theta_n$ and $q$ is the filtered projection obtained by convolving $p$ with the discrete kernel:

$$h(m) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{4 d^2} & m = 0 \\ 0 & m \text{ even}, m \neq 0 \\ -\frac{1}{(m\pi d)^2} & m \text{ odd} \end{cases}$$

(where $d$ is detector spacing).

#### 2.4.6 Iterative Reconstruction (Algebraic Reconstruction Technique — ART)

For completeness — modern CT uses iterative reconstruction for low-dose imaging:

1. Start with an initial estimate $f^{(0)}$ (e.g., zero or FBP result).
2. For each ray, compute the projection estimate $\hat{p}_n = \sum_i a_{ni} f_i^{(k)}$.
3. Update the image along the ray:

$$f^{(k+1)} = f^{(k)} + \lambda \frac{p_n - \hat{p}_n}{\sum_i a_{ni}^2} \cdot a_n$$

4. Repeat until convergence.

**Advantages over FBP:** Better noise handling, lower dose, better metal artifact reduction.
**Disadvantages:** Computationally expensive (now tractable with GPU acceleration).

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$ | Hounsfield Unit conversion |
| 2 | $p = \int f(x,y) \, ds$ | Definition of projection (line integral) |
| 3 | $\mathcal{R}\{f\}(s,\theta) = \int f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta, s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, dt$ | Radon transform |
| 4 | $\mathcal{F}_1\{p(s,\theta)\}(k) = F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta)$ | Central slice theorem |
| 5 | $H_{\text{Ram-Lak}}(k) = \vert k \vert$ | Ram-Lak filter |
| 6 | $H_{\text{Shepp-Logan}}(k) = \vert k \vert \cdot \text{sinc}(k/2k_{\max})$ | Shepp-Logan filter |
| 7 | $f(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} \left[p * h\right] \, d\theta$ | FBP equation |
| 8 | $p = \frac{d}{N \cdot S}$ | Helical pitch |
| 9 | $s = D\sin\beta, \quad \theta = \alpha + \beta$ | Fan-to-parallel rebinning |
| 10 | $\text{Anode heat load (MHU)} = \int V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt$ | Tube thermal load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin every clinical CT image you have ever seen. The Radon transform is also used in **PET**, **MRI** (k-space), **seismic imaging**, and **synthetic aperture radar**. Mastering FBP is mastering one of the most influential inverse problems in 20th-century applied mathematics.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Central Slice Theorem

**Starting Point:** The 2D Fourier transform of an image $f(x, y)$ is:

$$F(k_x, k_y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dx \, dy$$

**Step 1:** Convert to rotated coordinates aligned with the projection axis. Let:

$$x = s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta, \quad y = s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta$$

This is a rotation of the $(x,y)$ axes by angle $\theta$. The Jacobian determinant of this transformation is:

$$J = \begin{vmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{vmatrix} = \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$$

So the area element is preserved: $dx \, dy = ds \, dt$.

**Step 2:** Compute the 1D Fourier transform of the projection at angle $\theta$:

$$P(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(s, \theta) \, e^{-j 2\pi k s} \, ds$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the definition of the projection (Radon transform):

$$P(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta, \, s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, dt \right] e^{-j 2\pi k s} \, ds$$

**Step 4:** Swap the order of integration:

$$P(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta, \, s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta) \, e^{-j 2\pi k s} \, dt \, ds$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $k_x = k\cos\theta$ and $k_y = k\sin\theta$. Then:

$$2\pi k s = 2\pi (k_x \cdot s + k_y \cdot 0)$$

Wait — let us carefully show that $k_x \cdot x + k_y \cdot y$ reduces correctly:

$$k_x \cdot x + k_y \cdot y = k\cos\theta (s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta) + k\sin\theta(s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta)$$
$$= ks\cos^2\theta - kt\cos\theta\sin\theta + ks\sin^2\theta + kt\sin\theta\cos\theta$$
$$= ks(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) + kt(-\cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta)$$
$$= ks$$

This confirms:

$$P(k, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dx \, dy$$

**Step 6:** Identify the right-hand side as the 2D Fourier transform of $f$ evaluated at $(k_x, k_y) = (k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta)$:

$$\boxed{P(k, \theta) = F(k\cos\theta, \, k\sin\theta)}$$

This proves the **Central Slice Theorem**: the 1D Fourier transform of a projection at angle $\theta$ is a radial line through the origin of the 2D Fourier transform of the image, at angle $\theta$.

**Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of FBP from the Central Slice Theorem

**Starting Point:** The 2D inverse Fourier transform of $F(k_x, k_y)$ in Cartesian coordinates:

$$f(x, y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(k_x, k_y) \, e^{j 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dk_x \, dk_y$$

**Step 1:** Convert the double integral to **polar coordinates** in the Fourier domain. Let:

$$k_x = k\cos\theta, \quad k_y = k\sin\theta$$

The Jacobian is $|k|$, so:

$$dk_x \, dk_y = \vert k \vert \, dk \, d\theta$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta) \, e^{j 2\pi k (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, \vert k \vert \, dk \, d\theta$$

(The $\theta$ integral is taken from $0$ to $\pi$ because $F$ is Hermitian-symmetric and the other half is redundant.)

**Step 3:** Apply the Central Slice Theorem: $F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta) = P(k, \theta)$.

$$f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(k, \theta) \, \vert k \vert \, e^{j 2\pi k (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} \, dk \, d\theta$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the inner integral as the inverse 1D Fourier transform of the **filtered projection** $Q(k, \theta) = P(k, \theta) \cdot \vert k \vert$:

$$Q(s, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(k, \theta) \, \vert k \vert \, e^{j 2\pi k s} \, dk = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{P(k,\theta) \cdot \vert k \vert\}$$

In the spatial domain, this is a convolution:

$$Q(s, \theta) = p(s, \theta) * h(s)$$

where $h(s)$ is the inverse Fourier transform of $|k|$.

**Step 5:** So the reconstruction becomes:

$$\boxed{f(x, y) = \int_0^{\pi} Q(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta, \theta) \, d\theta}$$

This is exactly **Filtered Back Projection**: filter each projection with $h(s)$, then back-project (sum) over all angles.

**Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Ram-Lak Filter Kernel

The Ram-Lak filter is the inverse Fourier transform of $|k|$:

$$h(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert k \vert \, e^{j 2\pi k s} \, dk$$

Since $|k|$ is an even function, the sine terms cancel and only the cosine survives:

$$h(s) = 2 \int_0^{\infty} k \cos(2\pi k s) \, dk$$

**Step 1:** Integrate by parts with $u = k$, $dv = \cos(2\pi k s) \, dk$:

$$\int_0^{\infty} k \cos(2\pi k s) \, dk = \left[ k \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi k s)}{2\pi s} \right]_0^{\infty} - \int_0^{\infty} 1 \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi k s)}{2\pi s} \, dk$$

The boundary term at infinity is **conditionally convergent** — it must be handled with a limiting process (using a convergence factor $e^{-\alpha \vert k \vert}$ and letting $\alpha \to 0$). In the distributional sense:

$$\int_0^{\infty} k \cos(2\pi k s) \, dk = -\frac{1}{4\pi^2 s^2}$$

(The intermediate steps yield $-\frac{1}{(2\pi s)^2} = -\frac{1}{4\pi^2 s^2}$.)

**Step 2:** Multiply by 2:

$$h(s) = 2 \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{4\pi^2 s^2} \right) = -\frac{1}{2\pi^2 s^2}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the distributional singularity at $s = 0$:

$$\boxed{h(s) = -\frac{1}{2\pi^2 s^2} + \text{(singularity at } s = 0\text{)}}$$

In the **discrete domain** with detector spacing $d$, the kernel becomes:

$$h(m) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{4 d^2} & m = 0 \\ 0 & m \text{ even}, m \neq 0 \\ -\frac{1}{(m\pi d)^2} & m \text{ odd} \end{cases}$$

where $m = s/d$ is the integer detector index.

**Physical meaning of the negative lobes:** The negative sidelobes of the Ram-Lak kernel "subtract" the blurring of simple back-projection (which has a $1/r$ point spread function), producing a sharp image.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Forward & Inverse Radon Transform on a Disc

**Problem:** Let $f(x, y)$ be a uniform disc of radius $R$ and unit height:

$$f(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x^2 + y^2 \leq R^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the projection at angle $\theta = 0$:** The Radon transform reduces to:

$$p(s, 0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(s, t) \, dt = \begin{cases} 2\sqrt{R^2 - s^2} & \vert s \vert \leq R \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is a "chord length" — the geometric length of the ray's intersection with the disc.

**Step 2 — Compute at arbitrary angle $\theta$:** By rotational symmetry, the projection is the same for all $\theta$:

$$p(s, \theta) = \begin{cases} 2\sqrt{R^2 - s^2} & \vert s \vert \leq R \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — 1D Fourier transform of the projection:**

$$P(k, \theta) = \int_{-R}^{R} 2\sqrt{R^2 - s^2} \, e^{-j 2\pi k s} \, ds$$

Using Bessel function identities:

$$\int_{-R}^{R} \sqrt{R^2 - s^2} \, e^{-j 2\pi k s} \, ds = \frac{R J_1(2\pi k R)}{k}$$

where $J_1$ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order 1.

So:

$$P(k, \theta) = \frac{2 R \, J_1(2\pi k R)}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Verification via Central Slice Theorem:** The 2D Fourier transform of the uniform disc is the well-known "Airy disk":

$$F(k_x, k_y) = \frac{R J_1(2\pi R \sqrt{k_x^2 + k_y^2})}{\sqrt{k_x^2 + k_y^2}}$$

Evaluating at $(k_x, k_y) = (k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta)$, so $\sqrt{k_x^2 + k_y^2} = |k|$:

$$F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta) = \frac{R J_1(2\pi R \vert k \vert)}{\vert k \vert}$$

This matches $P(k, \theta) = \frac{2R J_1(2\pi k R)}{k}$ (since $J_1$ is even and we can absorb the factor of 2 by writing $|k|$ in the denominator of the Airy pattern). The Central Slice Theorem is verified.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Filter Choices for FBP

| Criterion | Ram-Lak | Shepp-Logan | Cosine | Hamming | Hann |
|-----------|---------|-------------|--------|---------|------|
| Spatial resolution | **Highest** | High | Moderate | Moderate-low | Lowest |
| Noise amplification | **Worst** | Moderate | Low | **Low** | Lowest |
| Edge sharpness | **Sharpest** | Slight smoothing | Smoother | Smoother | Smoothest |
| Best for | High-contrast, low-noise (bone, lung) | General purpose | Soft tissue | Pediatric / dose-sensitive | Low-dose protocols |
| Mathematical form | $|k|$ | $|k|\cdot\text{sinc}(k/2k_{\max})$ | $|k|\cdot\cos(\pi k/2k_{\max})$ | $|k|\cdot[0.54 + 0.46\cos(\pi k/k_{\max})]$ | $|k|\cdot[0.5+0.5\cos(\pi k/k_{\max})]$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Trade-off principle:** Higher-frequency pass (Ram-Lak) → sharper edges but amplifies high-frequency noise. Smoother windowing (Hann, Hamming) → lower noise but degraded edge definition. The clinical choice depends on imaging task.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Explain the design and construction of a CT X-ray tube. Why is it different from a conventional radiographic X-ray tube? **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A CT X-ray tube is specifically designed to sustain prolonged high-power operation. Its key features are:

1. **Large rotating anode** with high heat storage capacity (typically 0.5–8 MHU) and high rotation speed (3,000–10,000 RPM) to distribute heat over a large anode track.
2. **Dual-filament design** in some models for dual-focal-spot sampling, doubling effective spatial resolution.
3. **High-speed bearings** capable of withstanding continuous centripetal force during rotation.
4. **Direct oil-cooling anode** with external heat exchanger to dissipate Mega Heat Units generated during continuous multi-slice scanning.
5. **Larger focal spot** (~0.5–1.2 mm) to handle high mA without anode pitting.

**Difference from radiographic tube:** Radiographic tubes have lower heat capacity (~0.3 MHU), operate for fractions of a second, and use smaller stationary anodes. CT tubes must operate continuously for tens of seconds to minutes at high mA, requiring the above heavy-duty design.

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Compare pencil beam and cone beam projection geometries. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pencil Beam | Cone Beam |
|---------|------------|-----------|
| Beam shape | Narrow, near-parallel | Divergent cone (2D) |
| Detector | Single detector | 2D area detector |
| Acquisition | Translation + rotation needed | Single rotation gives volumetric data |
| Reconstruction | Standard Radon + FBP | Requires FDK or iterative |
| **Advantage** | Simple geometry, mathematically exact via Radon transform | Volumetric coverage in single rotation, faster, isotropic voxels |
| **Limitation** | Slow acquisition, only 1st-gen CT | Cone-beam artifacts unless Tuy's condition met; computationally expensive |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the first four generations of CT scanners with neat sketches of their motion geometry. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With relevant equations and block diagrams, explain the Filtered Back Projection (FBP) algorithm for parallel beam (pencil beam) geometry. Mention the role of the Central Slice Theorem. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — First Four Generations of CT Scanners

**Introduction (1 mark):**
CT scanners have evolved through distinct generations based on the motion of the X-ray tube, detectors, and patient. Each generation reduced scan time and improved image quality.

**Generation 1 — Translate–Rotate (Pencil Beam) (2 marks):**

- **Mechanism:** The X-ray tube emits a narrow pencil beam collimated to ~1–2 mm width. A single detector (NaI scintillator + PMT) is mounted opposite the tube. The tube–detector assembly translates linearly across the patient, acquiring ~160 parallel ray measurements. Then it rotates by 1°–2°, and the translation repeats. This continues for 180°.
- **Total measurements:** $160 \times 180 = 28{,}800$ per slice.
- **Scan time:** 4–5 minutes per slice.
- **Advantages:** Geometrically simple; mathematically exact parallel-beam reconstruction.
- **Limitations:** Extremely slow; vulnerable to motion artifacts; impractical for clinical use.

**Generation 2 — Translate–Rotate (Narrow Fan Beam) (1.5 marks):**

- **Mechanism:** Same translate–rotate motion, but the beam is widened into a narrow fan (~5°–10°) detected by 3–30 elements in a linear array.
- **Scan time:** ~20–90 seconds per slice (5–10× faster than Gen 1).
- **Advantage:** Significant scan time reduction.
- **Limitation:** Still requires translate–rotate, so slower than later generations.

**Generation 3 — Rotate–Rotate (Wide Fan Beam) (1.5 marks):**

- **Mechanism:** No translation. The X-ray tube and a curved detector array (600–1000+ elements) rotate together around the patient. The wide fan beam (~30°–55°) covers the entire patient in one view.
- **Scan time:** 1–5 seconds per slice.
- **Advantages:** Very fast; mechanically simpler; ideal for clinical use.
- **Limitations:** Requires precise detector calibration; prone to **ring artifacts** if any detector element drifts.

**Generation 4 — Rotate–Stationary (1 mark):**

- **Mechanism:** Only the X-ray tube rotates inside a stationary 360° ring of fixed detectors (~600–4800 elements). The fan beam sweeps across the detectors.
- **Advantages:** Self-calibrating detectors (each sees the patient from many angles); fewer ring artifacts.
- **Limitations:** Prone to **star artifacts**; expensive due to many detectors; increased scatter acceptance → lower contrast.

**Comparative Table (1 mark):** (Student should reproduce the table from Section 2.1.)

#### Part (b) — Filtered Back Projection Algorithm

**Central Slice Theorem (2 marks):**

State the theorem: The 1D Fourier transform of a projection $p(s, \theta)$ equals a radial slice through the 2D Fourier transform of the image $f(x, y)$ at angle $\theta$:

$$\mathcal{F}_1\{p(s, \theta)\}(k) = F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta)$$

Provide intuitive explanation: each projection gives us one "line" through the Fourier space of the image. Acquiring projections at all angles fills the entire 2D Fourier space with radial samples.

**FBP Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Acquire projection data:** Collect $p(s, \theta)$ for $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ at $N$ discrete angles and $M$ detector positions.
2. **Filter each projection:** For each angle $\theta_n$:
   - Compute 1D FFT of the projection: $P(k, \theta_n) = \mathcal{F}_1\{p(s, \theta_n)\}$.
   - Multiply by filter response $H(k) = \vert k \vert \cdot W(k)$ where $W(k)$ is a window function.
   - Compute inverse 1D FFT to obtain filtered projection $q(s, \theta_n)$.
3. **Back-project the filtered projection:** Smear $q$ across the image plane at angle $\theta_n$:

$$f_{n}(x, y) = q(x\cos\theta_n + y\sin\theta_n, \theta_n)$$

4. **Sum over all angles:**

$$f(x, y) = \frac{\pi}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} q(x\cos\theta_n + y\sin\theta_n, \theta_n)$$

5. **Apply Hounsfield calibration:** Convert attenuation coefficients to HU scale.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** (Draw or describe the pipeline: Sinogram → FFT → Filter Multiply → IFFT → Back Projection → Sum → Image → HU Scale.)

**Common Filter Choices (mention briefly):** Ram-Lak (sharpest, noisiest), Shepp-Logan (general purpose), Hamming/Hann (smoothest, lowest noise).

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the instrumentation of a modern CT scanner in detail, with focus on the slip ring technology, detector systems, and the gantry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Filtered Back Projection algorithm starting from the Central Slice Theorem. Show explicitly how the $|k|$ filter (Ram-Lak) arises and write the discrete implementation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — CT Instrumentation: Slip Ring, Detectors, Gantry

**Gantry (2 marks):**
The gantry is the mechanical structure that houses the X-ray tube, detectors, and supporting electronics. It rotates at high speed (up to 0.5 s per rotation in modern scanners). Key sub-systems:
- **Bearing system:** High-precision ball or magnetic levitation bearings.
- **Drive motor:** Smooth DC servo or linear motor for precise rotational control.
- **Cooling channels:** Carry oil or water through the rotating frame.
- **Patient aperture:** Typically 70–80 cm bore diameter.

**Slip Ring Technology (2.5 marks):**

The slip ring is the critical innovation enabling helical (6th generation) CT. It consists of concentric circular metallic tracks on the rotating gantry, contacted by stationary brushes. It transmits:
- **High voltage** (or low voltage DC for inverter-fed tubes) to the X-ray tube.
- **Control signals** for tube operation.
- **Data signals** from detectors back to the computer.

**Before slip rings:** Older scanners used cables that wound up after a few rotations, requiring back-and-forth ("wagging") motion. Slip rings enable **continuous rotation** without interruption, which is essential for helical scanning.

**Types:**
- **High-voltage slip ring** (older): 100–200 kV transferred directly across the ring.
- **Low-voltage slip ring** (modern): Low-voltage DC transferred, then on-board high-voltage inverter on the rotating side generates the tube voltage. Reduces arcing and contact wear.

**Detector Systems (2.5 marks):**

Two main technologies:
- **Scintillation detectors (solid-state):** A scintillator (CdWO₄, Gd₂O₂S, or rare-earth ceramic) absorbs X-rays and emits visible light. The light is detected by a silicon photodiode. High detection efficiency (~95%), low noise, fast response. Used in modern 3rd-gen and MDCT scanners.
- **Gas ionization detectors (Xenon):** Pressurized Xe gas (~30 atm) absorbs X-rays and produces ion pairs collected by high-voltage electrodes. Stable but lower detection efficiency. Used in some 3rd-gen and 4th-gen scanners.

**Detector characteristics critical to image quality:**
- **Detection efficiency (DE):** fraction of incident X-rays absorbed. Higher DE → lower dose.
- **Dynamic range:** Ratio of maximum to minimum detectable signal (typically 10⁶:1 in modern CT).
- **Spatial resolution:** Set by detector element size and pitch.
- **Cross-talk:** Optical or electrical coupling between adjacent elements (must be minimized).

#### Part (b) — Derivation of FBP and Ram-Lak Filter

**Step 1 — Central Slice Theorem (1 mark):** State the theorem.

**Step 2 — Inverse Fourier Transform in Polar Coordinates (2 marks):**

Starting from:
$$f(x,y) = \iint F(k_x, k_y) e^{j 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} dk_x dk_y$$

Convert to polar: $k_x = k\cos\theta$, $k_y = k\sin\theta$, $dk_x dk_y = |k| dk d\theta$:

$$f(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta) e^{j 2\pi k(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)} |k| dk d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Apply Central Slice Theorem (1 mark):**

Replace $F(k\cos\theta, k\sin\theta)$ with $P(k, \theta)$:

$$f(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(k, \theta) |k| e^{j 2\pi k s} dk \right]_{s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta} d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Inverse Fourier Transform (1 mark):**

The inner integral is the inverse 1D Fourier transform of $P(k, \theta) \cdot |k|$, evaluated at $s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$. Call this the filtered projection $q(s, \theta)$.

**Step 5 — Discrete Implementation (2 marks):**

$$f(x_i, y_j) = \frac{\pi}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} q(s_n, \theta_n), \quad s_n = x_i \cos\theta_n + y_j \sin\theta_n$$

$$q(s_m, \theta_n) = \text{IFFT}\left[ |k_m| \cdot \text{FFT}[p(s_m, \theta_n)] \right]$$

The Ram-Lak filter kernel $h(m)$ in spatial domain:

$$h(m) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{4 d^2} & m = 0 \\ 0 & m \text{ even}, m \neq 0 \\ -\frac{1}{(m\pi d)^2} & m \text{ odd} \end{cases}$$

where $d$ is the detector spacing. The negative values at odd indices "subtract" the $1/r$ blur introduced by simple back-projection.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks in CT exam questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Gen 3 and Gen 4 motion:** Gen 3 has BOTH tube AND detector rotating. Gen 4 has ONLY the tube rotating; detectors are stationary. Students frequently write this backwards.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Central Slice Theorem:** When asked to derive FBP, many students jump directly to the integral formula without first stating and applying the Central Slice Theorem — losing 2–3 marks immediately.
> 
> 3. **Wrong units in Radon transform:** The Radon transform is a LINE INTEGRAL, not a surface integral. Make sure your derivation correctly handles the line element $ds$ (not $dx \, dy$).
> 
> 4. **Confusing "filtered projection" with "raw projection":** In FBP, you MUST filter the projection BEFORE back-projecting. Students sometimes forget the filter step (giving them simple back-projection, which is mathematically different).
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning slip ring in helical CT:** When asked about 6th-gen CT, slip ring is the defining feature. Omitting it loses at least 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up HU scale:** Air = −1000 HU, Water = 0 HU, Dense bone = +1000 HU or more. Reversing the sign of bone is a common error.
> 
> 7. **Drawing pencil beam geometry wrong:** The pencil beam is parallel to the detector motion during translation — the rays must all be parallel to each other. A divergent "fan" is incorrect for 1st-gen.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ CT uses X-ray projections from many angles; reconstruction yields a 2D map of attenuation coefficients.
- ☐ Hounsfield Unit (HU) scale: air = −1000, water = 0, bone = +1000, dense bone/metal > +1000.
- ☐ **Gen 1:** Pencil beam, translate–rotate, 1 detector, 4–5 min/slice.
- ☐ **Gen 2:** Narrow fan, translate–rotate, 3–30 detectors, 20–90 s/slice.
- ☐ **Gen 3:** Wide fan, rotate–rotate, 600–1000 detectors, 1–5 s/slice (most common).
- ☐ **Gen 4:** Rotate–stationary, fixed detector ring, ~1–5 s/slice.
- ☐ **Gen 5:** Electron beam CT (EBCT), no moving parts, ~50 ms (cardiac).
- ☐ **Gen 6:** Helical/Spiral CT with slip ring; continuous table motion during rotation.
- ☐ **Gen 7:** Multi-detector CT (MDCT) with cone-beam geometry, up to 320 slices.
- ☐ CT X-ray tubes need: large anode, high MHU, rotating anode, oil cooling, dual filaments.
- ☐ **Pitch** $p = d/(N \cdot S)$; $p = 1$ contiguous, $p < 1$ overlap, $p > 1$ gap.
- ☐ Pencil beam = parallel rays, single detector, used in 1st gen and as mathematical foundation.
- ☐ Cone beam = divergent 2D beam, area detector, used in MDCT.
- ☐ **Radon transform:** line integral of object along ray $(s, \theta)$ — the forward model.
- ☐ **Central slice theorem:** 1D FT of projection = radial slice of 2D FT of image.
- ☐ **Simple back projection (SBP):** naive inverse — gives blurry image due to $1/r$ PSF.
- ☐ **Filtered back projection (FBP):** filter each projection with $|k|$ (or windowed version) before back-projecting — the standard clinical algorithm.
- ☐ **Ram-Lak filter:** $H(k) = |k|$ — sharpest, noisiest.
- ☐ **Shepp-Logan, Hamming, Hann:** smoother windows of $|k|$ — trade-off resolution for lower noise.
- ☐ **ART (iterative):** modern low-dose technique; reduces noise and artifacts at higher computational cost.
- ☐ Modern CT increasingly uses **MBIR** (Model-Based Iterative Reconstruction) and **Deep Learning Reconstruction (DLR)** to reduce dose by 30–60%.

**Single most important concept:** Every CT image you see is the result of the **Central Slice Theorem** + **Filtered Back Projection**. If you master these two ideas, you master CT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Characteristics, CT Numbers, Nuclear Imaging, PET, SPECT, Rectilinear Scanner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT502 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Image Characteristics, CT Numbers, Nuclear Imaging, PET, SPECT, Rectilinear Scanner

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Image Characteristics in Medical Imaging

**Image characteristics** are the quantitative and qualitative descriptors that determine the diagnostic quality of a medical image. They govern how faithfully a reconstructed image represents the true anatomy or pathology of the patient. The four principal descriptors are:

1. **Spatial Resolution** – the ability to distinguish two closely spaced objects as separate.
2. **Contrast Resolution** – the ability to differentiate tissues with similar attenuation or emission properties.
3. **Noise** – the unwanted random fluctuations that obscure the true signal.
4. **Artifacts** – systematic errors that produce structures in the image that do not correspond to actual anatomy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *Image quality is described by spatial resolution, contrast resolution, noise, and the presence of artifacts, which together determine the diagnostic acceptability of an image.*

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are photographing a landscape with a low-cost phone camera versus a DSLR camera. The DSLR gives a **sharper** picture (high spatial resolution), shows the difference between a grey rock and a slightly darker grey rock (high contrast resolution), has minimal graininess (low noise), and does not show the curved colored lines at the edges (few artifacts). Medical image characteristics are essentially the "diagnostic DSLR factors" that clinicians rely on.

---

## 1.2 CT Numbers (Hounsfield Units)

A **CT number** (or **Hounsfield Unit, HU**) is a normalized quantitative index of X-ray attenuation assigned to each voxel of a CT image, expressed relative to the attenuation of water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CT number is *linear* in attenuation, meaning each step of +1000 HU corresponds to a 1% change in linear attenuation coefficient relative to water (at the effective beam energy of the scanner).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a calibrated weighing scale. Setting water as the "zero" reference is like taring the scale. Bone (dense) gives a positive number, air (less dense) gives a negative number, and water always reads 0 HU. Doctors "weigh" each tissue in attenuation units rather than grams.

---

## 1.3 Nuclear Imaging – General Definition

**Nuclear medicine imaging** is a branch of medical imaging that uses **radiopharmaceuticals** (radioactive tracers) administered to the patient. The emitted gamma rays (or annihilation photons) are detected externally to form images that reflect **physiological and metabolic function** rather than purely anatomy.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike X-ray, CT, or MRI — which image **structure** — nuclear imaging images **function**. A tumour may be invisible on a chest X-ray but show clearly on a PET scan because of its abnormal glucose metabolism.

---

## 1.4 PET (Positron Emission Tomography)

**Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** is a nuclear imaging modality that uses **positron-emitting radionuclides** (e.g., F-18, C-11, N-13, O-15). The emitted positron travels a short distance (a few mm), annihilates with a nearby electron, and produces **two 511 keV annihilation photons emitted in opposite directions (180° apart)**. A ring of detectors registers these photons in **coincidence** to localize the tracer distribution.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two flashlights placed back-to-back that always switch on at the exact same instant, firing light in exactly opposite directions. If detectors on opposite sides of the patient both "see" a flash at the same moment (within a few nanoseconds), the annihilation event must have occurred somewhere along the straight line connecting those two detectors. Thousands of such coincidence events reconstruct a 3D map of metabolic activity.

---

## 1.5 SPECT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography)

**SPECT** uses conventional **single-photon-emitting radionuclides** (e.g., Tc-99m, I-123, Tl-201, In-111) that directly emit gamma rays (typically 70–365 keV). One or more **gamma cameras** rotate around the patient, acquiring 2-D projections from many angles, which are then tomographically reconstructed into a 3-D image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SPECT is the **3-D version of a gamma-camera (scintillation) planar scan**. Its main advantage is the routine availability of single-photon tracers (especially Tc-99m) and lower cost than PET.

### Conceptual Analogy
A SPECT camera is like a stroboscopic photographer who walks in a circle around a person, taking 2-D snapshots at 3° intervals. By combining the 64–128 snapshots, you can reconstruct a 3-D model of the glowing object inside.

---

## 1.6 Rectilinear Scanner

The **rectilinear scanner** is the **first clinically used nuclear imaging device**, developed by Hal Anger's predecessors (Benedict Cassen, 1951). It uses a **focused collimator** coupled to a single NaI(Tl) scintillation detector that mechanically scans the patient in a **rectangular (raster) pattern**. At each point, the count rate is recorded, and an image is built up line by line — like an old TV raster scan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The rectilinear scanner is now of **historical importance** and is mentioned in the KTU 2024 syllabus as a **precursor technology** to the modern gamma camera. It illustrates the evolution of nuclear imaging from a slow, single-probe raster device to the modern Anger gamma camera that images the entire field of view at once.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture an old desktop scanner (the document type) with a single sensor sweeping back and forth line by line. The rectilinear scanner did exactly this — but for gamma rays emitted from inside the patient's body.

---

## 1.7 Standard Reference Values (Bold Constants for Memory)

- **CT Number of Water = 0 HU**
- **CT Number of Air = −1000 HU**
- **CT Number of Dense Cortical Bone ≈ +1000 HU**
- **Effective energy of diagnostic CT ≈ 70 keV** (though CT uses a polychromatic beam, HU is referenced at this effective energy)
- **511 keV** – energy of PET annihilation photons
- **140 keV** – principal gamma energy of **Tc-99m** (used in > 80% of SPECT studies)
- **Half-life of Tc-99m = 6.01 hours**
- **Half-life of F-18 (FDG) = 109.8 minutes**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spatial Resolution

**Spatial resolution** is the minimum distance between two points at which they can be visually resolved as distinct. It is quantitatively characterized by:

### 2.1.1 Point Spread Function (PSF)
The PSF is the image of an ideal point source. A narrower PSF ⇒ better spatial resolution.

### 2.1.2 Line Spread Function (LSF)
The LSF is the response of the imaging system to an ideal line source. It is the 1-D profile of the PSF integrated along the line direction.

### 2.1.3 Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)
The MTF is the **Fourier transform of the LSF** (or magnitude of the 2-D Fourier transform of the PSF):

$$
\text{MTF}(f) = \frac{\vert H(f) \vert}{\vert H(0) \vert}
$$

where $f$ is spatial frequency (line pairs per mm, lp/mm) and $H(f)$ is the system transfer function.

- **MTF(0) = 1** (perfect response at zero frequency).
- The **cut-off frequency** $f_c$ is the spatial frequency at which MTF drops to a small threshold (often 0.1 or 0.05).
- Spatial resolution improves as $f_c$ increases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, you must know that **MTF describes how well the imaging system preserves contrast at each spatial frequency** — a value of 1 means perfect transfer, 0 means total loss of contrast.

### 2.1.4 Limiting Spatial Resolution
For a typical modern CT, **limiting in-plane resolution ≈ 0.4–0.5 mm**; high-resolution modes reach ≈ 0.24 mm. For SPECT, intrinsic resolution of a gamma camera is ≈ 3–5 mm FWHM, with overall system resolution of 7–10 mm.

---

## 2.2 Contrast Resolution

**Contrast** is the relative difference in signal between two regions. The **contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR)** determines whether a low-contrast lesion is detectable:

$$
\text{CNR} = \frac{\vert S_A - S_B \vert}{\sigma_B}
$$

where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are mean signals in regions A (lesion) and B (background), and $\sigma_B$ is the standard deviation of the background noise.

A lesion is **just detectable** when CNR exceeds a threshold (Rose criterion): **CNR ≥ 3–5**.

---

## 2.3 Noise

**Noise** in medical images arises from:

1. **Quantum (Poisson) noise** – fluctuation in the number of detected photons. Dominant in nuclear imaging and low-dose CT.
2. **Electronic noise** – from detector and amplifier electronics.
3. **Structured (anatomic) noise** – overlapping normal anatomic structures that obscure a lesion.

The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{S}{\sigma}
$$

For Poisson-distributed counts $N$:

$$
\text{SNR} = \sqrt{N}
$$

This is the **fundamental noise law** of nuclear imaging: **to double SNR you must quadruple the count (and therefore the dose or scan time)**.

---

## 2.4 Artifacts

**Artifacts** are structures in the image that do not correspond to actual anatomy. KTU 2024 requires familiarity with:

- **Beam hardening** (cupping, dark streaks between dense bone) — CT.
- **Motion artifact** (ghosting, blurring) — all modalities.
- **Ring artifacts** (mis-calibrated detector channels) — CT.
- **Partial volume effect** (averaging of signal within a voxel containing different tissues) — CT, MRI, SPECT, PET.
- **Metallic artifacts** (streaks) — CT, MRI.
- **Aliasing** (undersampling) — MRI, CT reconstruction.

---

## 2.5 CT Number (Hounsfield Unit) — Derivation Logic

The CT number is defined as:

$$
\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** at the effective energy of the X-ray beam.

**Why "×1000"?** It is a scaling convention to make the integer scale user-friendly. Water is fixed at 0 HU; air is fixed at −1000 HU (since the attenuation of air is essentially zero, so $\text{HU}_{\text{air}} = 1000 \times (0 - \mu_w)/\mu_w = -1000$).

---

## 2.6 Nuclear Imaging Physics — Core Concepts

### 2.6.1 Radiopharmaceutical
A **radiopharmaceutical** is a pharmaceutical molecule labeled with a radioactive atom. Example: **${}^{99m}\text{Tc}$-MDP** (methylene diphosphonate) is taken up by bone, **${}^{18}\text{F}$-FDG** (fluorodeoxyglucose) is taken up by metabolically active cells.

### 2.6.2 Collimator
A **collimator** is a lead plate with thousands of parallel (or converging/diverging) holes that absorb obliquely-incident gamma photons. **Without a collimator, no useful nuclear image can be formed** — the collimator is the analog of the X-ray tube's collimation but acts on the *emission* side, not the exposure side.

Types of collimators:
- **Parallel-hole** (most common, projects 1:1 image)
- **Converging / Pinhole** (magnification)
- **Diverging** (minification, fits large organs)
- **High-resolution vs. high-sensitivity** (trade-off: resolution ↓ when sensitivity ↑).

### 2.6.3 The Anger Gamma Camera (SPECT base)
Components:
1. **Collimator** (lead)
2. **Scintillator** – NaI(Tl) crystal, 25–40 cm diameter, 6–12 mm thick
3. **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) array** – typically 37, 53, 61, or 91 PMTs
4. **Position-computation electronics** – Anger position logic
5. **Pulse-height analyzer (PHA)** – accepts only the photopeak (energy window)

### 2.6.4 SPECT Acquisition
A single-head or dual-head gamma camera rotates around the patient in 3° steps over a 360° (or 180° for cardiac) arc, acquiring 120–128 projections of 10–30 seconds each. **Filtered back-projection (FBP)** or **iterative reconstruction (OSEM)** produces transverse slices.

### 2.6.5 PET Physics
- **Positron emission**: $\text{p} \to \text{n} + e^+ + \nu_e$
- **Positron range** in tissue: 0.5–2 mm (FWHM) — sets a physical lower limit on spatial resolution.
- **Annihilation**: $e^+ + e^- \to 2\gamma$ (each 511 keV, 180° apart).
- **Coincidence detection** within a narrow time window (≈ 6–12 ns) localizes the line of response (LOR).
- **Attenuation correction** in PET requires a **transmission scan** (with a Ge-68/Ga-68 rod source) or, in modern PET/CT, the **CT image** is used to correct attenuation.
- **Time-of-Flight (TOF) PET** measures the difference in arrival times of the two 511 keV photons, narrowing the LOR to a small segment → better SNR.

### 2.6.6 Rectilinear Scanner Architecture
- **Single NaI(Tl) crystal** (typically 3″ × 2″) coupled to a single PMT.
- **Focused collimator** (multi-hole, tapered holes focus at a specific depth).
- Mechanical **raster scanning** in X and Y directions.
- Image built up as a dot-density map on a photographic film or oscilloscope screen.
- Scan time: 30–60 minutes for a whole-body bone scan (compared with 15–25 min on a modern gamma camera).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | CT Number (HU) | $\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$ | dimensionless, integer scale |
| 2 | MTF | $\text{MTF}(f) = \vert H(f) \vert / \vert H(0) \vert$ | unitless, 0 → 1 |
| 3 | SNR (Poisson) | $\text{SNR} = \sqrt{N}$ | N = detected counts |
| 4 | CNR | $\text{CNR} = \vert S_A - S_B \vert / \sigma_B$ | Rose criterion: $\ge 3$–$5$ |
| 5 | Spatial resolution (FWHM) | $R \approx 0.94 \cdot \Delta x$ (pixel size) | mm |
| 6 | Positron energy at rest | $E_{e^+} = m_e c^2 = 511 \text{ keV}$ | per photon |
| 7 | Coincidence time window | $\Delta t \approx 6$–$12$ ns | PET |
| 8 | Tc-99m gamma energy | $E_\gamma = 140 \text{ keV}$ | SPECT |
| 9 | Tc-99m half-life | $T_{1/2} = 6.01 \text{ h}$ | SPECT |
| 10 | F-18 half-life (FDG) | $T_{1/2} = 109.8 \text{ min}$ | PET |
| 11 | CT # for water | $0$ | reference |
| 12 | CT # for air | $-1000$ | reference |
| 13 | CT # for dense bone | $\approx +1000$ | reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Tested:** The CT number formula and the reference values (0, −1000, +1000) appear in **every** recent KTU ESE. Memorize them.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CT HU** enables **quantitative imaging** (e.g., classifying adrenal incidentalomas as adenoma vs. non-adenoma based on HU thresholds; monitoring pulmonary nodules).
- **MTF / LSF / PSF** are the **figure of merit** for detector and reconstruction engineers when designing new CT or PET detectors.
- **SNR ∝ √N** is the engineering constraint behind every **low-dose CT** and **short-time PET** protocol.
- **Coincidence timing resolution** is the active area of R&D in modern TOF-PET systems (target: < 100 ps).
- **Rectilinear scanner** knowledge is essential for engineers maintaining legacy systems in smaller Indian hospitals and for the **KTU 2024 syllabus** historical-evolution question.
