# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MR-PET and explain why SiPM detectors are preferred over PMTs in MR-PET systems. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**MR-PET** is a hybrid imaging modality that integrates a Magnetic Resonance (MR) scanner and a Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scanner within the same gantry, enabling *simultaneous* acquisition of anatomical/functional MR images and metabolic PET images from the same anatomical region without patient repositioning.

**SiPMs vs PMTs in MR-PET:**

| Property | PMT (Vacuum Tube) | SiPM (Solid State) |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetic field immunity | Poor — electron avalanche deflected | **Excellent — no electron transport in vacuum** |
| Size | Bulky (~3–5 cm diameter) | **Compact (~1–3 mm microcells)** |
| Gain | ~10⁶–10⁷ | ~10⁶ |
| Bias voltage | ~ 1000 V | ~ 30–70 V |
| Timing resolution | ~ 300–500 ps | ~ 100 ps |
| Quantum efficiency at 420 nm | ~ 25 % | ~ 30–50 % |

**Conclusion:** SiPMs are preferred because they are **insensitive to strong static magnetic fields** (B₀ = 1.5 T or 3 T) and the **rapidly switching gradient fields** of the MR, allowing the PET detector ring to be placed *inside* the MR bore without performance degradation.

---

### Question 2: What is intraoperative imaging? List any two clinical applications. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Intraoperative imaging** refers to the acquisition of medical images *during* a surgical procedure, allowing the surgeon to verify instrument placement, resection margins, and physiological changes in real-time, without closing the patient.

**Two clinical applications:**
1. **Intraoperative MRI (iMRI)** in brain tumor resection — verifies completeness of tumor removal before dural closure.
2. **Intraoperative CT (iCT) / O-arm** in spinal instrumentation — confirms pedicle screw placement within the vertebra, reducing the risk of nerve root injury.
3. (Bonus) **C-arm fluoroscopy** in orthopedic fracture reduction — verifies alignment before final fixation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE question from a choice of two per module. Each 14-mark question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — MR-PET Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

#### (a) Describe the MR-PET system architecture. Explain in detail the technical challenges in integrating MR and PET systems, and the engineering solutions employed. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. System Architecture:**

The MR-PET system consists of:
- A **superconducting magnet** (typically 3 T) producing the B₀ field.
- **Gradient coils** (X, Y, Z) for spatial encoding of MR signals.
- An **RF body coil** tuned to ~128 MHz (at 3 T) for proton excitation.
- A **PET detector ring** using SiPMs coupled to LYSO or LSO scintillators, placed *inside* the gradient coil but outside the RF coil.
- **Mu-metal shielding** between PET electronics and the magnet.
- **Fiber-optic readout** for PET data to prevent RF interference.
- **MR-based attenuation correction** using Dixon or UTE sequences (since CT cannot be co-located).

**2. Technical Challenges & Solutions:**

| Challenge | Mechanism | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Magnetic field on PMTs | Electron avalanche in PMT dynodes is deflected by B₀ | **Replace PMTs with SiPMs** |
| RF interference from 128 MHz pulse | MR RF coil induces voltages in PET electronics | **RF shielding, fiber-optic isolation** |
| Gradient field dB/dt induces eddy currents | Switching gradients (40 mT/m/ms) cause currents in PET components | **Common-mode chokes, differential signaling** |
| B₀ inhomogeneity from PET components | Any ferromagnetic material distorts B₀ | **Non-magnetic materials, careful geometric placement** |
| PET pulse pile-up during MR sequence | Random coincidences falsely recorded | **MR sequence-synchronized PET gating** |
| Attenuation correction without CT | Cannot use 511 keV μ-map directly | **MR Dixon sequence for water/fat/air segmentation → synthetic μ-map** |

**Conclusion:** MR-PET is a tour-de-force of electromagnetic compatibility engineering. The combination of SiPMs, mu-metal shielding, and fiber-optics enables simultaneous operation of two complex imaging modalities in the same physical space.

---

#### (b) With neat block diagram, explain PET-CT system. Describe attenuation correction in PET-CT in detail. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. PET-CT Block Diagram** (refer Section 4.2 above).

**2. Attenuation Correction in PET-CT:**

At 511 keV, photons are attenuated significantly by tissue. Without correction, deep structures (e.g., mediastinal lymph nodes) appear falsely hypo-metabolic, leading to misinterpretation.

**Steps:**

**Step 1: Acquire CT transmission data.**
The CT scanner produces a 3D attenuation map at effective energy E_CT (~ 70–80 keV).

**Step 2: Convert CT numbers to μ at 511 keV (Bilinear scaling).**
Since CT provides attenuation at low energy and PET requires attenuation at 511 keV, a bilinear transformation is applied:

$$\mu_{511}(x) = a \cdot \mu_{CT}(x) + b$$

where a and b are constants determined by calibration against water and bone (derived in Section 3.4 above).

**Step 3: Forward-project the μ-map along each LOR.**
For each line of response (LOR), compute the line integral:

$$\int_{LOR} \mu_{511}(x,y) \, dl$$

**Step 4: Compute ACF for each LOR:**

$$ACF_{LOR} = e^{\int \mu_{511}(x,y) \, dl}$$

**Step 5: Apply ACF to emission data during reconstruction:**

$$I_{corrected}(LOR) = I_{measured}(LOR) \cdot ACF_{LOR}$$

**Step 6: Reconstruct using iterative methods** (e.g., OSEM — Ordered Subset Expectation Maximization):

$$\lambda^{n+1} = \frac{\lambda^n}{\sum_j a_{ij}} \sum_j a_{ij} \cdot \frac{y_i}{\sum_k a_{ik} \lambda^n_k}$$

where λ is the reconstructed activity, a_ij is the system matrix, and y_i are the corrected counts.

**Step 7: Co-register CT and PET** in a common coordinate system using the shared patient bed geometry.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Intraoperative & X-Ray Hybrid Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

#### (a) Explain in detail the concept of Image-Guided Therapy (IGT). Describe the components of an image-guided neurosurgical system with a block diagram. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of IGT:**

Image-Guided Therapy is the use of medical imaging (pre-operative CT/MR, intraoperative fluoroscopy, ultrasound, or iMRI) integrated with **spatial tracking systems** to provide the surgeon with real-time visualization of instrument position relative to patient anatomy. It reduces surgical trauma, improves resection accuracy, and shortens operative time.

**2. Components of an Image-Guided Neurosurgical System:**

(i) **Pre-operative imaging dataset** — high-resolution MR or CT with voxel size ~1 mm³, with skin fiducial markers or bone-implanted screws.

(ii) **Patient registration hardware:**
   - **Optical camera** (e.g., Polaris, BrainLab) — IR cameras tracking reflective spheres.
   - **Reference frame** clamped to skull clamp (e.g., Mayfield head frame).
   - **Tracked probe** or surgical instrument with reflective markers.

(iii) **Registration software:**
   - **Point-to-point registration**: Surgeon touches fiducials with probe; software computes a rigid-body transformation T (rotation + translation) mapping pre-op image coordinates to OR coordinates.
   - **FRE and TRE calculation** to assess accuracy.

(iv) **Navigation display:**
   - Real-time overlay of instrument trajectory on tri-planar (axial, sagittal, coronal) MR/CT views.
   - Distance-to-target indicator.

(v) **Intraoperative verification imaging:**
   - iMRI or iCT to verify resection margins and correct for **brain shift** (5–25 mm deformation after dural opening).
   - Updates the navigation dataset.

**3. Block Diagram** (refer Section 4.4 above).

**4. Typical accuracy:**
- **TRE < 1 mm** achievable with bone-implanted fiducials in skull-base neurosurgery.
- **TRE 2–5 mm** with skin fiducials (acceptable for many procedures).

---

#### (b) Describe in detail the X-ray hybrid modalities used for image guidance, with emphasis on C-arm fluoroscopy and Cone-Beam CT (CBCT). [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. X-Ray Hybrid Modalities for Image Guidance — Overview:**

These include:
- **C-arm fluoroscopy** — 2D real-time X-ray imaging.
- **Cone-Beam CT (CBCT)** — 3D volumetric reconstruction from C-arm rotation.
- **Angiography suites** — fixed-room C-arms with full DSA capability.
- **Intraoperative CT (iCT)** — diagnostic CT moved into OR.
- **Mobile CT (e.g., BodyTom, CereTom)** — portable slip-ring CT.

**2. C-arm Fluoroscopy:**

A C-arm consists of an **X-ray tube** mounted opposite a **flat-panel detector** on a C-shaped gantry. The C-arm pivots around the patient, providing projection views from arbitrary angles.

**Key technical specs:**
- Tube voltage: 50–120 kVp
- Tube current: 1–10 mA (pulsed fluoroscopy)
- Detector: a-Si or CMOS flat-panel, 20×20 to 40×40 cm, pixel pitch 150–200 µm
- Pulse rate: 4–30 fps (pulsed fluoroscopy reduces dose by 50–80 % vs continuous)
- Rotation range: up to 360° (motorized)

**3. Cone-Beam CT (CBCT):**

Acquisition: The C-arm rotates through ~200° in 5–20 seconds while acquiring 200–600 projection frames.

Reconstruction: **FDK algorithm** (Feldkamp-Davis-Kress) — derived in Section 3.6 above:

$$f(x,y,z) = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{R}{R-s} \cdot P_\alpha(s,t) \cdot h\left(\frac{Rt}{R-s} - y\right) d\alpha$$

**Clinical applications:**
- **Spinal surgery** — pedicle screw placement verification.
- **Trauma** — intra-articular fracture assessment.
- **Endovascular** — stent and coil deployment verification.
- **ENT/skull base** — cochlear implant placement.

**4. Advantages vs Limitations:**

| Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|
| Real-time 2D + 3D in OR | Increased scatter due to cone geometry |
| No patient transport needed | Limited soft-tissue contrast |
| Lower cost than full iCT | Higher skin dose for 3D spin |
| Compact, mobile | Limited FOV (~15×15×15 cm³ typical) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PET and SPECT energies** — PET uses 511 keV (annihilation); SPECT uses ~140 keV (Tc-99m). Examiners frequently test this.
> 2. **Writing "PMTs are used in MR-PET"** — This is FALSE and will lose full marks. SiPMs are mandatory in MR-PET.
> 3. **Confusing sequential vs simultaneous hybrid imaging** — PET-CT is sequential (table translates); MR-PET is simultaneous.
> 4. **Skipping the "why"** — Don't just write "SiPM is better than PMT." Write WHY (magnetic field immunity, compact, low voltage).
> 5. **Wrong formula usage** — Bilinear scaling for PET-CT ACF, NOT a single linear scale.
> 6. **Ignoring units** — Always state units (mGy, mm, ps, MeV, MHz).
> 7. **In IGT questions, missing brain shift correction** — Real navigation must address brain-shift after dural opening.
> 8. **In CBCT questions, naming the FDK algorithm without describing it** — Examiners expect brief mention of weighted back-projection with ramp filter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density bullets — Read just before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] **MR-PET** = simultaneous MR + PET; uses **SiPMs** (not PMTs) due to B₀ immunity.
- [ ] **Larmor frequency** at 3 T = **128 MHz** → drives RF shielding design.
- [ ] **PET-CT** = sequential; **SPECT-CT** = sequential; **MR-PET** = simultaneous.
- [ ] **PET photon energy = 511 keV** (annihilation); **SPECT Tc-99m = 140 keV**.
- [ ] **Attenuation correction** uses **bilinear scaling** of CT (low keV) → 511 keV μ-map.
- [ ] **SPECT-CT attenuation** rescales CT to 140 keV μ-map.
- [ ] **MR-based AC** (in MR-PET) uses **Dixon** or **UTE** sequences (no CT inside MR).
- [ ] **Intraoperative imaging** = real-time imaging during surgery (iMRI, iCT, C-arm).
- [ ] **IGT components**: pre-op image, fiducials, optical tracker, reference frame, registration, navigation display, intra-op verification.
- [ ] **FRE** = fiducial pair error; **TRE** = target point error (target metric).
- [ ] **C-arm CBCT** uses **FDK algorithm** for 3D reconstruction; ~200° rotation in 5–20 s.
- [ ] **DSA** = mask image subtracted from contrast image → vessels visible alone.
- [ ] **DQE** = MTF² / (NPS × q); gold-standard detector metric.
- [ ] **Positron range** for F-18 ~ **2.3 mm max** (~0.6 mm mean) — fundamental PET resolution limit.
- [ ] **TOF-PET** with 500 ps CTR → ~ **2.3× SNR gain** for 40 cm object.
- [ ] **Dose monitoring** in fluoroscopy: DAP (Dose-Area Product) + cumulative skin dose.
- [ ] **Skin erythema threshold** ~ 2 Gy; **epilation** ~ 3–6 Gy — critical for interventional procedures.
- [ ] **SiPM gain** G = C(V_bias − V_BD) / q_e — ~10⁶, comparable to PMT.
- [ ] **Clinical domains**: PET-CT = oncology staging; SPECT-CT = cardiac perfusion; MR-PET = neuro-oncology & pediatric; iCT/iMRI = neurosurgery; C-arm CBCT = orthopedic/vascular.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — PCEBT502 — KTU 2024 Scheme** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT502 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Technology of Ultrasound-Guided Therapy, CT Guided Interventions: Current Practice and Future Directions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Image-Guided Therapy — The Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Image-Guided Therapy (IGT)** refers to minimally invasive therapeutic procedures in which real-time or near-real-time medical imaging (Ultrasound, CT, MRI, Fluoroscopy, or PET) is used to precisely plan, monitor, navigate, and control the delivery of energy, instruments, or pharmaceuticals to a target tissue, while sparing the surrounding healthy structures.

The core philosophy of IGT is to combine **diagnostic visualization** with **therapeutic action** in a single integrated clinical workflow. Instead of opening the patient surgically, the clinician uses imaging as both the "roadmap" and the "surveillance camera" to direct a needle, electrode, transducer, or radiation beam to a diseased focus (typically a tumor, abscess, or vascular lesion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IGT Matters (KTU Board Hot Topic):**
> - Reduces patient morbidity, hospital stay, and recovery time.
> - Enables treatment of inoperable/deep-seated lesions.
> - Allows real-time feedback on treatment efficacy (e.g., perfusion, temperature, tissue stiffness).

---

## 1.2 Ultrasound-Guided Therapy — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Ultrasound-Guided Therapy (US-Guided Therapy)** is a class of interventional procedures where a real-time B-mode (or Doppler) ultrasound beam is used to localize a lesion, guide a needle/probe into it, and monitor the therapeutic energy deposition (thermal, mechanical, or cavitational) inside the tissue.

### Sub-Modalities of US-Guided Therapy

| Sub-Modality | Therapeutic Agent | Typical Clinical Use |
|--------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| US-Guided Biopsy | Core-cut / FNA needle | Histology of breast, thyroid, liver, lymph node lesions |
| US-Guided Drainage | Catheter / pigtail | Abscess, pleural effusion, ascites |
| HIFU (High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound) | Focused acoustic energy | Uterine fibroids, prostate, liver tumors, bone metastases |
| US-Guided RF / Microwave Ablation | Electrode heat | Liver, kidney, thyroid nodules |
| Extracorporeal Shock Wave Lithotripsy (ESWL) | Focused shock pulses | Renal / ureteric calculi |
| Sonothrombolysis | Microbubbles + tPA | Acute ischemic stroke (investigational) |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sonar Surgeon"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:**
> Think of ultrasound-guided therapy as **a submarine's sonar being used by a surgeon to perform surgery in the dark.** The ultrasound probe sends out sound "pings" (the ultrasound beam) into the body, and the echoes paint a real-time grayscale picture on the monitor. The clinician can then steer a needle, watch it travel through tissue, and confirm that its tip is exactly inside a tumor — all without cutting the patient open. For HIFU, the same "ping" is turned up to billions of times more powerful and is focused to a single pinpoint, where the converging energy cooks the tissue — like using a magnifying glass to focus sunlight, but with sound instead of light.

---

## 1.3 CT-Guided Interventions — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **CT-Guided Intervention (CTGI)** is a minimally invasive procedure in which a computed tomography scanner is used to acquire sequential cross-sectional images (axial, helical, or cone-beam CT) that guide the operator in real-time (CT fluoroscopy) or step-by-step (incremental helical CT) toward a target lesion for biopsy, drainage, ablation, or augmentation.

### Sub-Modalities of CT-Guided Intervention

| Sub-Modality | Therapeutic Agent | Typical Clinical Use |
|--------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| CT-Guided Percutaneous Biopsy | Coaxial / core needle | Lung nodule, bone, retroperitoneum, deep pelvic mass |
| CT-Guided Drainage | 8–14 Fr catheter | Deep abscess, empyema, pancreatic pseudocyst |
| CT-Guided RF / Microwave / Cryoablation | Thermal/cold probe | Lung, liver, kidney, bone, adrenal tumors |
| CT-Guided Vertebroplasty / Kyphoplasty | Bone cement (PMMA) | Osteoporotic / metastatic vertebral compression fractures |
| CT-Guided Nerve Block / Pain Injections | Local anesthetic + steroid | Facet joint, celiac plexus, trigeminal neuralgia |
| CT Fluoroscopy | Real-time CT (4–8 FPS) | Continuous needle tip visualization |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS for the Body"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:**
> CT-guided intervention is like using a **3D Google Earth map of the body to drive a needle to a target** the size of a peanut hidden inside the abdomen. Before each "drive" the operator takes a fresh CT slice (the map), calculates the exact angle and depth (the GPS coordinates), advances the needle a few millimetres, and re-scans to verify the new position. It is slower than ultrasound, but it works beautifully for deep targets and air-filled regions (lung) where ultrasound beam cannot penetrate.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Overview — US vs CT Guidance

| Parameter | Ultrasound Guidance | CT Guidance |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Real-time feedback | Yes (≥ 30 FPS) | CT fluoroscopy (4–8 FPS) or intermittent |
| Ionizing radiation | **None** | Yes (typical skin dose 0.5–5 cGy per pass) |
| Cost per procedure | **Low** (~$30–80) | High (~$300–800) |
| Operator dependency | High (probe-hand skill) | Moderate (axial slice reads) |
| Best for superficial targets | Thyroid, breast, vascular | — |
| Best for deep / air-bone targets | Limited | **Lung, bone, deep pelvis, retroperitoneum** |
| Needle-tip visualization | Direct (echogenic tip) | Indirect (metal artifact / grid) |
| Thermal ablation compatible | Yes (HIFU, RFA) | Yes (RFA, MWA, cryo) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Suites:** Modern interventional radiology combines both — using **US for initial localization and CT for precise needle-tip confirmation and ablation planning.** This is the current gold-standard for complex ablations (e.g., liver RFA, lung MWA).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Ultrasound-Guided Therapy

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Acoustic Parameters

The ultrasound beam is a longitudinal pressure wave governed by the wave equation in a viscoelastic medium:

$$\nabla^2 p - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

where $p$ is the acoustic pressure (Pa) and $c$ is the **speed of sound** in the medium.

> **Speed of sound (c) in biological tissues:** $c \approx \mathbf{1540\ m/s}$ (the KTU-required benchmark value, even though fat = 1450 m/s, muscle = 1580 m/s, bone ≈ 4080 m/s).

### 2.1.2 Acoustic Intensity and Mechanical / Thermal Indices

Two safety indices are mandated by the FDA / IEC 62359 for diagnostic and therapeutic ultrasound displays:

$$MI = \frac{P_{r,0.3}}{\sqrt{f_c}}$$

where $P_{r,0.3}$ is the **derated peak rarefactional pressure** in MPa, and $f_c$ is the centre frequency in MHz.

$$TI = \frac{W_{deg}}{W_{deg,deg}}$$

where $W_{deg}$ is the **derated acoustic power** and $W_{deg,deg}$ is the power required to raise tissue temperature by **1 °C** in a standardized model.

For **HIFU (High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound)**, the spatial-peak temporal-average intensity can reach:

$$I_{SPTA} \approx 1{,}000 \text{ to } 10{,}000\ \text{W/cm}^2$$

which is **5 to 6 orders of magnitude** higher than diagnostic US ($\sim 0.05$ W/cm²).

### 2.1.3 Bio-Heat Transfer Equation (Pennes 1948)

The temperature rise $\Delta T$ in tissue due to absorbed acoustic energy is described by:

$$\rho c_t \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + Q_a + Q_p - Q_b$$

where:
- $\rho$ = tissue density ($\approx 1050\ \text{kg/m}^3$)
- $c_t$ = specific heat of tissue ($\approx 3600\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity ($\approx 0.5\ \text{W/(m·K)}$)
- $Q_a$ = volumetric absorbed acoustic power density (W/m³)
- $Q_p$ = metabolic heat (usually negligible in ablation)
- $Q_b$ = **perfusion-mediated heat sink** (W/m³)

The absorbed power density is given by:

$$Q_a = 2 \alpha I$$

where $\alpha$ is the **acoustic absorption coefficient** (Np/m/cm) and $I$ is the local acoustic intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In HIFU planning, $Q_b$ is the parameter that determines whether a tumour at the rim of a large vessel can be successfully ablated — the flowing blood "steals" the heat and protects the endothelium.

### 2.1.4 Acoustic Cavitation and Inertial Collapse

When $MI \geq 0.3$, microbubbles undergo **stable (non-inertial) cavitation** — rhythmic oscillation — used in **sonoporation** (transient opening of cell membranes for drug/gene delivery). When $MI \geq 0.7$, **inertial cavitation** occurs — violent collapse releasing shock waves and free radicals, used in **histotripsy** (mechanical tissue fractionation without heat).

---

## 2.2 Physics of CT-Guided Interventions

### 2.2.1 CT Number and Target Visibility

The **CT attenuation** of any tissue is expressed in Hounsfield Units (HU):

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$$

| Tissue | Typical HU | CT-Guided Implication |
|--------|-----------|-----------------------|
| Air | **−1000** | Lung biopsy: target = solid nodule within air-filled lung |
| Fat | **−100 to −50** | Useful anatomical landmark for needle path planning |
| Water / CSF | 0 | Reference for dose calibration |
| Soft tissue / muscle | **+30 to +80** | Most abdominal targets |
| Acute blood | **+60 to +80** | Hyperdense haematoma |
| Cortical bone | **+1000** | Vertebroplasty / bone biopsy |
| Metallic needle tip | **>+3000** | **Beam-hardening streak artifact** is used to localize the needle tip on CT |

### 2.2.2 CT Fluoroscopy Dose

In **real-time CT fluoroscopy** (4–8 frames/s), the skin dose rate is:

$$\dot{D}_{skin} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 5\ \text{cGy/s}$$

For a typical 20–60 s procedure, cumulative skin dose is:

$$D_{skin} = 10 \text{ to } 300\ \text{cGy}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that CT fluoroscopy requires the operator to wear lead apron, thyroid shield, and lead glasses, AND that dose-reduction techniques (low mA, 4 FPS, foot-pedal control) are mandatory. The **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) is always a board-expected phrase.

### 2.2.3 Geometric Accuracy of CT Guidance

The in-plane geometric accuracy of a CT scanner is governed by:

$$\Delta x_{min} = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}$$

For a 35 cm FOV and 512 × 512 matrix:

$$\Delta x_{min} = \frac{350\ \text{mm}}{512} \approx 0.68\ \text{mm}$$

This is the **fundamental spatial resolution limit** of any CT-guided planning.

The needle-tip to target error in practice is dominated by **angular misalignment** of the needle:

$$\text{Lateral error at depth } d = d \cdot \tan(\Delta \theta)$$

For $d = 10\ \text{cm}$ and $\Delta \theta = 1°$:

$$E_{lateral} = 100\ \text{mm} \times 0.01745 \approx 1.75\ \text{mm}$$

---

## 2.3 Thermal Ablation Physics (Common to US & CT)

Both RFA (Radiofrequency Ablation) and MWA (Microwave Ablation) — frequently delivered under CT or US guidance — produce a thermal lesion whose size is governed by the **bio-heat equation** solved over the treatment time $t$. The standard Arrhenius thermal-damage model is:

$$\Omega(t) = \int_0^t A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T(\tau))}\ d\tau$$

A thermal dose of $\Omega \geq 1$ corresponds to **near-complete cell death** (equivalent to 240 minutes at 43 °C, or 1 second at ~70 °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** Remember the 60-°C / 1-minute rule — heating any tissue to 60 °C for 1 minute produces **instant coagulative necrosis** (used clinically as the target endpoint in RFA/MWA).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $c = 1540\ \text{m/s}$ | Speed of sound in soft tissue | Fixed KTU benchmark |
| 2 | $MI = P_{r,0.3} / \sqrt{f_c}$ | Mechanical Index (cavitation risk) | $MI < 1.9$ (FDA limit) |
| 3 | $TI = W / W_{deg}$ | Thermal Index (heating risk) | $TI < 6$ |
| 4 | $I_{SPTA}$ | Spatial-peak time-average intensity | Diagnostic: 0.05 W/cm²; HIFU: 10³–10⁴ W/cm² |
| 5 | $Q_a = 2\alpha I$ | Volumetric absorbed acoustic power | W/m³ |
| 6 | $HU = 1000(\mu_t - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ | CT Hounsfield Unit | Air −1000; Bone +1000 |
| 7 | $D_{skin}$ (CT fluoro) | Skin dose per procedure | 10 – 300 cGy |
| 8 | $\Delta x = FOV / N_{pix}$ | In-plane CT resolution | $\approx 0.68$ mm |
| 9 | $E_{lat} = d \cdot \tan(\Delta \theta)$ | Needle lateral error at depth $d$ | mm |
| 10 | $T_{kill} \geq 60\ °C$ for 1 min | Coagulative necrosis threshold | Universal ablation endpoint |
| 11 | $\Omega \geq 1$ | Arrhenius thermal dose for cell death | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_{acoustic} = \eta \cdot P_{electrical}$ | HIFU transducer efficiency | $\eta \approx 0.3 - 0.7$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations govern the design of every commercial HIFU system (Insightec ExAblate, Philips Sonalleve, EDAP TMS), every CT-fluoroscopy dose-optimization algorithm, and every FDA submission for an ablation device.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Speed of Sound in Tissue from Acoustic Impedance

**Statement:** The speed of a longitudinal acoustic wave in a homogeneous, isotropic medium is given by:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}$$

**Derivation:**

Consider a thin slab of tissue of thickness $dx$ and cross-sectional area $A$. A pressure pulse $p$ compresses it by an amount $d\xi$. The bulk modulus is defined as:

$$K = -V \frac{dp}{dV} = - \frac{A \cdot dx}{A \cdot d\xi} \cdot \frac{dp}{d\xi / dx} \cdot dx$$

Step 1 — Write Newton's second law for the displaced mass element:

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot dx \cdot \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = -A \cdot \left[ p(x+dx) - p(x) \right]$$

Step 2 — Divide both sides by $A \cdot dx$ and take the limit $dx \to 0$:

$$\rho \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$

Step 3 — Use the constitutive relation from the bulk modulus:

$$dp = -K \frac{dV}{V} = -K \frac{A \cdot d\xi}{A \cdot dx} = -K \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} dx$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -K \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial x^2}$$

Step 4 — Substitute back into the equation of motion:

$$\rho \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = K \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial x^2}$$

Step 5 — Compare with the standard 1-D wave equation $\partial^2 \xi/\partial t^2 = c^2 \cdot \partial^2 \xi/\partial x^2$:

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho}}}$$

**Numerical evaluation for soft tissue:**

Using $K \approx 2.2 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa}$ and $\rho \approx 1050\ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{2.2 \times 10^9}{1050}} = \sqrt{2.095 \times 10^6} \approx 1447\ \text{m/s}$$

The discrepancy with the 1540 m/s benchmark arises from the inclusion of shear-viscoelastic terms in real tissue; the KTU-required value **c = 1540 m/s** is the speed used for all scanner calibrations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Focal Zone Pressure in HIFU

**Statement:** The pressure amplitude at the geometric focus of a spherically-curved HIFU transducer of radius $R$ and aperture $2a$ is:

$$p_{focus} = \frac{p_0 \cdot \pi a^2}{\lambda \cdot F}$$

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — The on-axis pressure of a circular piston source (radius $a$) at axial distance $z$ is:

$$p(z) = 2 p_0 \sin\left[ \frac{\pi}{\lambda}\left(\sqrt{a^2 + z^2} - z\right) \right]$$

Step 2 — At the geometric focus $z = F = R/2$, the argument of the sine becomes small, and $\sin(x) \approx x$:

$$p(F) = 2 p_0 \cdot \frac{\pi}{\lambda}\left[\sqrt{a^2 + F^2} - F\right]$$

Step 3 — For $F \gg a$ (typical for HIFU, e.g., $F = 10$ cm, $2a = 3$ cm), expand the square root:

$$\sqrt{a^2 + F^2} = F\sqrt{1 + (a/F)^2} \approx F\left[1 + \frac{a^2}{2F^2}\right]$$

Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:

$$\sqrt{a^2 + F^2} - F \approx \frac{a^2}{2F}$$

$$p(F) \approx 2 p_0 \cdot \frac{\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{a^2}{2F} = \frac{p_0 \pi a^2}{\lambda F}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{p_{focus} = \frac{p_0 \pi a^2}{\lambda F}}$$

**Numerical example for a clinical HIFU system:**

Let $p_0 = 0.5$ MPa (source pressure), $a = 1.5$ cm, $F = 10$ cm, $f_c = 1.5$ MHz, so $\lambda = 1540 / 1.5 \times 10^6 \approx 1.03$ mm.

$$p_{focus} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \times \pi \times (0.015)^2}{0.00103 \times 0.10}$$

$$= \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-4}}{1.03 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{353.4}{1.03 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.43 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} = 3.4\ \text{MPa}$$

This **3.4 MPa focal pressure** corresponds to an $I_{SPTA}$ of:

$$I = \frac{p^2}{2 \rho c} = \frac{(3.4 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 1050 \times 1540} \approx 3{,}650\ \text{W/cm}^2$$

which is firmly in the **ablative range** for HIFU.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Indications, Energies, Imaging

| Therapy | Guidance | Energy Range / Dose | Endpoint Imaging | Common Indication |
|---------|----------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| HIFU (US) | MRI or US | $I_{SPTA} = 1{,}000\text{–}10{,}000\ \text{W/cm}^2$ | MR thermometry / CEUS | Uterine fibroid, prostate Ca |
| US-guided RFA | US | 200 W, 12 min, $T_{tip} \geq 60$ °C | CEUS / contrast CT | Liver HCC, thyroid nodule |
| US-guided MWA | US | 60–150 W, 5–15 min | CEUS | Liver / lung MWA |
| ESWL | Fluoroscopy / US | 18–24 kV, 2000 shocks | Fluoroscopy | Renal stone <2 cm |
| CT-guided lung RFA | CT | 200 W, 10–12 min | CT + PET 3 mo follow-up | Lung metastasis / NSCLC |
| CT-guided vertebroplasty | CT + fluoro | 2–6 mL PMMA | CT / fluoro | Vertebral compression fracture |
| CT-guided cryoablation | CT | −40 °C, double freeze-thaw | CT (ice-ball) | Renal / bone / lung |
| Sonothrombolysis (research) | TCD US | 2 MHz, MI 0.8–1.2 | TCD | Acute stroke |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — CT-Guided Lung Biopsy Planning

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Style):** A 14-mm spiculated lung nodule is identified on diagnostic CT in the right upper lobe, located 4.5 cm from the pleural surface. The radiologist plans a CT-guided coaxial core biopsy using an 18G needle. If the CT-fluoroscopy skin dose rate is 1.2 cGy/s, and the procedure requires 45 seconds of cumulative beam-on time, calculate: (a) the cumulative skin dose, (b) the in-plane CT resolution, and (c) the lateral error for a 2° angular misalignment.

**Solution:**

**(a) Cumulative skin dose**

$$D_{skin} = \dot{D}_{skin} \times t = 1.2\ \text{cGy/s} \times 45\ \text{s} = 54\ \text{cGy} = 0.54\ \text{Gy}$$

This is **below the deterministic skin-injury threshold of 2 Gy**, so no erythema is expected.

**(b) In-plane CT resolution** (FOV = 35 cm, matrix = 512):

$$\Delta x = \frac{350\ \text{mm}}{512} \approx 0.68\ \text{mm}$$

**(c) Lateral error at 4.5 cm depth, $\Delta \theta = 2°$**

$$E_{lat} = d \cdot \tan(\Delta \theta) = 45\ \text{mm} \times \tan(2°) = 45 \times 0.0349 \approx 1.57\ \text{mm}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1.57 mm lateral error is well within the **3 mm safety margin** of a 14 mm nodule — the procedure is geometrically feasible.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — US-Guided Therapy Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient on US Couch] --> B[Apply Acoustic Coupling Gel]
    B --> C[2D B-Mode Localization of Lesion]
    C --> D{Doppler Check for Adjacent Vessels}
    D --> E[Plan Needle / Probe Trajectory]
    E --> F[Local Anesthesia]
    F --> G[Real-Time Needle Insertion Under US]
    G --> H{Verify Tip Inside Lesion}
    H --> I[Deploy Therapeutic Energy: RFA / MWA / HIFU / Drug]
    I --> J[Monitor with CEUS or Elastography]
    J --> K{Treatment Endpoint Reached}
    K -- No --> I
    K -- Yes --> L[Withdraw Needle, Apply Compression]
    L --> M[Post-Procedure CEUS / Doppler Scan]
    M --> N[Transfer to Recovery, Document]
```

```mermaid
    S --> T[Define Target Lesion + Critical Structures]

    U --> V[Place Sterile Drape + CT Grid on Skin]

    W --> X[Re-Scan at Each Step to Verify Position]
    X --> Y[Acquire Limited CT Fluoro or Helical Confirmation]
    Y --> Z[Deploy Therapy: Biopsy / Drain / Ablate / Cement]
    Z --> AA[Post-Procedure CT to Rule Out Pneumothorax or Haemorrhage]
    AA --> AB[Transfer to Recovery, Document DAP Dose]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define image-guided therapy. List any two advantages of CT-guided biopsy over US-guided biopsy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image-guided therapy** is a minimally invasive procedure in which medical imaging modalities (US, CT, MRI, fluoroscopy) are used in real time to plan, guide, and monitor the delivery of a therapeutic intervention to a target tissue.

**Two advantages of CT-guided biopsy over US-guided biopsy (2 marks):**

1. **Superior visualization of deep-seated and air-containing targets** — CT is unaffected by air (lung) or bone, while US beam is blocked by both. Hence, CT is the gold standard for lung, bone, and deep retroperitoneal biopsy.
2. **Precise geometric planning** — CT provides sub-millimetre in-plane resolution (~0.68 mm) and multiplanar reformatting, enabling exact calculation of entry angle, depth, and avoidance of critical structures (vessels, nerves, pleura).
3. *Bonus point:* CT documents the needle path for medico-legal and follow-up comparison; US does not.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is HIFU? State the principle of operation in two sentences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**HIFU (High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound)** is a non-invasive therapeutic technique in which a high-power acoustic beam (I_{SPTA} = 1000–10{,}000\ \text{W/cm}^2) from an external phased-array transducer is geometrically and electronically focused to a millimetre-scale focal zone inside the body, where the converging energy is absorbed and converted into heat (60–90 °C in seconds), causing **coagulative necrosis** of the target tissue while sparing the surrounding normal tissue.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i) [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model]

**a)** Describe the **physics and clinical workflow of US-guided radiofrequency ablation (RFA)** of a hepatic tumour. Add a neat labelled diagram of the setup. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **role of contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) in monitoring RFA endpoint** and list two complications of hepatic RFA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
RFA uses a 200–250 kHz alternating current delivered through a needle electrode (17–19 G, often with internally cooled tip). The ionic agitation in tissue around the active tip produces **frictional (Joule) heating**. Tissue temperature is raised to **≥ 60 °C for ≥ 1 minute**, causing coagulative necrosis.

**2. US-Guided Workflow (4 marks):**
- **Step 1 — Pre-procedure planning:** B-mode US identifies lesion, its depth, and adjacent vessels (Doppler). CEUS confirms viable tumour and rules out necrosis.
- **Step 2 — Anaesthesia and positioning:** IV conscious sedation + local anaesthesia; patient supine.
- **Step 3 — Real-time US guidance:** The RFA electrode is advanced through a coaxial introducer under continuous US visualization, with the echogenic tip clearly seen.
- **Step 4 — Electrode deployment:** For tumours >2.5 cm, multi-tined or umbrella electrodes are deployed (1–3 cm array diameter).
- **Step 5 — Ablation cycle:** Generator delivers 100–200 W for 8–12 minutes. Internally cooled electrodes use chilled saline perfusion to prevent charring.
- **Step 6 — Endpoint verification:** See Part (b).

**3. Diagram (1 mark):** See block diagram in Section 4.4 — "US-Guided Therapy Workflow."

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Role of CEUS in monitoring endpoint (5 marks):**
- **Mechanism:** Microbubble contrast agents (SonoVue/Lumason) are pure intravascular tracers, 1–10 µm in size. They produce strong non-linear harmonics on low-MI US.
- **Pre-ablation CEUS:** Identifies viable, hyper-enhanced tumour tissue versus non-enhancing necrotic core — defines the **true treatment zone**.
- **Immediate post-ablation CEUS (5–15 min):** A successful ablation shows a **non-enhancing avascular zone with a 5–10 mm circumferential margin** beyond the original tumour. Any residual nodular enhancement within the ablation cavity indicates residual viable tumour requiring re-treatment.
- **Follow-up CEUS (1 month, 3 months, then quarterly):** Tracks recurrence. A new peripheral nodular enhancement or centrifugal fill-in is the hallmark of recurrent HCC.
- **Advantages over contrast CT/MRI in this context:** No nephrotoxicity, no ionizing radiation, real-time, repeatable at the bedside, and more sensitive to microvascular flow than CT in the immediate post-RFA period.

**2. Two complications of hepatic RFA (2 marks):**
- **Haemorrhage** (subcapsular haematoma, haemoperitoneum) — risk 1–2%.
- **Thermal injury to adjacent structures** — diaphragm, colon, stomach, bile ducts, or inadvertent skin burn at the grounding pad site.
- *Others (for completeness):* tumour seeding along the tract, hepatic abscess, post-ablation syndrome (fever, pain, leucocytosis), biloma.

---

### Question B — Option (ii) [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model]

**a)** Discuss the **clinical workflow of CT-guided percutaneous lung biopsy**. Add a flowchart. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **future directions of CT-guided interventions** including AI-based planning, robotic needle drivers, and augmented-reality guidance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Indication and pre-procedure work-up (1.5 marks):**
- Indication: solitary pulmonary nodule (SPN) 8–30 mm, suspected metastasis, suspected lymphoma, persistent consolidation.
- Work-up: review prior CT/PET-CT, coagulation profile (INR < 1.5, platelets > 50{,}000), pulmonary function (FEV1 > 0.8 L predicted post-resection), informed consent.

**2. Patient positioning (0.5 mark):**
- Supine, prone, or lateral decubitus chosen such that the **shortest straight-line path to the lesion** avoids fissures, bullae, vessels, and at least 1 cm of aerated lung (to minimize pneumothorax).

**3. CT planning and needle insertion (3 marks):**
- Step 1: Topogram + limited axial CT (3 mm slices, 5 mm gap) through the nodule.
- Step 2: Identify entry point on skin using a **CT-visible radiopaque grid** placed on the patient.
- Step 3: Calculate **cranio-caudal angle, medio-lateral angle, and depth-to-target** from the axial + sagittal/coronal reformats.
- Step 4: Local anaesthetic (2% lignocaine) down to pleura.
- Step 5: Insert **coaxial 19G introducer** in small increments; re-scan every 1–2 cm to confirm trajectory.
- Step 6: Once tip is within 5–10 mm of lesion, deploy **22G FNA or 20G core-cut needle** through the introducer.
- Step 7: Acquire final CT to document needle tip position within lesion.

**4. Post-procedure (1 mark):**
- Immediate limited CT through the chest to exclude **pneumothorax (incidence 15–25%)** and **pulmonary haemorrhage (5–10%)**.
- Patient observed for 2–4 hours with erect chest X-ray at 1 and 4 hours; chest drain set ready.
- Sample sent for cytology, histopathology, microbiology, and molecular testing (EGFR, ALK, PD-L1 for lung cancer).

**5. Flowchart (1 mark):** See block diagram in Section 4.2 — "CT-Guided Intervention Workflow."

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**1. AI-based automatic lesion segmentation and trajectory planning (2 marks):**
- Deep-learning models (U-Net, nnU-Net) automatically segment the **gross tumour volume (GTV)** and organs at risk (OAR — aorta, heart, oesophagus, bronchial tree) on pre-procedure CT within seconds.
- AI then proposes **2–5 candidate needle trajectories** that minimize the lung parenchymal crossing distance, maximize the angle to the nearest vessel, and avoid fissure/bulla — replacing 5–10 minutes of manual planning with a 30-second automated report.
- It also predicts the **probability of pneumothorax** per trajectory, allowing risk-stratified selection.

**2. Robotic needle drivers (2 marks):**
- 6-DOF robotic arms (e.g., MAXIO, EPIC, RobioEx, XACT Robotics) hold and advance the needle with **0.1 mm translational and 0.1° rotational precision**, immune to human tremor and respiratory drift.
- The robot executes the AI-planned trajectory under closed-loop CT or cone-beam CT feedback. Clinical studies report **first-pass accuracy of 90–95%** vs 60–70% for freehand, with **pneumothorax rates reduced to <10%**.
- Real-time force/torque sensing enables the robot to detect pleural puncture and reduce overshoot.

**3. Augmented-reality (AR) guidance (1.5 mark):**
- AR headsets (Microsoft HoloLens, Magic Leap) overlay the **holographic 3D model of the lesion, vessels, and planned needle path** directly onto the patient's skin and onto the operator's view through the headset.
- The hologram is **co-registered** to the patient's surface using structured-light or visible-light cameras, and is continuously updated with intra-procedural cone-beam CT or electromagnetic tracking of the needle tip.
- This eliminates the need to repeatedly look away from the patient to the CT monitor.

**4. Other emerging directions (1.5 marks):**
- **4D cone-beam CT** for respiratory-gated real-time targeting of moving lung and liver lesions.
- **Photons-counting CT** for sub-millisievert dose per needle pass with spectral material decomposition to differentiate tumour from atelectasis.
- **Multimodal image fusion (PET-CT + MR)** for metabolic- and perfusion-guided biopsy of the most aggressive tumour sub-region.
- **Closed-loop ablation** with AI-driven power titration based on real-time intratumoral temperature feedback from MR thermometry or microwave-radiometry.
- **Cloud-based outcome registries** to predict individual patient prognosis from multi-institutional data using federated learning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing "image-guided" with "image-diagnostic"** — students write a CT physics answer when asked about CT-guided therapy. Always start with: *"In this procedure, imaging is used to navigate the instrument, not just to diagnose."*
> 2. **Forgetting the dose value** — CT-guided intervention is an ionizing procedure; examiners expect a numerical mention of **typical skin dose (10–300 cGy)** and the **ALARA principle**.
> 3. **Skipping complications** — every image-guided procedure question in KTU 2024 scheme requires at least **2 named complications** (pneumothorax, haemorrhage, infection, thermal injury).
> 4. **No diagram / flowchart** — KTU valuation key explicitly allocates **1 mark** for a labelled block diagram or flowchart. Even a hand-drawn ASCII flow in the answer booklet scores.
> 5. **Vague future-directions** — listing "AI" without specifying the *type* of AI (segmentation, trajectory planning, endpoint prediction) loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong speed of sound** — never use 340 m/s (that is the speed of sound in air, NOT tissue). Always use **1540 m/s** for soft tissue in any US-based derivation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Image-Guided Therapy (IGT)** = imaging used in real time to plan, navigate, and monitor a minimally invasive treatment.
- **US-Guided Therapy** uses real-time B-mode (≥30 FPS); advantages: no radiation, low cost, real-time; limitations: operator-dependent, blocked by air and bone.
- **HIFU** is a non-invasive ablative technique; I_{SPTA} = 1000–10{,}000\ \text{W/cm}^2; focal zone 1×3×3 mm; 60–90 °C in seconds; coagulative necrosis endpoint.
- **Speed of sound in soft tissue (KTU benchmark):** $\mathbf{c = 1540\ m/s}$.
- **Mechanical Index (MI)** = $P_{r,0.3} / \sqrt{f_c}$; FDA limit **< 1.9**; MI ≥ 0.7 → inertial cavitation (histotripsy).
- **Thermal Index (TI)** = derated power / power for 1 °C rise; FDA limit **< 6**.
- **CT-guided intervention** uses sequential axial CT or CT-fluoroscopy (4–8 FPS, 0.5–5 cGy/s); best for lung, bone, deep retroperitoneum.
- **Hounsfield Unit** $HU = 1000(\mu_t - \mu_w)/\mu_w$; air = **−1000**, fat = **−100 to −50**, soft tissue = **+30 to +80**, bone = **+1000**.
- **In-plane CT resolution:** $\Delta x = FOV / N_{pix} \approx 0.68$ mm (35 cm FOV, 512 matrix).
- **Needle lateral error:** $E_{lat} = d \cdot \tan(\Delta \theta)$.
- **Thermal ablation endpoint:** **60 °C × 1 min** → coagulative necrosis (clinical rule of thumb).
- **Arrhenius thermal dose:** $\Omega \geq 1$ = cell death.
- **Common US-guided therapies:** biopsy, drainage, RFA, MWA, HIFU, ESWL, sonothrombolysis.
- **Common CT-guided interventions:** lung/retroperitoneal biopsy, drainage, RFA, MWA, cryoablation, vertebroplasty, nerve blocks, CT fluoroscopy.
- **Complications to always mention:** pneumothorax (lung biopsy), haemorrhage, infection, thermal injury to adjacent organs, tumour seeding.
- **Dose concern in CT fluoroscopy:** cumulative skin dose 10–300 cGy; ALARA mandatory.
- **Future directions:** AI auto-segmentation & trajectory planning, 6-DOF robotic needle drivers (first-pass accuracy 90–95%), AR holographic overlay (HoloLens), 4D cone-beam CT, photon-counting CT, federated outcome learning.
- **Hybrid IR suite** = US + CT in the same room — current gold standard for complex ablations.
- **CT-visible radiopaque grid** is the standard skin-marker for CT-guided procedures.
- **Coaxial introducer technique** reduces the number of pleural punctures and allows multiple core samples through a single puncture.
- **Post-procedure CT** is mandatory in lung biopsy to rule out pneumothorax.
- **ESWL** uses focused shock waves (18–24 kV, 2000 shocks) guided by fluoroscopy or US for renal stones.
